f: 2024-03-29_10-44-20 - FW_ _EXTERNAL_ Comments on Alameda Lane Reduction proposal.pdf
s: FW: Comments on Alameda Lane Reduction proposal
to: "Lanphier; Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering" <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>;
b:
Sorry my email isn’t as fun to share as Greg’s was

Hadar Albo, PE | Engineer
City & County of Denver
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Implementation Division
Pronouns<https://www.mypronouns.org/what-and-why> | She/Her/Hers
hadar.albo@denvergov.org<mailto:hadar.albo@denvergov.org>

From: Jill Anschutz <jill.hamilton@mac.com>
Sent: Thursday, March 28, 2024 9:53 PM
To: Albo, Hadar - DOTI CE0397 Engineer <Hadar.Albo@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Comments on Alameda Lane Reduction proposal
Hello,

I wanted to voice serious concerns my husband and I have about DOTI's proposed lane changes to the one-mile stretch of Alameda between S Logan and S Franklin. We have lived within a few blocks of Alameda in this area for over 10 years in several different homes, so we are very familiar with this area both as drivers and pedestrians. We have flexible work-from-home schedules so we use Alameda in this stretch in both directions at all times of the day and on all days of the week, including during the busiest hours in the morning commute and afternoon commute on weekdays.

Clearly, Alameda is a critical East-West corridor for vehicles. As traffic has increased significantly on Speer Blvd to the north of this stretch of Alameda, and with Wash Park and then I-25 to the south, there is not another efficient thoroughfare for vehicles needing to travel east or west in this neighborhood. We absolutely must preserve the flow of traffic in this area for the convenience of residents and for access to local businesses and one of the city's best parks for others.

While we certainly would not want to see changes that make things less safe for pedestrians - and we are supportive of the flashing cross walk addition at S Franklin - we feel strongly that choking down on traffic lanes for vehicles by designating a turn only center lane will have a net negative impact on this part of the city.

We have watched closely as DOTI has reduced traffic lanes in other areas of the city in the name of safety; the areas where we experience this the most frequently are along S Broadway and also on east-bound 7th Avenue in the historic district area (near Little Cheeseman). As regular pedestrians and drivers in these areas, we think reducing lanes by designating a lane for turning only creates confusion for drivers, which makes things much less safe for pedestrians.

Anyone driving regularly on Alameda has surely experienced traffic backing up while someone waits to turn across opposing traffic, however even with highly frequent use of this stretch of Alameda, it is infrequent that this backup becomes so significant that it's notable. In our experience, 4-5 pm is the toughest time of day to turn and people are traveling slower along Alameda at that time anyway because of the volume of cars. Therefore we believe designating a turn lane by reducing the other lanes will choke up on the regular flow of traffic, creating frequent frustration and more regular backups for drivers at all times of day. Yes, it will be easier to turn, but it should be weighted out the number of drivers turning vs. how many need to drive smoothly, safely and efficiently along Alameda without stopping or turning in this stretch? That is not a trade off we would ever want to make.

When I wrote to Councilman Kashmann, I also noted the differences that can already be observed between one-lane and two-lane sections of Alameda in this part of the city. The one-lane areas experience HUGE and frustrating backups throughout the day, with it becoming almost unnavigable for drivers during morning and afternoon rush hours. On the other hand, the two-lane portions of Alameda typically have good flow for vehicles throughout the day. Perhaps the city would consider installing the flashing crosswalk at S Franklin and leaving it at that for some time to see how that impacts pedestrian safety before making drastic changes to the lanes in this same section of Alameda?

We would also be interested in seeing data from DOTI about the impacts the city is seeing on safety in the areas where vehicle lanes have been reduced and new pedestrian access points have been added. Is the presentation from the March 20 community meeting available? Our own experiences as drivers and pedestrians in areas where similar changes have been made by DOTI have not convinced us that these are "improvements." We think they are confusing and frustrating for drivers and pedestrians and would be very disappointed to see these "improvements" made along Alameda.

Sincerely,

Jill Anschutz

Subject: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction PI Specifications

Hi Amy – I do not believe we have ever communicated with the property owners about this change to RI-RO access. Throughout the corridor, the only locations we are “physically preventing” LT’s are right near intersections where we have flex posts (to prevent LT’s/crossing near intersections). The two sections boxed in red below would be the only locations on the corridor that have double-double yellow centerline without flex posts (illegal to cross). This is why I was thinking a TWTL might be appropriate. But let me know what you think!

Thanks,

Brett Boncore, PE| Engineering Supervisor
City & County of Denver
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Design
Phone: 720.865.3214
brett.boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:brett.boncore@denvergov.org>

Hi Brett—I think this is a possible solution.  But I’m not sure I have the full context of the project and project discussions with property owners to weigh-in on the decision.  Did we tell them they were only getting Right in-Right out access?  Is this solution equitable to all the other property owners along the corridor?  Will other owners want this treatment?  Or is this a unique situation along Alameda?

Hi Amy – I do not believe we have ever communicated with the property owners about this change to RI-RO access. Throughout the corridor, the only locations we are “physically preventing” LT’s are right near intersections where we have flex posts (to prevent LT’s/crossing near intersections). The two sections boxed in red below would be the only locations on the corridor that have double-double yellow centerline without flex posts (illegal to cross). This is why I was thinking a TWTL might be appropriate. But let me know what you think!

Thanks,

Throughout the corridor, the only locations we are “physically preventing” LT’s are right near intersections where we have flex posts (to prevent LT’s/crossing near intersections). The two sections boxed in red below would be the only locations on the corridor that have double-double yellow centerline without flex posts (illegal to cross). This is why I was thinking a TWTL might be appropriate. But let me know what you think!

From: Griggs, Gail - MO MO Constituent Services Specialist <Gail.Griggs@denvergov.org>
Sent: Monday, April 7, 2025 3:45 PM
To: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>
Subject: Upcoming Roadway Changes to Alameda: [EXTERNAL] No contact received

Hello Molly –

Please read below regarding upcoming Roadway Changes to Alameda.

Below is the email that was sent from 311, copied from Salesforce.
Dear Mayor Johnston and Staff,

I am writing this email to inform you that *many members of the community
are concerned about the proposed change of Alameda from Logan to Franklin
to one lane in each direction with a middle turn lane.*

I attended a community meeting in 2024 regarding this proposed change.
There were many members of the community there. * A lot of people did not
think this proposed change was a good idea.*
1. This proposed roadway change will make traffic on a major road
(Alameda) which connects to another major road (University) even worse and
will cause traffic to flow into the west Washington Park neighborhood and
also increase traffic on Logan and Downing (which are already overloaded).
2. This type of change to Alameda (in the same stretch) had occurred in
2008-2009 but was reverted to the present design. There was too much
traffic backing up to keep the design. When asked how this change is going
to be better than the design in 2008...the r*esponse from Molly Lanphier
(DOTI CE 2294 Project Manager) is that there is less traffic on Alameda now
than in 2008 and thus makes this a good option. *I find this hard to
believe unless the road engineers are using traffic data from 2020-2022
when there was a pandemic and a lot of people were working from home.
Furthermore, a number of BIG apartment buildings on Alameda and Bannock
have been completed so the traffic has increased since 2008 NOT decreased.
3. It is stated that Alameda (Logan to Franklin) is a high accident area
and needs to be altered for safety. However, when I ask what the metrics
for success were, a general response of turning a four lane road to a two
lane road with a turning lane usually will cause a reduction of crashes
around 19-47%. No specific data to this stretch of Alameda was given.
4. I also asked if the grading of intersections for cars would be
considered as a metric. I have become aware that intersections are
graded (A-F) based on how many traffic light cycles need to be waited
through before a car can proceed through an intersection. Molly said she
did not know about this metric.

I have lived in Denver since 2008. From 2013 onward the roads have become
congested and I would agree more dangerous. However, a contributing factor
is that the *road engineers are not taking into account how to slow traffic
BUT keep it flowing. The fact is that the city is growing and novel ideas
should be considered - such as roundabouts.*

There were supposed to be more community outreach days regarding this
proposed change to Alameda but they were not scheduled. I received a
general email about 2 months ago stating the Alameda project was going
ahead. I emailed Molly a couple of times but it seems the decision has
been made.

This email is to let the mayor's office know that a lot of people do not
want this change to occur. *When the complaints of how bad traffic on this
stretch of Alameda start to come in, you will know the community did not
agree with this change but it was pushed through by the engineers on the
project.*

There is a part of me that hopes that you can put a stop to this change but
I truly feel it is a done deal. I just want you all to know that a lot of
people in the community do not want this change on Alameda to occur.

Sincerely,
Martina Hauptmann

Thank-you.



    Best Regards!

    Gail Griggs
    Office of The Mayor| City and County of Denver
    Pronouns<https://www.mypronouns.org/what-and-why> | She/Her/Hers
    phone: (720) 865-9090 | cell: (303) 829-2203
    311<https://www.denvergov.org/content/denvergov/en/denver-311-help-center.html> | denvergov.org<http://www.denvergov.org/> | Denver 8 TV<https://denver8.tv/> | Facebook<http://www.facebook.com/cityandcountyofdenver> | Twitter<https://twitter.com/CityofDenver> | Instagram<https://www.instagram.com/therealcityofdenver/>







From: Martina Hauptmann <martina.e.h@gmail.com<mailto:martina.e.h@gmail.com>>
Sent: Sunday, April 6, 2025 9:02 AM
To: Mayorsoffice <mayorsoffice@denvergov.org<mailto:mayorsoffice@denvergov.org>>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] No contact received
Hi,

I had contacted the mayor's office regarding the upcoming changes to Alameda.  I received an email on March 23rd stating that I would be contacted within 72 hours.  My case number is #10183067.

However, I have not received a response... whether the mayor has noted the potential of a traffic issue occurring and/or if he or his staff has looked into the details of this proposed change with the engineers at DOTI.

There will be a big traffic congestion problem with this proposed change.  This change on Alameda was done in 2008-2009 but was reverted to the present design because of traffic congestion.

The engineers state there are less people using Alameda now BUT the only way this is true is if the engineers are using data from 2020-2022 when people were working at home secondary to the pandemic.

I would like to know if this complaint has been noted or some action has been taken to understand the proposed changes to Alameda and if these changes make sense...

Sincerely,

Martina Hauptmann

f: 2025-04-15_09-26-27 - RE_ Organizing to stop lane reduction on Alameda.pdf
s: RE: Organizing to stop lane reduction on Alameda
cc: "Lanphier; Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering" <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>
b:
Alaina,

Good call. The outreach is on the project website, but I am including it here:

   *   Data Collection: October-December 2019 – Completed
   *   Analysis/ Alternatives Evaluation: January-March 2020 – Completed
   *   Public Outreach: May 2020 – Completed
   *   Design Procurement – August 2021 – Completed
   *   Design: Fall 2023 – Completed
   *   Public Outreach: March 2024 – Completed
   *   Construction Advertisement: Winter 2025
   *   Construction: mid-2025

This project was presented to the community in person on March 20, 2024 and virtually on May 19, 2020.

We heard from both sides of this project, many of those opposed to the project were commuters that drive the corridor, and many of the residents and businesses along the corridor support the project. Many are concerned with speeds and crashes that have occurred.

Lastly, we flyered the corridor yesterday (4/14) to provide businesses and residents information on the upcoming project. We did not hear any negative concerns or comments from those we met with.

Thanks,
Greg


Greg Cieciek, PLA | Community Designer
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Implementation Division
720.865.2944 Phone | 720.217.9125 Cell
gregory.cieciek@denvergov.org<mailto:gregory.cieciek@denvergov.org>

Subject: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction - Lincoln to Franklin

Morning Councilman Kashmann-

My colleague and I just went and flyered the businesses last week, letting them know that construction should begin in the summer and last approximately 4 months on a rolling basis. We will give them another heads up once we know exact timing at least 2 weeks before. We will also let you all know this as well to include in your communications. It will start at Lincoln and move its way to Franklin. We handed out the attached letter and are also going to post this information to the website in the coming days. I am happy to answer any other questions you may have.

Thank you!
Molly

To: Colton, Paige H. - DOTI CE0429 City Planner Senior <Paige.Colton@denvergov.org>; Melgoza, Rolando G. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Rolando.Melgoza@denvergov.org>; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>; Thompson, Maggie M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Maggie.Thompson@denvergov.org>; Zacharias, Kayla - DOTI CE0371 City Planner Associate <Kayla.Zacharias@denvergov.org>
Cc: Piper, Samuel - DOTI CE1566 City Planner Supervisor <Samuel.Piper@denvergov.org>; Vigil, Rachael - DOTI CE0397 Engineer <Rachael.Vigil@denvergov.org>; Najar, Kristine K. - DOTI CE2291 Project Manager I Engineering <Kristine.Najar@denvergov.org>; Good, Karen A. - DOTI CE3476 Project Manager Engineering Supervisor <Karen.Good@denvergov.org>
Subject: RE: Upcoming Ped Crossing Construction: Updated Outreach Approach

I have to meet with the Polo Club about another issue, so perhaps Kayla and I can tag team that meeting? However, I don’t want to give them too much time between when we meet and construction, so perhaps we wait until August? Happy to defer to Kayla as well on this.

Thanks!
Molly

From: Colton, Paige H. - DOTI CE0429 City Planner Senior <Paige.Colton@denvergov.org>
Sent: Wednesday, April 23, 2025 6:44 PM
To: Melgoza, Rolando G. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Rolando.Melgoza@denvergov.org>; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>; Lanphier, Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>; Thompson, Maggie M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Maggie.Thompson@denvergov.org>; Zacharias, Kayla - DOTI CE0371 City Planner Associate <Kayla.Zacharias@denvergov.org>
Cc: Piper, Samuel - DOTI CE1566 City Planner Supervisor <Samuel.Piper@denvergov.org>; Vigil, Rachael - DOTI CE0397 Engineer <Rachael.Vigil@denvergov.org>; Najar, Kristine K. - DOTI CE2291 Project Manager I Engineering <Kristine.Najar@denvergov.org>; Good, Karen A. - DOTI CE3476 Project Manager Engineering Supervisor <Karen.Good@denvergov.org>
Subject: Upcoming Ped Crossing Construction: Updated Outreach Approach

See below for our revised outreach strategy for the upcoming pedestrian crossing program construction package. As a reminder, here is the overall schedule we’re looking at:

   *   Construction Procurement – beginning of summer, late May
   *   Construction – Fall

Besides Council outreach, we are holding all the other outreach strategies until we start construction procurement since we’ll have a better timeline at that point. To confirm, is that the approach we want to take for the Steele/Alameda projects? These require a more complex level of outreach and I had written “as soon as possible”.

 Paige

Sent: Friday, April 11, 2025 1:15 PM
To: Melgoza, Rolando G. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Rolando.Melgoza@denvergov.org<mailto:Rolando.Melgoza@denvergov.org>>; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org<mailto:Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>>; Lanphier, Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>>; Thompson, Maggie M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Maggie.Thompson@denvergov.org<mailto:Maggie.Thompson@denvergov.org>>
Cc: Piper, Samuel - DOTI CE1566 City Planner Supervisor <Samuel.Piper@denvergov.org<mailto:Samuel.Piper@denvergov.org>>; Zacharias, Kayla - DOTI CE0371 City Planner Associate <Kayla.Zacharias@denvergov.org<mailto:Kayla.Zacharias@denvergov.org>>; Vigil, Rachael - DOTI CE0397 Engineer <Rachael.Vigil@denvergov.org<mailto:Rachael.Vigil@denvergov.org>>; Najar, Kristine K. - DOTI CE2291 Project Manager I Engineering <Kristine.Najar@denvergov.org<mailto:Kristine.Najar@denvergov.org>>; Good, Karen A. - DOTI CE3476 Project Manager Engineering Supervisor <Karen.Good@denvergov.org<mailto:Karen.Good@denvergov.org>>
Subject: Upcoming Ped Crossing Construction: Outreach Approach

Hi Community Design team,

I understand you met with Rachael and Sam last week to discuss our proposed outreach strategies for the Ped Crossing construction package that we’re looking to build this summer. I’m sending over a summary of each strategy below based on individual project impacts – let us know if you have any edits/comments. We’d like to then meet to discuss in more detail the High Risk projects – Kayla scheduled a meeting for 4/22.

You can see which outreach strategy (column AC) we’re aiming for on each project that’s moving into construction (column B = Pre-Construction) in our   project spreadsheet<https://denvercity-my.sharepoint.com/:x:/g/personal/rachael_vigil_denvergov_org/EWIOnSDPu5NHvNbWnBdfIloBezD258fs4sc3B3wuC0HV9A?e=nFODCx>. Designs are saved here: K:\DOTI_TI\SHARED\PROJECTS\24XXXX_2024 Ped Construction Package\Construction\Plans.

Typical Outreach (10 intersections, daylighting is only impact)
Notify Council these projects are coming - CD, as soon as you are able
Typical construction flyering (1 – 2 weeks prior to construction) - Contractor

Medium Risk Outreach (1 intersection: Bayaud/Utica – half block face of residential curbside impact)
Notify Council this project is coming – CD, as soon as you are able
Flyer adjacent property owners and summarize exact impacts (as soon as construction procurement begins) – Ped Crossing Program to draft flyers, possible assistance from CDs
Typical construction flyering (1 – 2 weeks prior to construction) - Contractor

High Risk Outreach (2 intersections: Lincoln/11th - 2 block faces (total) of downtown adjacent curbside impacts; MLK/Birch – closure of median access across MLK)
Brief Council on these projects – CD, as soon as you are able
Notify local RNOs/stakeholders/BID of upcoming project (as soon as construction procurement begins) – CD
Flyer adjacent property owners and summarize exact impacts (as soon as construction procurement begins) – Ped Crossing Program to draft flyers, possible assistance from CDs
Flyer adjacent property owners and summarize exact impacts (1+ month prior to construction) – Contractors
Typical construction flyering (1 – 2 weeks prior to construction) - Contractor

High Risk Outreach/Steele St Safety Improvements (2 intersections + NB improvements: Steele/Alameda + Steele/Exposition) – start right away
Activity        Purpose Responsible Party
Notify Councilwoman Sawyer      April   Molly
Meet with Polo Club     Review design, describe what changes are coming Kayla
Meet with 3200 Cherry Creek S Dr        Review design, describe what changes are coming Kayla
Provide email update & newsletter content to CW Sawyer  Remind her of changes coming, ask her to celebrate improvements in her newsletter       Community Design (Molly)
Send email to project list      Summarize improvements and construction timing  Kayla
Typical construction flyering   Alert residents/workers to upcoming construction        Contractor


Construction procurement – beginning of summer, May
Construction – Fall

 Paige

 Paige H. Colton | Senior City Planner
 City & County of Denver
 Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation & Mobility Planning
 Phone: (720) 913-4507
 Paige.Colton@denvergov.org<mailto:Paige.Colton@denvergov.org>
 Pronouns<https://www.mypronouns.org/what-and-why> | She/Her/Hers

 Upcoming out of the office: Thurs, 4/24 – Mon, 4/28, 2025

Sent: Wednesday, July 26, 2023 5:15 PM
To: Rems, Damoni C. - DOTI CE2291 Project Manager I Engineering <Damoni.Rems@denvergov.org<mailto:Damoni.Rems@denvergov.org>>; Robert W. Hatch II <rhatch@hatchlawyers.com<mailto:rhatch@hatchlawyers.com>>
Cc: Lucero, Nathan J. - CAO Asst City Attorney - Sr <Nathan.Lucero@denvergov.org<mailto:Nathan.Lucero@denvergov.org>>; Christopher N. Loy <cloy@hatchlawyers.com<mailto:cloy@hatchlawyers.com>>; Edington, Lisa M. - DOTI CE0431 Engineer Senior <Lisa.Edington@denvergov.org<mailto:Lisa.Edington@denvergov.org>>
Subject: RE: University Bv. Sidewalk Prelim. Design

All – Great to jump into this project & apologies in advance for any delays in my response time – I am transitioning to a new role and may have delays in replying. I have pulled the main points from original message below along with responses in italics. Please let me know if you have any other questions. Best, Karen

Here are additional questions raised by the Polo Club HOA Board of directors:
      -- Have the sidewalk plans been further revised or completed? We are still working on the revised plans and will share as soon as we have available, likely in September.

      -- Is there any way to reduce the width of the sidewalk to 4' or so?  That would make it easier to shovel snow and lessen the need to cut trees.  And, it would save the city money. The design currently only proposes the removal of three (3) trees at the north edge of the project.  The sidewalk width is determined by the guidelines for the street characteristics. This portion of University is a Parkway and a collector. The current design has the least impact to the trees as minimizing impact to trees was a design goal.
 
     -- Can the sidewalk be moved further east toward the current walls and fencing so it will reduce its proximity to fast-moving traffic?  Making it safer. As noted above we are using our design guidelines and other design guidance to help identify the appropriate distance between the sidewalk and the street and how the sidewalk meets bus stops and curb ramps at intersections.

      -- I know I've asked this question before, but we are hoping the city will accept the responsibility to maintain the sidewalk given that the walls along South University will make it virtually impossible for owners to get over or around them to access and shovel the sidewalk.  And, many of the owners are elderly, too.  We recognize that current ordinances impose that burden on homeowners, but given the circumstances here we are hopeful the city will find a way to maintain it.  The city does not plan to take on the responsibility of maintaining the sidewalk.

Karen Good, AICP | Transportation Project Manager

Subject: Alameda Lane Reduction



Hi Caleb – We are ready to procure construction services for the Alameda Lane Reduction project. RISE had committed $250k to this project and, as of now, only $60 has been allocated to the PRJ. Could you please help us allocate the remainder of the $250k to this project to fund construction? The latest PCW is attached for reference, which includes the $250k bond commitment.



Please let me know if you have any questions. Thank you!



Brett Boncore, PE| Engineering Supervisor

City & County of Denver

b:
Hi Brittany-

One option inside the Enterprise Map Viewer Form, is to leave all the “Search Criteria“ fields blank and then just click the “Search” button.
From there, a list of projects all projects you currently have access to will appear inside the “Search Results” table.
Select all the projects from the Search Results list- and then click the “Generate Map View” button, like circled.



From there, you can enter the specific location in the Address Search Bar (like highlighted), to determine if there are other projects in that location.


Thanks,

Emily Louie | Program Manager
City & County of Denver
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Program Management Division

Subject: RE: GIS Project Mapping

Patience/MW Team,

Do you or your team have a step by step guide for someone who wanted to use Masterworks to display a map of every project on a specific corridor or area? For example, I was asked yesterday to produce a map of all the projects on Alameda Ave. I tried to use Masterworks but couldn’t figure out how to look up projects by the map. (It seems like you have to already know the project names and then select to display them… what if I don’t know the names?)

I ended up using the One Build map tool… which I think gets its project data from Masterworks. Is this the best way?

Thanks,
Brittany

Subject: GIS Project Mapping

Directors, this is a friendly nudge about your teams’ projects with no GIS mapping in MW. Its only takes a few minutes and your PMD Port partner can support your PMs. See attached report.
As a reminder, GIS information gathered is being used to make an interactive map for the City website, to increase and bolster Public Communications, Outreach, Process Improvement, assist with budget inputs, and Project Analysis. Please note that drawing the project footprint in the Location Module is required unless it fits an exception listed below. PMD worked with Jason Smiley on the following.

Exceptions (No GIS Mapping Needed If Any of the Following Apply):
                     •       Projects with general scope statements that have "citywide" language do not need to map GIS locations say a plan for the entire city, etc.
                     •       Urgent response contracts, for example: An urgent response contract with random activities that don’t lend themselves to locations on maps (e.g., test holes on one project, traffic control on another, flow monitoring, pipe purchasing, etc.)

Thank you for your help.

Subject: Masterworks Monday Update #33

 Important Updates & Reminders:
   *   System Enhancements
        *   Sprint 1 of the “mini project” to address users’ feedback and requests is underway.
        *   The Change Order fix is being pushed back as the City has decided to invest the additonal time needed to ensure the right change is made the first time around and that the fix will align with City needs.
        *   UAT has been pushed back as Aurigo is behind schedule in creating test cases.
   *   Project Mapping
        *   The number of projects in Masterworks without their location mapped is rising. Mapping your project in Masterworks is required unless it falls into one of the exceptions listed below. This is an extremely important step as information gathered will be used to make an interactive map for the City website. If you haven’t already done so, map your project (see  <https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:b:/s/PWDocuments/pro/EaCtBBsw02lEhp0N_2decUoBJYwnyZQngYPotedyskxDWQ>  M06 Project Management 1 pg.106<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:b:/s/PWDocuments/pro/EaCtBBsw02lEhp0N_2decUoBJYwnyZQngYPotedyskxDWQ> for step-by-step instructions). The information provided will be critical to making the interactive map effort a success.
            Exceptions (no GIS mapping needed if any of the following apply)
   *   Projects with general scope statements that have ‘citywide’ language do not need to map GIS locations (i.e., Citywide Storm Drainage Master Plan).
   *   Urgent response contracts       
   *   An urgent response contract with random activities that don’t lend themselves to locations on maps (i.e., test holes on one project, traffic control on another, flow monitoring, pipe purchasing, etc.).
   *   Weekly Refresher Course
        *   This week we will be reverting to a once weekly training schedule and begin training following the project lifecycle. Trainings will be recorded and stored on the PMD SharePoint.
        *   Join us this Wednesday for our weekly Masterworks training from 10 am to 11 am. This week’s topic will be Creating a Planned Project & Managing Users in a Project.

            Join the training here<https://teams.microsoft.com/l/meetup-join/19%3ameeting_ZDY1YzViZjEtNDg2NC00MTczLWI0YTMtNWI0Y2U3ZGIxOWM1%40thread.v2/0?context=%7b%22Tid%22%3a%2239c87ab3-6612-42c0-9620-a696d12df803%22%2c%22Oid%22%3a%228b090184-0c8d-442a-9cf6-f8c38af85766%22%7d>
        *   See below for links to recordings of previous weeks’ sessions:
             *    Mapping Project Locations<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/ESwwpJfTJGlGjbsRCoZRGMgBZ6Dbm_zDtvaH7RwFgvFYvw?e=K7fBsr>   Funding Rules<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/EXtBqNr_VCxIuSCUUjreDVQBjHtGCqD_J9KztThafvjrRA?e=IPSYW1>   Budget Estimates<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/EexKeA1PaFlGlQx5ehQhDbABKyR71VD5MykaJvLjppYYsg?e=oqHdNo>  Budget Estimate Revisions<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/EeSd-akEOLxDh6xTUWgVy9gBOBc2nWWOgRAEgZjVN8sl1g?e=qjTs3N>
             *    Notifications<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/ESiW3vmeMuVLkPpNjReQeTEB1eD6tG3MaZEo-zrzpKGd_g?e=SALDKt>   Document Management <https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/EYnpYzEJn9JIrM9eaZMMCgkB3SJ9l4-Ai-Ii6JYv_I9d_Q?e=PNkYD6>    Task Order/Work Orders<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/Eb7CqrnYFY5PrgeCzS9JnDkB-TC5ISFLBWWwzx8iU8YzXw?e=29bUrN>  Construction Item Posting<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/Ef1e_zIpvjBFhR8uQ-S5P1MBZS7qBVlIteE0Uj3EDyDlxQ?e=fdbIKa>
             *    Auto-forecasting<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/Ed9Vb2aPq1NDuJ-0DOe31moBkpYMVkYJK-e6EsSM2Vx1rg?e=FC9okN>  CCP <https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/EUGFMI0_6zBNsWKpAxzI2JwBRfPaGOGS4ev3-32HKqkReQ?e=dYg6fV>    Item Quantities<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/EU1bqq4P3dBPnmqOqcF_IL8BZ3-0DDSyYFoBVFicBWdKuw?e=wq2sDV>  Split Funds <https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/ET2yzRQeu0xFtyij21NSZ6gBOuBig_DBM1RZMjOA9D1dUQ?e=pcP1PK>    Editing Distribution Details<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/r/sites/DOTIpro/PRO%20Library/Masterworks%20Resources/MW%20Weekly%20Trainings/Editing%20Distribution%20Details.mp4?csf=1&web=1&e=AhxWBs>
             *     KPIs<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/EW0deXm8iNNIh41ldJAjjuQBG7sel60OYoagftIUFVtjDg?e=iPmBl3>   PCOs<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:v:/s/DOTIpro/ETs5l6ls2PpMkS8xJehfXJcB2N0WVK4gsqwIWktLb60nrw?e=6Ho8vX>    Transcripts<https://denvercity.sharepoint.com/:f:/r/sites/DOTIpro/PRO%20Library/Masterworks%20Resources/MW%20Weekly%20Trainings/Transcripts?csf=1&web=1&e=eBlsxb>

 Did you know:
There is a Masterworks Collaboration Team that meets with PMD every two weeks to discuss and decide on major changes to the system or process changes, resolve conflicts, and address escalated issues/items. The team has representatives from the DOTI delivery groups as well as Contracts and Finance.  This team is also charged with communicating all decisions and updates back to their respective teams to ensure organization-wide alignment and implementation. The members are:

Jim Potter, City Engineer
Chuck Hart, Infra Rep
Lisa Hilt, Bridge Rep
Brad Chamberlin, Bridge Rep
Brittany Pirtle, Contracts Compliance Rep
Kelly Crum, Enviro Rep
Erica Bramlet, Finance Rep
Cara Sequino, IA Rep
Dave Ems, Procurement Rep
Dani Abbott, Procurement Rep
Amy Foreman, TI Rep
Katie Gargaro, TI Rep

 Masterworks Tip of the Week:
Instead of navigating through multiple steps in Masterworks to see which users are assigned to a workflow, one end user shared a faster method: check the email notification you receive! The notification includes the list of assigned users—saving you time and clicks.
Got a time-saving tip of your own? Send it our way!


  Angela Merritt | Project Manager I Engineering
  City & County of Denver
  Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Program Management Division
   Pronouns<https://www.mypronouns.org/what-and-why> | She/Her/Hers

b:
Hi all –

Thank you for the discussion today. I think we made a lot of progress in 30 minutes. Here are my notes from our meeting:

Two options were considered for restaffing active CMGC projects to help improve efficiency of the team. The following option will be moved forward:

Morrison

  *   Dallas H. (PM support - invoicing)
  *   Scott Reed (PM, staff aug)
Buchtel

  *   Eric P. (PM)
5280

  *   Sam B. (PM)
  *   Greg C. (PM Support)
Washington (Sept construction NTP)

  *   Mark G. – He will lead through Construction NTP, with plan to replace him in fall with rep from Construction team

Projects that may be held due to staffing issues (lack of construction PM), changes above will help:

  *   Federal Medians (Dallas)
  *   Federal/Amherst
  *   VZ Ped 2020/21 & Steele/Alameda (Kristine)
  *   SMS Colfax (remove)
  *   Mill 10
  *   Others? Please let me know. We need to compile a list.

Communication of staffing changes:

  *   Brittany to communicate to bond team and DOTI leadership
  *   Supervisors to communicate to staff who are being moved (Ken to talk with Dallas, Amy to talk with Sam, Brittany to talk with Mark, Katie to talk with Eric P, Brittany to talk with Greg)

Thanks,
Brittany

-----Original Appointment-----

Hi Dallas,

There has not been movement since Brett left. I hope to review the last call items he prepared this week.

Please note that there has been a bit of feedback from the community in the last week (they are concerned this project will negatively impact traffic on Alameda) and I am working to set up a meeting with the lobbyist who is helping us navigate and who is speaking directly with the concerned individuals. We have needed to elevate the issue and it may cause further delay on the construction advertisement.

Thanks,
Brittany

f: 2025-05-12_16-31-38 - FW_ Questions on E_ Alameda project____ (13).pdf
s: FW: Questions on E. Alameda project....
to: "Holloway; Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager" <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; "Hillhouse; Jennifer L. -
b:
Questions being asked… NK

From: Mark Samuelson <mark@samuelsonassoc.com>
Sent: Monday, May 12, 2025 4:02 PM
To: Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director <Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Questions on E. Alameda project....
Hi Nancy! Couple of questions on that, after having gone through the materials...


  1.  I assume basic goal remains to improve safety and reduce serious accidents? I read about the HIN accident corridor focus, and assume that’s still primary...
  2.  Went thru the video of the zoom meeting on this in 2021. Am I correct that the plan to close Alameda down to just two thru lanes at Downing and Corona is a new element to the plan? I thought I recall in the meeting that those intersections would remain as before.
  3.  Are you holding another public meeting before the start of construction on that? Are area residents aware of the final plan for the entire stretch, including at Downing and Corona?
  4.  When do you plan for construction to begin?
  5.  Looks like that 5-foot buffer on both the north and south sides of Alameda, east of Pearl to Humboldt. Are those to become bike lanes?  Would they have flex posts or other vertical structures?
  6.  Are you planning speed cameras as part of the project?
  7.  I read in your details that there had been a previous attempt to reduce the lanes a decade ago that had caused backups, and that the new plan is based on traffic counts that have been revised downward? When were those done?

Thanks, Nancy; feel free to call if I can clarify....

Mark Samuelson
The Denver Gazette
303-641-0196
mark@marksamuelson.com<mailto:mark@marksamuelson.com>
DenverGazette.com

  3.  Are you holding another public meeting before the start of construction on that? Are area residents aware of the final plan for the entire stretch, including at Downing and Corona?
  4.  When do you plan for construction to begin?
  5.  Looks like that 5-foot buffer on both the north and south sides of Alameda, east of Pearl to Humboldt. Are those to become bike lanes?  Would they have flex posts or other vertical structures?
  6.  Are you planning speed cameras as part of the project?
  7.  I read in your details that there had been a previous attempt to reduce the lanes a decade ago that had caused backups, and that the new plan is based on traffic counts that have been revised downward? When were those done?

Thanks, Nancy; feel free to call if I can clarify....

Mark Samuelson
The Denver Gazette
303-641-0196
mark@marksamuelson.com<mailto:mark@marksamuelson.com>
DenverGazette.com

f: 2025-05-14_15-11-18 - FW_ Questions on E_ Alameda project____ (1).pdf
s: FW: Questions on E. Alameda project....
to: "Cieciek; Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering" <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>;
cc: "Williams; Nicholas - DOTI Deputy Manager Internal & External Affairs"
b:
Here’s what I sent, for future reference, after various tweaks and fixing a few typos. 😊  I won’t be surprised to see a few follow up questions.  Thanks all. NK

From: Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director
Sent: Wednesday, May 14, 2025 3:09 PM
To: 'mark@marksamuelson.com' <mark@marksamuelson.com>
Subject: RE: Questions on E. Alameda project....

Hi Mark,

Starting with a little overview:

Yes, the basic goals are to improve safety and reduce crashes. Alameda Avenue is on our high injury network – the streets where most fatal and serious injury crashes are occurring in the city.  We began hearing concerns about safety on Alameda Ave between Lincoln and Franklin streets from the community a few years back. We did an analysis and found issues of speeding and vehicle rear-end and turning collisions due to a lack of left hand turn lanes. The community was also requesting pedestrian crossing improvements to help people get to the nearby school and businesses along Alameda more easily and safely, given all the vehicle activity.

With that, we developed a design that would work to slow vehicle speeds, reduce incidents of rear end and left turn collisions and minimize conflict for all users traveling along the corridor. The design does not change the lane configuration along Alameda from Lincoln to Logan. The design provides drivers one vehicle travel lane in each direction on Alameda Avenue between Pennsylvania and Franklin, adding a center turning lane in many sections for safer turning movements - a place for drivers to be while they wait to make a turn, preventing backups in the through-travel lane and the unsafe maneuvers drivers sometimes take to get around cars waiting to turn.

We went into the community last year to update residents on the proposed design.  Shortly thereafter, in June, we launched our SPEED initiative<https://denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/News-Outreach/News/Reduce-Traffic-Fatalities>, which is deploying other targeted actions to reduce speeds on Alameda Avenue from city limit to city limit.   The SPEED measures we have completed since June include:

   *   Saturating the corridor with speed limit signage, increasing sign density to approximately every ¼ mile to raise awareness of the speed limit.
   *   Placing additional electronic feedback signs along the corridor to communicate driver speeds.
   *   Programming traffic signals to slow vehicles down to the posted speed limits.
   *   Shortening signal cycle lengths in the overnight hours, reducing the amount of time that signals rest in green, discouraging speeding when vehicle volumes are low.
   *   Increasing the number of intersections on Federal and Alameda with leading pedestrian intervals, or LPIs. LPIs give pedestrians a head start crossing the street and an opportunity to establish themselves in an intersection for greater visibility before cars get the green.
   *   Worked with Xcel to increase visibility on the corridors by addressing any damaged/dark street lights
   *   Completed a Slow Down for the Love of Safety billboard campaign, telling people to Slow Down. Picture attached.

Up next: The SPEED initiative also calls for deploying fixed, automated speed enforcement cameras on Alameda Ave and Federal Blvd.  We just learned that in late spring/early summer, we will have a chance to try out the cameras – not for speed enforcement (people won’t be getting tickets) -  but to see how they work in terms of gauging speeds as well as the number of people who are speeding on the corridor and how our interventions have worked so far.

The testing of these cameras may push out our implementation of the lane repurposing project a bit, also affording us some time to address any new or lingering community concerns about the lane repurposing plan.

Responses to your questions are below.

I assume basic goal remains to improve safety and reduce serious accidents? I read about the HIN accident corridor focus, and assume that’s still primary...
Correct

Went thru the video of the zoom meeting on this in 2021. Am I correct that the plan to close Alameda down to just two thru lanes at Downing and MARION is a new element to the plan? I thought I recall in the meeting that those intersections would remain as before.  There are currently two travel lanes on Alameda in each direction at the intersections of Downing and Marion and no left turns are permitted from Alameda onto Downing or Marion.  Here, it will go to one lane in each direction, consistent with what’s happening on either side.  No left turns will be permitted, as is the case right now.  The center or middle travel lanes of Alameda at these intersections will be re-striped to offer a safer pedestrian crossing. Pedestrians will cross two vehicle travel lanes on Alameda instead of four, and the striping plan will provide a mid-block pedestrian refuge. Here’s a visual of that:



Are you holding another public meeting before the start of construction on that? Are area residents aware of the final plan for the entire stretch, including at Downing and MARION?  Below is a look at some of the outreach we’ve done so far and recently. We typically engage the community through traditional media and social media as we get closer to construction and will review what other methods might be needed or helpful as we get closer.  My understanding is that businesses along this stretch have been supportive.

Data Collection: October-December 2019
Analysis/ Alternatives Evaluation: January-March 2020
Public Outreach: May 2020
Design Procurement – August 2021
Design: Fall 2023
Public Outreach: 3/20/24
Constant Contact Notification on 2/4/25 – attached
Flyered corridor on April 14, 2025 – attached

When do you plan for construction to begin?   No confirmed date.  Per note above, we want to test out the fixed camera technology on Alameda and Federal; that may adjust the anticipated schedule for the Alameda lane repurposing work, which was planned for mid-year/summer 2025.

Looks like that 5-foot buffer on both the north and south sides of Alameda, east of Pearl to Humboldt. Are those to become bike lanes?  Would they have flex posts or other vertical structures?  No plans to install a bike lane.  The buffer supports narrowing the roadway to one lane in each direction.

Are you planning speed cameras as part of the project?  Speed cameras are a separate effort, but yes, our goal is to deploy fixed, automatic speed camera technology, starting on Federal and Alameda corridors. We will also be doing community outreach and engagement with the public to discuss the program, how it would work, the technology and our approach to safety as part of the program development.

I read in your details that there had been a previous attempt to reduce the lanes a decade ago that had caused backups, and that the new plan is based on traffic counts that have been revised downward? When were those done?  DOTI installed a lane reduction on Alameda back in the 2008-2010 timeframe in the eastbound direction at Downing, which resulted in a stacking of vehicles on the eastbound approach to Downing Street. During that time, the Alameda average daily traffic was over 20,000.  Average daily traffic on that stretch is now much lower, so Alameda is a better candidate now for a lane reduction.

Thanks, Nancy; feel free to call if I can clarify....

Mark Samuelson
The Denver Gazette
303-641-0196
mark@marksamuelson.com<mailto:mark@marksamuelson.com>
DenverGazette.com

f: 2025-05-23_10-18-29 - RE_ Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders.pdf
s: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
to: "Price; Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager" <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>; "Cieciek;
cc: "Boncore; Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor" <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>;
b:
Hi Jason-

Sorry for the delay. Brittany asked me to coordinate some times that may work for you all. I know school is almost out and I would assume this will affect traffic patterns. It looks like Steele Elementary starts at 8:05 am and ends at 3:05pm. Given that, could you make either May 30th at 2:30pm or June 2 at 7:30 am work? If not, we can go further out. I just know DPS’ last day is June 3rd.

Thank you!
Molly

Molly Lanphier, MPH | Community Designer
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Implementation Division
720.913.4549 Phone | 720.619.1018 Cell
Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org>

CONNECT WITH US | 311<https://www.denvergov.org/content/denvergov/en/denver-311-help-center.html> | pocketgov.com<http://www.pocketgov.com/> | denvergov.org<http://www.denvergov.org/> | Denver 8 TV<https://denver8.tv/> | Facebook<http://www.facebook.com/cityandcountyofdenver>




From: Jason Gallardo <jason@mgpublicaffairs.com<mailto:jason@mgpublicaffairs.com>>
Sent: Wednesday, May 14, 2025 9:44 AM
To: Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org<mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>>
Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org<mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Re: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
Hi Brittany,

Thanks for reaching out. Yes, I agree that it would be helpful to get everyone together—including the concerned neighbors—for a productive discussion about the Alameda Lane Reduction project.

We’d prefer an in-person meeting that includes a walkthrough of the area during peak traffic hours so we can see the impacts firsthand. This will give us all a better understanding of the issues being raised.

Some of the primary concerns we’ve heard include:
      •     Turn lane backups and overflow into through lanes, causing significant traffic delays
      •     Increased neighborhood cut-through traffic, raising safety concerns due to speeding and reckless driving
      •     Higher vehicle volumes on Virginia, which isn’t designed to support that level of traffic, possibly requiring more DOTI involvement

Please let me know your availability and any proposed dates/times that work on your end, and we can coordinate from there.

Best,


Jason A. Gallardo J.D.
Partner | MG Public Affairs
www.mgpublicaffairs.com<https:/www.mgpublicaffairs.com>
M: 970-366-1128
________________________________
From: Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org<mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>>
Sent: Monday, May 12, 2025 3:30:30 PM
To: Jason Gallardo <jason@mgpublicaffairs.com<mailto:jason@mgpublicaffairs.com>>
Cc: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>>; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org<mailto:Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>>
Subject: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting


Hi Jason,



I wanted to coordinate on scheduling a meeting to further discuss the Alameda Lane Reduction project. Its my understanding in talking with Tykus that you’d like to get us and the concerned neighbors together to talk through their concerns.



What timing and format were you thinking for this meeting?



Thanks,

Brittany



Brittany Price, PE| Engineering Manager

City & County of Denver
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Implementation - Design

Phone: 720-865-3154

brittany.price@denvergov.org<mailto:brittany.price@denvergov.org>

To: Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org<mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>>
Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org<mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Re: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
Hi Brittany,

Thanks for reaching out. Yes, I agree that it would be helpful to get everyone together—including the concerned neighbors—for a productive discussion about the Alameda Lane Reduction project.

We’d prefer an in-person meeting that includes a walkthrough of the area during peak traffic hours so we can see the impacts firsthand. This will give us all a better understanding of the issues being raised.

Some of the primary concerns we’ve heard include:
      •     Turn lane backups and overflow into through lanes, causing significant traffic delays
      •     Increased neighborhood cut-through traffic, raising safety concerns due to speeding and reckless driving
      •     Higher vehicle volumes on Virginia, which isn’t designed to support that level of traffic, possibly requiring more DOTI involvement

Please let me know your availability and any proposed dates/times that work on your end, and we can coordinate from there.

Best,


Jason A. Gallardo J.D.
Partner | MG Public Affairs
www.mgpublicaffairs.com<https:/www.mgpublicaffairs.com>
M: 970-366-1128
  ________________________________

Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org<mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Re: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
Hi Brittany,

Thanks for reaching out. Yes, I agree that it would be helpful to get everyone together—including the concerned neighbors—for a productive discussion about the Alameda Lane Reduction project.

We’d prefer an in-person meeting that includes a walkthrough of the area during peak traffic hours so we can see the impacts firsthand. This will give us all a better understanding of the issues being raised.

Some of the primary concerns we’ve heard include:
      •     Turn lane backups and overflow into through lanes, causing significant traffic delays
      •     Increased neighborhood cut-through traffic, raising safety concerns due to speeding and reckless driving
      •     Higher vehicle volumes on Virginia, which isn’t designed to support that level of traffic, possibly requiring more DOTI involvement

Please let me know your availability and any proposed dates/times that work on your end, and we can coordinate from there.

Best,


Jason A. Gallardo J.D.
Partner | MG Public Affairs
www.mgpublicaffairs.com<https:/www.mgpublicaffairs.com>
M: 970-366-1128

Subject: [EXTERNAL] Re: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
Hi Brittany,



Thanks for reaching out. Yes, I agree that it would be helpful to get everyone together—including the concerned neighbors—for a productive discussion about the Alameda Lane Reduction project.



We’d prefer an in-person meeting that includes a walkthrough of the area during peak traffic hours so we can see the impacts firsthand. This will give us all a better understanding of the issues being raised.



Some of the primary concerns we’ve heard include:

      •     Turn lane backups and overflow into through lanes, causing significant traffic delays

      •     Increased neighborhood cut-through traffic, raising safety concerns due to speeding and reckless driving

      •     Higher vehicle volumes on Virginia, which isn’t designed to support that level of traffic, possibly requiring more DOTI involvement



Please let me know your availability and any proposed dates/times that work on your end, and we can coordinate from there.



Best,





Jason A. Gallardo J.D.

Partner | MG Public Affairs

www.mgpublicaffairs.com<https:/www.mgpublicaffairs.com>

f: 2025-06-05_20-31-45 - _EXTERNAL_ Concerns about Alameda Lane Repurposing project.pdf
s: Concerns about Alameda Lane Repurposing project
b:
Dear Ms. Ford,
I am writing to you as a resident of the neighborhood where DOTI’s “Alameda Lane Repurposing” project is slated to take place this summer. (I live at E Alameda Ave & Race St.) As a daily driver and pedestrian in this area, I have serious concerns about the project, which I believe are shared by many other residents – if they’re even aware.
I’ve summarized three of my concerns below. I would appreciate any consideration you can give to this.
First, neighborhood access to E Alameda Ave will be significantly compromised. Neighbors like me relying on Alameda to exit the area will face longer delays, especially during peak times. I live a couple blocks off Alameda and already struggle to turn onto Alameda from my residential street. It’s not uncommon for me to wait 8-10 minutes to turn left onto Alameda when I’m taking my kids to school in the morning. (This is truly not an exaggeration! I have timed it with my phone’s stopwatch many times to plan for our school route.) For example, when traffic that is currently flowing in two lanes is combined into a single lane and then stopped at the lights near Downing St, more residential streets will be effectively blocked during the light cycle by the increased backup, which will slow residents’ efforts to turn onto Alameda and move on to other areas.
I’m also very concerned about the areas where DOTI plans to block left turns from Alameda onto residential streets with medians or other structures; I worry that an unintended consequence of that plan is that residents on those cross streets will no longer be able to turn left from their street onto Alameda. The impact of these changes is exacerbated by the location of Wash Park to the south and the Denver Country Club to the north of E Alameda Ave along the stretch that will be modified; these large landmarks limit the ways we can access our residential streets and areas.
Now that DOTI has “repurposed” a number of lanes in other residential areas, people in those communities report that frustrated drivers divert to their quiet residential streets. Often these drivers who divert drive aggressively to vent their feelings about the change in the traffic patterns. There is so much pedestrian traffic throughout these residential cross-streets. It would be an error to divert traffic there!
This leads to my next point: Unintended safety risks will emerge after the project is implemented. I have carefully reviewed all the publicly available information about this project. I was shocked to see that DOTI’s plans anticipate that traffic from Alameda will divert to Virginia Avenue as a result of the changes and DOTI does not see this as an issue. Virginia Avenue is a quiet, residential street that is adjacent to one of our city’s busiest parks, Wash Park. This street lacks the infrastructure needed to safely handle increased traffic volumes. I am extremely concerned about new safety issues for pedestrians, cyclists and park-goers if there is more traffic on Virginia Ave. I’d anticipate that this will require future intervention by DOTI.
Lastly, DOTI has not conducted enough community engagement around this project. I have mentioned this project to dozens of residents in this area and not a single person is aware of the plans. (Once aware, they share my surprise at what's proposed!) DOTI conducted just one community meeting about this project in early 2024 and then provided no new information for over a year. I really do not believe this will be well received. In the scope of DOTI’s projects, I imagine this is not a major one. But to residents of these neighborhoods and regular commuters along this stretch of Alameda, this will be a massive change to their daily route that likely adds time and frustration. I encourage DOTI to further engage the community about this project and determine if the benefits truly outweigh the negatives.
Sincerely,
Jill Anschutz
Jill.hamilton@mac.com<mailto:Jill.hamilton@mac.com>
502.938.2947

	We’d prefer an in-person meeting that includes a walkthrough of the area during peak traffic hours so we can see the impacts firsthand. This will give us all a better understanding of the issues being raised.

f: 2025-06-06_08-58-33 - RE_ _EXTERNAL_ Concerns about Alameda Lane Repurposing proje.pdf
s: RE: Concerns about Alameda Lane Repurposing project
cc: "Holloway; Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager" <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; "Cieciek; Gregory M. -
b:
Jill,

Thank you very much for your note and thoughtful insight about the proposed project. I’ve included our team members who are working on this plan in this email response and am aware that perhaps the team is working to organize a meeting with the key stakeholders and community members to discuss the project in more detail. We would like to include you in that discussion.

We appreciate that when we embark on projects like this to seek to address some of the many safety issues along the corridor (regular crashes, many of which attach to the left hand turn movements of folks, the queuing in the lanes and folks driving around), as well as safety issues with crossings for pedestrians and other, that how we approach the engineering solutions needs to be carefully reviewed.

We look forward to continuing the discussion with you and the community.

Amy



Amy Ford | Executive Director
City & County of Denver
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure
Phone: (303) 514-4913
amy.ford@denvergov.org<mailto:amy.ford@denvergov.org>

[cid:image001.png@01DBD6C0.C88B2A30]

From: Jill Anschutz <jill.hamilton@mac.com>
Sent: Thursday, June 5, 2025 8:32 PM
To: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Concerns about Alameda Lane Repurposing project
Dear Ms. Ford,
I am writing to you as a resident of the neighborhood where DOTI’s “Alameda Lane Repurposing” project is slated to take place this summer. (I live at E Alameda Ave & Race St.) As a daily driver and pedestrian in this area, I have serious concerns about the project, which I believe are shared by many other residents – if they’re even aware.
I’ve summarized three of my concerns below. I would appreciate any consideration you can give to this.
First, neighborhood access to E Alameda Ave will be significantly compromised. Neighbors like me relying on Alameda to exit the area will face longer delays, especially during peak times. I live a couple blocks off Alameda and already struggle to turn onto Alameda from my residential street. It’s not uncommon for me to wait 8-10 minutes to turn left onto Alameda when I’m taking my kids to school in the morning. (This is truly not an exaggeration! I have timed it with my phone’s stopwatch many times to plan for our school route.) For example, when traffic that is currently flowing in two lanes is combined into a single lane and then stopped at the lights near Downing St, more residential streets will be effectively blocked during the light cycle by the increased backup, which will slow residents’ efforts to turn onto Alameda and move on to other areas.
I’m also very concerned about the areas where DOTI plans to block left turns from Alameda onto residential streets with medians or other structures; I worry that an unintended consequence of that plan is that residents on those cross streets will no longer be able to turn left from their street onto Alameda. The impact of these changes is exacerbated by the location of Wash Park to the south and the Denver Country Club to the north of E Alameda Ave along the stretch that will be modified; these large landmarks limit the ways we can access our residential streets and areas.
Now that DOTI has “repurposed” a number of lanes in other residential areas, people in those communities report that frustrated drivers divert to their quiet residential streets. Often these drivers who divert drive aggressively to vent their feelings about the change in the traffic patterns. There is so much pedestrian traffic throughout these residential cross-streets. It would be an error to divert traffic there!
This leads to my next point: Unintended safety risks will emerge after the project is implemented. I have carefully reviewed all the publicly available information about this project. I was shocked to see that DOTI’s plans anticipate that traffic from Alameda will divert to Virginia Avenue as a result of the changes and DOTI does not see this as an issue. Virginia Avenue is a quiet, residential street that is adjacent to one of our city’s busiest parks, Wash Park. This street lacks the infrastructure needed to safely handle increased traffic volumes. I am extremely concerned about new safety issues for pedestrians, cyclists and park-goers if there is more traffic on Virginia Ave. I’d anticipate that this will require future intervention by DOTI.
Lastly, DOTI has not conducted enough community engagement around this project. I have mentioned this project to dozens of residents in this area and not a single person is aware of the plans. (Once aware, they share my surprise at what's proposed!) DOTI conducted just one community meeting about this project in early 2024 and then provided no new information for over a year. I really do not believe this will be well received. In the scope of DOTI’s projects, I imagine this is not a major one. But to residents of these neighborhoods and regular commuters along this stretch of Alameda, this will be a massive change to their daily route that likely adds time and frustration. I encourage DOTI to further engage the community about this project and determine if the benefits truly outweigh the negatives.
Sincerely,
Jill Anschutz
Jill.hamilton@mac.com<mailto:Jill.hamilton@mac.com>
502.938.2947

Dear Ms Ford,

I am part of a community of neighbors who are very concerned with DOTI's upcoming planned "Alameda Lane Repurposing" project. I live on Marion Pkwy, just north of Alameda (between Cedar and Alameda) and use this and the surrounding streets every single day to get to and from my home. I also have a busy small business in Wash Park (at Kentucky and Pearl) that I have operated for 11 years and myself and clients rely heavily on Alameda and the streets surrounding to get to and from my business.

Alemeda is one of the major roads Wash Park residents and visitors rely on to get to their homes, the park, businesses and schools in the community. To reduce the capacity of this street to two lanes will significantly impact traffic in the neighborhood. Not only will Alameda itself get overcrowded, but I am specifically concerned about the impact and crowding that will ultimately spill over onto Downing (which is already extremely backed-up in Wash Park during rush hour), access to University and the ability to get to Cherry Creek North (the only other major access from Wash Park is Speer, which itself is overcrowded) and Marion Pkwy (which is a major host to bicyclists, kids at Steele Elementary School and access to the extremely busy Washington Park). The last thing this busy neighborhood needs is less room on this major thoroughfare and more traffic. It will force people to divert into the neighborhood streets, endangering pedestrians with speeding cars trying to "cut-through" to get to their destinations faster. I already am a witness first-hand the danger that cars traveling on these "side streets" pose to kids, bikers, scooters and runners that are headed in and out of the park, specifically on Marion Pkwy and Virgina at the north entrance into the park itself. I also personally find it nearly impossible to currently turn west out of my alley and onto Alameda in the mornings due to an already overcrowded street with the current configuration. If and when it goes to two lanes, I and my neighbors on the north side of Alameda might truly not be able to access Alameda out of our alley at all during rush hour due to a complete back-up of cars.

As a resident and business owner in Wash Park, I send this email with deep concern about the impact of this project. Driving Alameda daily, I know the impact fewer lanes will cause. Wash Park residents use Alemeda to access their home streets, and that alone (someone waiting to turn off onto their street) will cause a HUGE backup consistently. Plus, I do wonder how the myriad of businesses on Alameda feel about the fewer lanes, thus, less people and less access of clients to their businesses. I hope that DOTI will consider these negative impacts and choose to pause this project to adequately assess its reasoning and to also reach out and engage more input from the community it will impact first-hand.

Thanks for taking the time to read and consider,

Kimberly Kolesar
kim@psdenver.com<mailto:kim@psdenver.com>
925-200-6260

--

Kimberly Kolesar
OWNER | THE PILATES STUDIO
Wash Park-Highlands
Nationally Certified Pilates Teacher, NCPT
303-733-3833
kim@psdenver.com<mailto:amy@psdenver.com>

Thanks!

Molly

From: Jason Gallardo <jason@mgpublicaffairs.com<mailto:jason@mgpublicaffairs.com>>
Sent: Wednesday, May 14, 2025 9:44 AM
To: Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org<mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>>
Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org<mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Re: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
Hi Brittany,



Thanks for reaching out. Yes, I agree that it would be helpful to get everyone together—including the concerned neighbors—for a productive discussion about the Alameda Lane Reduction project.



We’d prefer an in-person meeting that includes a walkthrough of the area during peak traffic hours so we can see the impacts firsthand. This will give us all a better understanding of the issues being raised.



Some of the primary concerns we’ve heard include:

      •     Turn lane backups and overflow into through lanes, causing significant traffic delays

      •     Increased neighborhood cut-through traffic, raising safety concerns due to speeding and reckless driving

      •     Higher vehicle volumes on Virginia, which isn’t designed to support that level of traffic, possibly requiring more DOTI involvement



Please let me know your availability and any proposed dates/times that work on your end, and we can coordinate from there.



Best,





Jason A. Gallardo J.D.

Partner | MG Public Affairs

www.mgpublicaffairs.com<https:/www.mgpublicaffairs.com>

M: 970-366-1128

________________________________

From: Jason Gallardo <jason@mgpublicaffairs.com<mailto:jason@mgpublicaffairs.com>>
Sent: Wednesday, May 14, 2025 9:44 AM
To: Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org<mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>>
Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org<mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Re: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
Hi Brittany,



Thanks for reaching out. Yes, I agree that it would be helpful to get everyone together—including the concerned neighbors—for a productive discussion about the Alameda Lane Reduction project.



We’d prefer an in-person meeting that includes a walkthrough of the area during peak traffic hours so we can see the impacts firsthand. This will give us all a better understanding of the issues being raised.



Some of the primary concerns we’ve heard include:

      •     Turn lane backups and overflow into through lanes, causing significant traffic delays

      •     Increased neighborhood cut-through traffic, raising safety concerns due to speeding and reckless driving

      •     Higher vehicle volumes on Virginia, which isn’t designed to support that level of traffic, possibly requiring more DOTI involvement



Please let me know your availability and any proposed dates/times that work on your end, and we can coordinate from there.



Best,





Jason A. Gallardo J.D.

Partner | MG Public Affairs

www.mgpublicaffairs.com<https:/www.mgpublicaffairs.com>

M: 970-366-1128

________________________________

f: 2025-06-11_22-19-29 - _EXTERNAL_ Concerns about Proposed Changes to East Alameda A.pdf
s: Concerns about Proposed Changes to East Alameda Ave
b:
Ms. Ford, Mr. Holloway and Mr. Williams,
I am a resident and homeowner in Central / East Denver (80220) and am reaching out to express concerns about proposed changes to East Alameda Avenue, which I read about in the Denver Gazette a few weeks ago: https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html<https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html>


I count on East Alameda as a primary east / west thoroughfare and am concerned that the proposed changes will reduce capacity and result in increased congestion, and related safety concerns. I worry, particularly, for local residents who will see increased traffic on side roads that are not built to handle that volume.


I believe you all have good intentions but worry that there will be unintended consequences from these changes. I urge you to slow down this process, do further engagement with impacted Denverites and carefully consider the risks of the proposed changes.


Thank you for your consideration.
Cody Belzley
Denver, CO 80220
303 913 1453

f: 2025-06-12_09-58-27 - _EXTERNAL_ Heads Up DSST Team___more traffic headed your way.pdf
s: Heads Up DSST Team...more traffic headed your way!
b:
Dear DOTI,
I recently read an article in the Denver Gazette about the East Alameda Lane Repurposing project that left me wondering how cutting the number of lanes on Alameda could possibly be a good idea.
https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html<https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html>



As a DPS parent who takes my kids to school in that area every morning (and thus drives Alameda often) I am concerned about the ramifications to what is already a busy street. Our school (DSST Cedar) already struggles with traffic congestion at both drop off and pick up. And getting in and out of the neighborhood already requires that I build in quite a bit of extra travel time. There are actually mornings where I sit on Alameda waiting for the light at University to cycle through for upwards of 10-12 minutes. I can't see how your plan to reduce lanes will improve traffic. I'm also concerned that this will make commuters use the side streets – which I assume is less efficient, less safe and not a great solution for the people who live/work/go to school in the neighborhood.


I've added the leadership at DSST to make sure they are aware of your plans. Traffic is already an issue for our little community...and I can't begin to see how this will help.


I hope you'll reconsider.


Amy Guttmann

To: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org<mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>>; Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org<mailto:Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>>; Williams, Nicholas - DOTI Deputy Manager Internal & External Affairs <Nicholas.Williams@denvergov.org<mailto:Nicholas.Williams@denvergov.org>>; Bailey Burkhardt <bailey.manning@scienceandtech.org<mailto:bailey.manning@scienceandtech.org>>; kim.martin@scienceandtech.org<mailto:kim.martin@scienceandtech.org> <kim.martin@scienceandtech.org<mailto:kim.martin@scienceandtech.org>>; webster.johnson@scienceandtech.org<mailto:webster.johnson@scienceandtech.org> <webster.johnson@scienceandtech.org<mailto:webster.johnson@scienceandtech.org>>; elin.curry@scienceandtech.org<mailto:elin.curry@scienceandtech.org> <elin.curry@scienceandtech.org<mailto:elin.curry@scienceandtech.org>>; Casias, Angela C. - MO Executive Officer <Angela.Casias@denvergov.org<mailto:Angela.Casias@denvergov.org>>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Heads Up DSST Team...more traffic headed your way!

Dear DOTI, I recently read an article in the Denver Gazette about the East Alameda Lane Repurposing project that left me wondering how cutting the number of lanes on Alameda could possibly be a good idea. https: //denvergazette. com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c. html

Dear DOTI,
I recently read an article in the Denver Gazette about the East Alameda Lane Repurposing project that left me wondering how cutting the number of lanes on Alameda could possibly be a good idea.
https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html<https:/denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html>

As a DPS parent who takes my kids to school in that area every morning (and thus drives Alameda often) I am concerned about the ramifications to what is already a busy street. Our school (DSST Cedar) already struggles with traffic congestion at both drop off and pick up. And getting in and out of the neighborhood already requires that I build in quite a bit of extra travel time. There are actually mornings where I sit on Alameda waiting for the light at University to cycle through for upwards of 10-12 minutes. I can't see how your plan to reduce lanes will improve traffic. I'm also concerned that this will make commuters use the side streets – which I assume is less efficient, less safe and not a great solution for the people who live/work/go to school in the neighborhood.

I've added the leadership at DSST to make sure they are aware of your plans. Traffic is already an issue for our little community...and I can't begin to see how this will help.

I hope you'll reconsider.

Amy Guttmann

f: 2025-06-13_16-35-00 - _EXTERNAL_ Concerns about proposed changes to Alameda.pdf
s: Concerns about proposed changes to Alameda
b:
Dear Ms. Ford,

I'm writing to express a few concerns about changes to Alameda Avenue that recently have been proposed.

My primary concern is with the idea of repurposing traffic lines along Alameda, turning four lanes of traffic into just two, as referenced in a recent article from the Denver Gazette<https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html>.

Turning travel lanes into turn lanes will not reduce the need for vehicle traffic through this corridor, but it will reduce overall capacity. The result? Increased traffic congestion. This will create backups into the proposed new turn lanes and remaining travel lanes, especially during high-traffic times of day.

I'm also concerned about ripple effects. While drivers may avoid Alameda, it is likely that they will continue to travel by vehicle through the neighborhood, but use residential streets instead, especially Virginia Avenue, which is not equipped to handle increased traffic. New safety issues will be created by the attempt to address safety issues along Alameda.

Furthermore, I have not seen compelling safety data to justify this redesign. Instead, it seems like an expensive project based on theoretical benefits rather than real-world conditions or a strong desire from the community.

I'm troubled by the lack of data to justify this change and by the lack of community engagement to create a thoughtful approach to addressing legitimate community concerns.

I strongly urge DOTI to slow down this project and engage the neighborhood in a broader, more productive dialogue to address community level traffic and safety concerns.

Sincerely,

Illana Poley
