Subject: [EXTERNAL] Re: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
Hi Brittany,



Thanks for reaching out. Yes, I agree that it would be helpful to get everyone together—including the concerned neighbors—for a productive discussion about the Alameda Lane Reduction project.



We’d prefer an in-person meeting that includes a walkthrough of the area during peak traffic hours so we can see the impacts firsthand. This will give us all a better understanding of the issues being raised.



Some of the primary concerns we’ve heard include:

      •     Turn lane backups and overflow into through lanes, causing significant traffic delays

      •     Increased neighborhood cut-through traffic, raising safety concerns due to speeding and reckless driving

      •     Higher vehicle volumes on Virginia, which isn’t designed to support that level of traffic, possibly requiring more DOTI involvement



Please let me know your availability and any proposed dates/times that work on your end, and we can coordinate from there.



Best,





Jason A. Gallardo J.D.

b:
Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering invited you to edit a folder



  Hi all,

  I set up a shared folder for the various Alameda Lane Repurposing emails. I placed the five Amy received from various residents, including her response to Jill Anschutz. I am working to develop a standard response to these emails based on Tykus' comment in the chat. I will send that out for review prior to responding.

f: 2025-06-17_17-25-15 - Upcoming Ped Crossing Construction_ Council Notification Rem.pdf
s: Upcoming Ped Crossing Construction: Council Notification Reminder
to: "Melgoza; Rolando G. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering"
cc: "Piper; Samuel - DOTI CE3124 City Planning Manager" <Samuel.Piper@denvergov.org>; "Vigil; Rachael -
b:
Hi Community Design Team,

I wanted to update you all that we’re still on track to get the package of pedestrian crossing projects referenced below out to bid this month and starting construction this fall. To that end, Kayla and I are working over the next this month to develop informational flyers for the high/medium risk projects and the Steele/Alameda projects. Can you prioritize notifying Council about all of the projects (all risk levels) by mid-July as well? Please let us know if you hear any issues or concerns from them. Starting in mid-July, we’ll target notifying RNOs/any relevant stakeholders, flyer the corridors of the high/medium risk projects, and meet with the two Steele/Alameda stakeholders.

Please mark when you’ve notified council in column U of   this spreadsheet<https://denvercity-my.sharepoint.com/:x:/r/personal/rachael_vigil_denvergov_org/Documents/Documents/Rachael%27s%20Documents/Projects/Master%20Ped%20Document.xlsx?d=w209d0e62bbcf4793bcd6d69c175f225a&csf=1&web=1&e=G006RG>. As a reminder, all the projects in column F marked as “Pre Construction” are those that are in this package. Greg, I’ve noted that you notified Hinds about Lincoln/11th.

Thanks all,

 Paige

Subject: Upcoming Ped Crossing Construction: Outreach Approach

Hi Community Design team,

I understand you met with Rachael and Sam last week to discuss our proposed outreach strategies for the Ped Crossing construction package that we’re looking to build this summer. I’m sending over a summary of each strategy below based on individual project impacts – let us know if you have any edits/comments. We’d like to then meet to discuss in more detail the High Risk projects – Kayla scheduled a meeting for 4/22.

You can see which outreach strategy (column AC) we’re aiming for on each project that’s moving into construction (column B = Pre-Construction) in our   project spreadsheet<https://denvercity-my.sharepoint.com/:x:/g/personal/rachael_vigil_denvergov_org/EWIOnSDPu5NHvNbWnBdfIloBezD258fs4sc3B3wuC0HV9A?e=nFODCx>. Designs are saved here: K:\DOTI_TI\SHARED\PROJECTS\24XXXX_2024 Ped Construction Package\Construction\Plans.

Typical Outreach (10 intersections, daylighting is only impact)
Notify Council these projects are coming - CD, as soon as you are able
Typical construction flyering (1 – 2 weeks prior to construction) - Contractor

Medium Risk Outreach (1 intersection: Bayaud/Utica – half block face of residential curbside impact)
Notify Council this project is coming – CD, as soon as you are able
Flyer adjacent property owners and summarize exact impacts (as soon as construction procurement begins) – Ped Crossing Program to draft flyers, possible assistance from CDs
Typical construction flyering (1 – 2 weeks prior to construction) - Contractor

High Risk Outreach (2 intersections: Lincoln/11th - 2 block faces (total) of downtown adjacent curbside impacts; MLK/Birch – closure of median access across MLK)
Brief Council on these projects – CD, as soon as you are able
Notify local RNOs/stakeholders/BID of upcoming project (as soon as construction procurement begins) – CD
Flyer adjacent property owners and summarize exact impacts (as soon as construction procurement begins) – Ped Crossing Program to draft flyers, possible assistance from CDs
Flyer adjacent property owners and summarize exact impacts (1+ month prior to construction) – Contractors
Typical construction flyering (1 – 2 weeks prior to construction) - Contractor

High Risk Outreach/Steele St Safety Improvements (2 intersections + NB improvements: Steele/Alameda + Steele/Exposition) – start right away
Activity        Purpose Responsible Party
Notify Councilwoman Sawyer      April   Molly
Meet with Polo Club     Review design, describe what changes are coming Kayla
Meet with 3200 Cherry Creek S Dr        Review design, describe what changes are coming Kayla
Provide email update & newsletter content to CW Sawyer  Remind her of changes coming, ask her to celebrate improvements in her newsletter       Community Design (Molly)
Send email to project list      Summarize improvements and construction timing  Kayla
Typical construction flyering   Alert residents/workers to upcoming construction        Contractor


Construction procurement – beginning of summer, May
Construction – Fall

From: Jason Gallardo <jason@mgpublicaffairs.com<mailto:jason@mgpublicaffairs.com>>
Sent: Wednesday, May 14, 2025 9:44 AM
To: Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org<mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>>

Subject: [EXTERNAL] Re: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
Hi Brittany,



Thanks for reaching out. Yes, I agree that it would be helpful to get everyone together—including the concerned neighbors—for a productive discussion about the Alameda Lane Reduction project.



We’d prefer an in-person meeting that includes a walkthrough of the area during peak traffic hours so we can see the impacts firsthand. This will give us all a better understanding of the issues being raised.



Some of the primary concerns we’ve heard include:

      •     Turn lane backups and overflow into through lanes, causing significant traffic delays

      •     Increased neighborhood cut-through traffic, raising safety concerns due to speeding and reckless driving

      •     Higher vehicle volumes on Virginia, which isn’t designed to support that level of traffic, possibly requiring more DOTI involvement



Please let me know your availability and any proposed dates/times that work on your end, and we can coordinate from there.



Best,





Jason A. Gallardo J.D.

Partner | MG Public Affairs

www.mgpublicaffairs.com<https:/www.mgpublicaffairs.com>

M: 970-366-1128

Brittany

b:
Hi Tykus and Brittany-

I just wanted to make sure everyone had the latest and greatest for the Alameda Lane Reduction project, as I have been out of town and Greg has taken the lead in securing an in person meeting with Jason for this Thursday at 3pm. Myself, Greg and Brett plan to attend. Given the political nature of the meeting, I was not sure if Tykus, you also wanted to attend as well?

Subject: [EXTERNAL] Re: Alameda Lane Reduction Project - Concerned Stakeholders Meeting
Hi Brittany,

Thanks for reaching out. Yes, I agree that it would be helpful to get everyone together—including the concerned neighbors—for a productive discussion about the Alameda Lane Reduction project.

We’d prefer an in-person meeting that includes a walkthrough of the area during peak traffic hours so we can see the impacts firsthand. This will give us all a better understanding of the issues being raised.

Some of the primary concerns we’ve heard include:
      •     Turn lane backups and overflow into through lanes, causing significant traffic delays
      •     Increased neighborhood cut-through traffic, raising safety concerns due to speeding and reckless driving
      •     Higher vehicle volumes on Virginia, which isn’t designed to support that level of traffic, possibly requiring more DOTI involvement

Please let me know your availability and any proposed dates/times that work on your end, and we can coordinate from there.

Best,


Jason A. Gallardo J.D.
Partner | MG Public Affairs

Hi Karen,

Yes. I have a few updates that were missed.
   *   In our Safe and Slow Streets presentation, we indicated that 29th Ave and Williams St were “Complete” as I understood this to be substantially complete. The website now says, “Mostly complete with final items to be installed by end of summer 2025.” Any issues with just saying “Complete?” Copying @Weis, Gavin<mailto:Gavin.Weis@denvergov.org> for insight.
   *   The 2024 bike paving project details all reference “DOTI is moving forward with a design…” Since these were already built, I think we need to change the tense to be “DOTI constructed a bikeway on X street that included the following elements:…”
   *   7th Ave: Mariposa Street to Delaware Street Project Status should match the timeline for the other NTMP projects (“Installation planned for fall 2025”). And it needs a “Project Description: This neighborhood bikeway will include pedestrian safety improvements such as curb extensions to shorten crossing distances as well as traffic calming elements that slow down vehicle speeds such as speed cushions.”
   *   W Kentucky Ave needs a “Project Description: This project includes adding bikeway protection elements along the Kentucky bike lane to prevent vehicle encroachment.”
   *   W Ohio Ave needs a “Project Description: This project includes adding a counterflow buffered bike lane to safely facilitate bike travel in the WB direction around Huston Lake Park. It will also include pedestrian safety improvements such as curb extensions at intersections and additional pedestrian crossing markings.”
   *   We should add the Knox Ct Neighborhood Bikeway upgrades from Kentucky to Alameda which are being carried forward through the Fall 2025 NTMP project. Details:
        *   Neighborhood: Westwood
        *   Project Status: Installation planned for fall 2025
        *   Project Description: This neighborhood bikeway upgrade will include pedestrian safety improvements such as pedestrian striping and signage, traffic calming that will slow down vehicle speeds, and wayfinding signage to bring the neighborhood bikeway up the DOTI’s latest bikeway standards.
        *   Program: Neighborhood Transportation Management Program (NTMP)
   *   Market Street Status should show as Complete
   *   GVR project status needs to be updated. @Pogue, Eric<mailto:Eric.Pogue@denvergov.org> – Can you indicate when construction is slated to be substantially complete? Also, this was not an NTMP project, it is a bond project
   *   Uvalda is all complete I believe. @Vigil, Rachael<mailto:Rachael.Vigil@denvergov.org> can you confirm? RACHEL OUT – Just hold on this.

Thanks all!

b:
Hi Karen,

Yes. I have a few updates that were missed.
   *   In our Safe and Slow Streets presentation, we indicated that 29th Ave and Williams St were “Complete” as I understood this to be substantially complete. The website now says, “Mostly complete with final items to be installed by end of summer 2025.” Any issues with just saying “Complete?” Copying @Weis, Gavin<mailto:Gavin.Weis@denvergov.org> for insight.
   *   The 2024 bike paving project details all reference “DOTI is moving forward with a design…” Since these were already built, I think we need to change the tense to be “DOTI constructed a bikeway on X street that included the following elements:…”
   *   7th Ave: Mariposa Street to Delaware Street Project Status should match the timeline for the other NTMP projects (“Installation planned for fall 2025”). And it needs a “Project Description: This neighborhood bikeway will include pedestrian safety improvements such as curb extensions to shorten crossing distances as well as traffic calming elements that slow down vehicle speeds such as speed cushions.”
   *   W Kentucky Ave needs a “Project Description: This project includes adding bikeway protection elements along the Kentucky bike lane to prevent vehicle encroachment.”
   *   W Ohio Ave needs a “Project Description: This project includes adding a counterflow buffered bike lane to safely facilitate bike travel in the WB direction around Huston Lake Park. It will also include pedestrian safety improvements such as curb extensions at intersections and additional pedestrian crossing markings.”
   *   We should add the Knox Ct Neighborhood Bikeway upgrades from Kentucky to Alameda which are being carried forward through the Fall 2025 NTMP project. Details:
        *   Neighborhood: Westwood
        *   Project Status: Installation planned for fall 2025
        *   Project Description: This neighborhood bikeway upgrade will include pedestrian safety improvements such as pedestrian striping and signage, traffic calming that will slow down vehicle speeds, and wayfinding signage to bring the neighborhood bikeway up the DOTI’s latest bikeway standards.
        *   Program: Neighborhood Transportation Management Program (NTMP)
   *   Market Street Status should show as Complete
   *   GVR project status needs to be updated. @Pogue, Eric<mailto:Eric.Pogue@denvergov.org> – Can you indicate when construction is slated to be substantially complete? Also, this was not an NTMP project. Program would be Bond I believe?
   *   Uvalda is all complete I believe. @Vigil, Rachael<mailto:Rachael.Vigil@denvergov.org> can you confirm?

Thanks all!

Brett Boncore, PE| Engineering Supervisor
City & County of Denver
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Design
Phone: 720.865.3214
brett.boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:brett.boncore@denvergov.org>

And it needs a “Project Description: This neighborhood bikeway will include pedestrian safety improvements such as curb extensions to shorten crossing distances as well as traffic calming elements that slow down vehicle speeds such as speed cushions.”
   *   W Kentucky Ave needs a “Project Description: This project includes adding bikeway protection elements along the Kentucky bike lane to prevent vehicle encroachment.”
   *   W Ohio Ave needs a “Project Description: This project includes adding a counterflow buffered bike lane to safely facilitate bike travel in the WB direction around Huston Lake Park. It will also include pedestrian safety improvements such as curb extensions at intersections and additional pedestrian crossing markings.”

cc: "Holloway; Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager" <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; "Lanphier; Molly - DOTI
b:
Hi Ms. Kolesar,

I’m reaching out on behalf of Tykus Holloway, as one of the Community Designers for the council districts where the Alameda Lane Repurposing project is located.

Thank you very much for your note and the thoughtful insight you've shared regarding the proposed Alameda Lane Repurposing project. We sincerely appreciate you taking the time to share your perspective both as a resident and a local business owner in the Wash Park neighborhood.

We understand that any changes to a corridor like Alameda may change travel patterns—especially to those who rely on it daily. Your concerns about traffic circulation, neighborhood safety, business access, and overall connectivity are important to us as we continue evaluating how best to improve safety and mobility in this area.

As part of our process, we are currently reviewing all the comments we've received and will provide an update in the coming months. We recognize that projects like this require careful consideration of the trade-offs and potential unintended impacts, and we are committed to ensuring that those are part of our ongoing assessment.

Again, thank you for sharing your perspective. We look forward to continuing the discussion and working together toward a solution that prioritizes safety while supporting the daily needs of residents, businesses, and all who use the corridor.

Regards,
Greg

Greg Cieciek, PLA | Community Designer

From: Kimberly Kolesar <kim@psdenver.com>
Sent: Sunday, June 8, 2025 10:17 PM
To: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Concerns about DOTI's "Alameda Lane Repurposing" project
Dear Mr. Holloway,

I am part of a community of neighbors who are very concerned with DOTI's upcoming planned "Alameda Lane Repurposing" project. I live on Marion Pkwy, just north of Alameda (between Cedar and Alameda) and use this and the surrounding streets every single day to get to and from my home. I also have a busy small business in Wash Park (at Kentucky and Pearl) that I have operated for 11 years and myself and clients rely heavily on Alameda and the streets surrounding to get to and from my business.

Alemeda is one of the major roads Wash Park residents and visitors rely on to get to their homes, the park, businesses and schools in the community. To reduce the capacity of this street to two lanes will significantly impact traffic in the neighborhood. Not only will Alameda itself get overcrowded, but I am specifically concerned about the impact and crowding that will ultimately spill over onto Downing (which is already extremely backed-up in Wash Park during rush hour), access to University and the ability to get to Cherry Creek North (the only other major access from Wash Park is Speer, which itself is overcrowded) and Marion Pkwy (which is a major host to bicyclists, kids at Steele Elementary School and access to the extremely busy Washington Park). The last thing this busy neighborhood needs is less room on this major thoroughfare and more traffic. It will force people to divert into the neighborhood streets, endangering pedestrians with speeding cars trying to "cut-through" to get to their destinations faster. I already am a witness first-hand the danger that cars traveling on these "side streets" pose to kids, bikers, scooters and runners that are headed in and out of the park, specifically on Marion Pkwy and Virgina at the north entrance into the park itself. I also personally find it nearly impossible to currently turn west out of my alley and onto Alameda in the mornings due to an already overcrowded street with the current configuration. If and when it goes to two lanes, I and my neighbors on the north side of Alameda might truly not be able to access Alameda out of our alley at all during rush hour due to a complete back-up of cars.

As a resident and business owner in Wash Park, I send this email with deep concern about the impact of this project. Driving Alameda daily, I know the impact fewer lanes will cause. Wash Park residents use Alemeda to access their home streets, and that alone (someone waiting to turn off onto their street) will cause a HUGE backup consistently. Plus, I do wonder how the myriad of businesses on Alameda feel about the fewer lanes, thus, less people and less access of clients to their businesses. I hope that DOTI will consider these negative impacts and choose to pause this project to adequately assess its reasoning and to also reach out and engage more input from the community it will impact first-hand.

Thanks for taking the time to read and consider,

Kimberly Kolesar
kim@psdenver.com<mailto:kim@psdenver.com>
925-200-6260

Kimberly Kolesar
OWNER | THE PILATES STUDIO
Wash Park-Highlands
Nationally Certified Pilates Teacher, NCPT

From: Cody Belzley <codybelzley@gmail.com>
Sent: Wednesday, June 11, 2025 10:19 PM
To: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>; Williams, Nicholas - DOTI Deputy Manager Internal & External Affairs <Nicholas.Williams@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Concerns about Proposed Changes to East Alameda Ave
Ms. Ford, Mr. Holloway and Mr. Williams,
I am a resident and homeowner in Central / East Denver (80220) and am reaching out to express concerns about proposed changes to East Alameda Avenue, which I read about in the Denver Gazette a few weeks ago: https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html<https:/denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html>

I count on East Alameda as a primary east / west thoroughfare and am concerned that the proposed changes will reduce capacity and result in increased congestion, and related safety concerns. I worry, particularly, for local residents who will see increased traffic on side roads that are not built to handle that volume.

I believe you all have good intentions but worry that there will be unintended consequences from these changes. I urge you to slow down this process, do further engagement with impacted Denverites and carefully consider the risks of the proposed changes.

Thank you for your consideration.
Cody Belzley
Denver, CO 80220
303 913 1453

From: Amy Guttmann <amymguttmann@gmail.com>
Sent: Thursday, June 12, 2025 9:58 AM
To: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>; Williams, Nicholas - DOTI Deputy Manager Internal & External Affairs <Nicholas.Williams@denvergov.org>; Bailey Burkhardt <bailey.manning@scienceandtech.org>; kim.martin@scienceandtech.org; webster.johnson@scienceandtech.org; elin.curry@scienceandtech.org; Casias, Angela C. - MO Executive Officer <Angela.Casias@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Heads Up DSST Team...more traffic headed your way!
Dear DOTI,
I recently read an article in the Denver Gazette about the East Alameda Lane Repurposing project that left me wondering how cutting the number of lanes on Alameda could possibly be a good idea.
https://denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html<https:/denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html>

As a DPS parent who takes my kids to school in that area every morning (and thus drives Alameda often) I am concerned about the ramifications to what is already a busy street. Our school (DSST Cedar) already struggles with traffic congestion at both drop off and pick up. And getting in and out of the neighborhood already requires that I build in quite a bit of extra travel time. There are actually mornings where I sit on Alameda waiting for the light at University to cycle through for upwards of 10-12 minutes. I can't see how your plan to reduce lanes will improve traffic. I'm also concerned that this will make commuters use the side streets – which I assume is less efficient, less safe and not a great solution for the people who live/work/go to school in the neighborhood.

I've added the leadership at DSST to make sure they are aware of your plans. Traffic is already an issue for our little community...and I can't begin to see how this will help.

I hope you'll reconsider.

Amy Guttmann

From: Illana Poley <illanapoley@gmail.com>
Sent: Friday, June 13, 2025 4:36 PM
To: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Proposed changes to Alameda
To Whom It May Concern:

I'm writing to express a few concerns about changes to Alameda Avenue that recently have been proposed.

My primary concern is with the idea of repurposing traffic lines along Alameda, turning four lanes of traffic into just two, as referenced in a recent article from the Denver Gazette<https:/denvergazette.com/news/alameda-road-diet-denver-traffic-lanes/article_61ff249d-fe60-44e5-b188-20e6d29eee5c.html>.

Turning travel lanes into turn lanes will not reduce the need for vehicle traffic through this corridor, but it will reduce overall capacity. The result? Increased traffic congestion. This will create backups into the proposed new turn lanes and remaining travel lanes, especially during high-traffic times of day.

I'm also concerned about ripple effects. While drivers may avoid Alameda, it is likely that they will continue to travel by vehicle through the neighborhood, but use residential streets instead, especially Virginia Avenue, which is not equipped to handle increased traffic. New safety issues will be created by the attempt to address safety issues along Alameda.

Furthermore, I have not seen compelling safety data to justify this redesign. Instead, it seems like an expensive project based on theoretical benefits rather than real-world conditions or a strong desire from the community.

I'm troubled by the lack of data to justify this change and by the lack of community engagement to create a thoughtful approach to addressing legitimate community concerns.

I strongly urge DOTI to slow down this project and engage the neighborhood in a broader, more productive dialogue to address community level traffic and safety concerns.

Sincerely,

Illana Poley

Name    Lisa Diedrich

Select a Topic  Other: Alameda reduction
Comments        Please do not reduce the lanes on Alameda Boulevard - it is one of the only thoroughfares through the central part of Denver -- we do drive cars, many of us, I know that the bike lobby is strong but please listen to working class people who need to drive to their jobs and who don't want a massive traffic jam clogging up Alameda. If you reduce the lanes you are going backwards - the traffic will remain but it is going to be at a standstill during busy times. How does this help? We don't want a reduced lane with white poles - enough already, stop meddling, the street is fine as it is. Please stop the Alameda street reduction. And another thing, can you actually clean the streets that currently have all your little white plastic poles? Leaves and trash pile up there and nobody cleans it, ever, only the bike lane on Marion gets cleaned because, you know, the bike lobby is strong. Think of others in this city besides the privileged bicyclists. Thank you.

Hi Karen,

Yes. I have a few updates that were missed.
   *   In our Safe and Slow Streets presentation, we indicated that 29th Ave and Williams St were “Complete” as I understood this to be substantially complete. Thanks all!

Subject: RE: Alameda Lane Repurposing

Hi all,

If you think you can obligate/spend all the bond money once the construction contract goes through, then I’m fine to sign the design change order as-is, I just wanted to make sure that we spend bond dollars first if possible to preserve the more flexible CIP funds for other purposes in case there is leftover after the project is complete.

My two related questions were a) is this PRJ budget sufficient to complete the project (I’m interpreting Dallas’s email below that it only covers design & construction administration, not the construction itself) and b) when should I expect to see the RQ come through for construction?



Let me know, thanks!
Erica

Hi Councilwoman Alvidrez and Mark,



We received a request from Jason Gallardo, who is now a lobbyist, to meet with some neighbors who have concerns regarding the Alameda Lane Repurposing project. We have scheduled an in-person meeting for this Thursday at 3pm. We are unsure of all the neighbors Jason is representing but wanted to give you a heads up.



We are planning to meet along Alameda at Franklin St to listen to their concerns and walk some of the corridor.



Please let me know if you or someone from the office would like / are able to attend. I will also be providing an update after the meeting.



Thank you-

Greg

From: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>
Sent: Tuesday, July 1, 2025 11:53 AM
To: Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>; Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>
Cc: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>
Subject: Alameda Jason Follow Up

Hi Brittany and Brett- Please find our response to Jason and the neighbors with the follow up information they had asked for. Let me know if you are ok with us sending. Thanks!
---

Jason-

I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. I know the neighbors had some questions that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:

Didn’t they also ask for the peak hour volumes? We can provide those if needed…

Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?

Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day. I am curious where this 490 number came from (here and in the FAQs)… The latest diversion analysis I found anticipates a total of about ~575 veh/day (or 1-2 vehicles per minute, during the peak hour). Either way, if we keep 490 per day, we should also say “(or ~1 vehicle per minute, during the peak hour).”

Crash Data (1/1/19- 7/31/24)- 144 Crashes over the most recent 5 year period

Although no pedestrian and bike facilities are present on Alameda, the improvements will improve safety where pedestrian and bicycle facilities intersect with the corridor. This will make it easier and safer for all users to reach destinations along Alameda regardless of mode choice.

Many of the questions that were asked, are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know, we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.

Thank you-
Molly

Molly Lanphier, MPH | Community Designer
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Implementation Division
720.913.4549 Phone | 720.619.1018 Cell
Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org>

Hello,
As our Alameda project is nearing kickoff, the neighbors have asked me to reach out again about a problem patio on Alameda at Downing (District 6) at Olivia's.  I've conversed with Gregory about this project and patio, so that's why I am including him on this email.  Please see the attached photos.


The patio at Olivia's is way too big (104").  The current sidewalk from the railing to curb is 56", but that sidewalk amount includes the ROW.  For comparison's sake, I have included a photo of the neighbor's patio at Starbucks, which is 57".  As you can see from the photo, Starbucks has a legal amount of sidewalk available, as well as a 29" ROW.  The ROW at my house on 1101 E Alameda Ave, Denver, CO 80209 is about 1'.


Because of this huge patio, people have to walk single file on the sidewalk to avoid being hit by vehicle mirrors.  My husband has been hit in the shoulder by a mirror on a passing vehicle.


I'd really like to know more about the ROW size on E Alameda and Downing and the required amount of sidewalk.  I have time to meet in person if you like.  From what I read online, the sidewalk is required to be at least 36", but 56" less 12" for a minimum ROW is 34".


This patio is never used for seating by the restaurant.  As a neighborhood, we'd all love to see the patio removed.


Thank you for your time.


Shawnda Maher
303-253-5599
1101 E Alameda Ave

From: Thomas Topero <thomas.topero@gmail.com<mailto:thomas.topero@gmail.com>>
Sent: Monday, June 23, 2025 5:59 PM
To: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org<mailto:Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>>
Cc: Montoya, Mark - CC YA2246 City Council Aide Senior <Mark.Montoya@denvergov.org<mailto:Mark.Montoya@denvergov.org>>; Jonathan Edwards <s34gull@gmail.com<mailto:s34gull@gmail.com>>
Subject: Re: [EXTERNAL] WWPNA updates
That’s great! It’ll be great to have you back!

We have 5 new board members so this will be a great opportunity to reintroduce yourself & the projects that DOTI is working on in & around the neighborhood.

Of course the Alameda lane reduction & Dakota bikeway update will be useful. The Broadway/Lincoln highway @ I-25 project, especially non-vehicular access across the highways (Broadway & Lincoln) to the RTD station is of interest. Planned pedestrian (& ‪bicyclist?) access has been frustrating to many in the neighborhood with a huge detour planned for non-vehicles across numerous high flow traffic lanes. I know RTD has as much to do with this conundrum, but understanding DOTIs perspective & how it’s making things work for the residents would be appreciated.

The leftover remnant land where Hurricane Drain was as well as the land at the end of Lincoln St is of interest to residents for a public use benefit. It’s unclear who controls the land & what DOTIs perspective is on this & similar waste land. Any additional insight would be appreciated.

I recently heard that the pair of bridges of Lincoln & 6th Ave over the Cherry Creek are at end of life & will need to be replaced as repairs are no longer sufficient to maintain standards. They’re at the corner of the neighborhood, but with the unique geometry and cluster of one-ways, it’s of interest to learn how DOTI is thinking about dealing with the regular traffic that would need to be diverted into either Speer or Baker or both when either bridge is replaced.

Clarification on what’s happening with the Alameda underpass is appreciated. Seems this is iterative repairs rather than the replacement project on the boards? Are they related?

Additionally, as the Vibrant Denver GO bond proceeds, having some idea of what DOTI projects are still on the short list for West Washington Park, Speer, Baker & East Washington Park would be appreciated. It’s a fast moving process & difficult to keep up with, so your best attempt is appreciated.

Hopefully the north Broadway “bicycle lane” project up to Colfax area is still in play (update on that regardless of bond status is of interest). The intersection of 7th/speer/Broadway is a challenge, especially for anyone trying to get north without riding on sidewalks and/or avoiding the hill that starts east of Broadway. Even getting some incremental improvements to 10th Ave from Speer so ‪bicyclists can cross over to the bike lanes/ways along Bannock or Cherokee would be a huge safety & accessibility benefit for the multitudes of us coming from south & east of the Cherry Creek, trying to get to the CBD/Upper downtown safely & with ease.

Lastly, with the abandonment buildings bulldozed the past week for 96 Emerson (Morningside Senior housing), I’m interested in understanding how the R.O.W. will be impacted and how residents can understand the impact on their access, parking & minimizing disruption to our lives during the long construction process. I emailed scott.burron@denvergov.org<mailto:scott.burron@denvergov.org> (?) on 6/11/25 about this question but without a reply, so I think I got the email wrong. Could you share it with me or who else is responsible for R.O.W. permitting & coordination for construction so we can improve communication?

I realize that’s a lot however I suspect a few will be rather quick with “ongoing” statuses😉. If you need more than 20 minutes for your presentation, please LMK. We’ll also provide time for Q&A/comments beyond that.

Thank you,
Thomas Topero

From: Thomas Topero <thomas.topero@gmail.com<mailto:thomas.topero@gmail.com>>
Sent: Monday, June 23, 2025 5:59 PM
To: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager II Engineering <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org<mailto:Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>>
Cc: Montoya, Mark - CC YA2246 City Council Aide Senior <Mark.Montoya@denvergov.org<mailto:Mark.Montoya@denvergov.org>>; Jonathan Edwards <s34gull@gmail.com<mailto:s34gull@gmail.com>>
Subject: Re: [EXTERNAL] WWPNA updates
That’s great! It’ll be great to have you back!

We have 5 new board members so this will be a great opportunity to reintroduce yourself & the projects that DOTI is working on in & around the neighborhood.

Of course the Alameda lane reduction & Dakota bikeway update will be useful. The Broadway/Lincoln highway @ I-25 project, especially non-vehicular access across the highways (Broadway & Lincoln) to the RTD station is of interest. Planned pedestrian (& ‪bicyclist?) access has been frustrating to many in the neighborhood with a huge detour planned for non-vehicles across numerous high flow traffic lanes. I know RTD has as much to do with this conundrum, but understanding DOTIs perspective & how it’s making things work for the residents would be appreciated.

The leftover remnant land where Hurricane Drain was as well as the land at the end of Lincoln St is of interest to residents for a public use benefit. It’s unclear who controls the land & what DOTIs perspective is on this & similar waste land. Any additional insight would be appreciated.

I recently heard that the pair of bridges of Lincoln & 6th Ave over the Cherry Creek are at end of life & will need to be replaced as repairs are no longer sufficient to maintain standards. They’re at the corner of the neighborhood, but with the unique geometry and cluster of one-ways, it’s of interest to learn how DOTI is thinking about dealing with the regular traffic that would need to be diverted into either Speer or Baker or both when either bridge is replaced.

Clarification on what’s happening with the Alameda underpass is appreciated. Seems this is iterative repairs rather than the replacement project on the boards? Are they related?

Additionally, as the Vibrant Denver GO bond proceeds, having some idea of what DOTI projects are still on the short list for West Washington Park, Speer, Baker & East Washington Park would be appreciated. It’s a fast moving process & difficult to keep up with, so your best attempt is appreciated.

Hopefully the north Broadway “bicycle lane” project up to Colfax area is still in play (update on that regardless of bond status is of interest). The intersection of 7th/speer/Broadway is a challenge, especially for anyone trying to get north without riding on sidewalks and/or avoiding the hill that starts east of Broadway. Even getting some incremental improvements to 10th Ave from Speer so ‪bicyclists can cross over to the bike lanes/ways along Bannock or Cherokee would be a huge safety & accessibility benefit for the multitudes of us coming from south & east of the Cherry Creek, trying to get to the CBD/Upper downtown safely & with ease.

Lastly, with the abandonment buildings bulldozed the past week for 96 Emerson (Morningside Senior housing), I’m interested in understanding how the R.O.W. will be impacted and how residents can understand the impact on their access, parking & minimizing disruption to our lives during the long construction process. I emailed scott.burron@denvergov.org<mailto:scott.burron@denvergov.org> (?) on 6/11/25 about this question but without a reply, so I think I got the email wrong. Could you share it with me or who else is responsible for R.O.W. permitting & coordination for construction so we can improve communication?

I realize that’s a lot however I suspect a few will be rather quick with “ongoing” statuses😉. If you need more than 20 minutes for your presentation, please LMK. We’ll also provide time for Q&A/comments beyond that.

Thank you,
Thomas Topero

Sent: Tuesday, July 1, 2025 11:53 AM
To: Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>>; Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org<mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>>
Cc: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org<mailto:Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>>
Subject: Alameda Jason Follow Up

Hi Brittany and Brett- Please find our response to Jason and the neighbors with the follow up information they had asked for. Let me know if you are ok with us sending. Thanks!
---

Jason-

I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. I know the neighbors had some questions that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:

Didn’t they also ask for the peak hour volumes? We can provide those if needed…

Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?

Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day. I am curious where this 490 number came from (here and in the FAQs)… The latest diversion analysis I found anticipates a total of about ~575 veh/day (or 1-2 vehicles per minute, during the peak hour). Either way, if we keep 490 per day, we should also say “(or ~1 vehicle per minute, during the peak hour).”

Crash Data (1/1/19- 7/31/24)- 144 Crashes over the most recent 5 year period

Although no pedestrian and bike facilities are present on Alameda, the improvements will improve safety where pedestrian and bicycle facilities intersect with the corridor. This will make it easier and safer for all users to reach destinations along Alameda regardless of mode choice.

Many of the questions that were asked, are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know, we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.

Thank you-
Molly

Hi Brittany and Brett- Please find our response to Jason and the neighbors with the follow up information they had asked for. Let me know if you are ok with us sending. Thanks!
---

Jason-

I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. I know the neighbors had some questions that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:

Didn’t they also ask for the peak hour volumes? We can provide those if needed…

Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?

Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day. I am curious where this 490 number came from (here and in the FAQs)… The latest diversion analysis I found anticipates a total of about ~575 veh/day (or 1-2 vehicles per minute, during the peak hour). Either way, if we keep 490 per day, we should also say “(or ~1 vehicle per minute, during the peak hour).”

Crash Data (1/1/19- 7/31/24)- 144 Crashes over the most recent 5 year period

Although no pedestrian and bike facilities are present on Alameda, the improvements will improve safety where pedestrian and bicycle facilities intersect with the corridor. This will make it easier and safer for all users to reach destinations along Alameda regardless of mode choice.

Many of the questions that were asked, are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know, we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.

Thank you-
Molly

I think we should remove these from the website, as that still shows old information that is no longer relevant. Can you coordinate this with Ken?

Thanks all,


Brett Boncore, PE| Engineering Supervisor
City & County of Denver
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Design
Phone: 720.865.3214
brett.boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:brett.boncore@denvergov.org>

From: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>>
Sent: Tuesday, July 1, 2025 11:53 AM
To: Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>>; Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org<mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>>
Cc: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI CE2294 Project Manager Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org<mailto:Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>>
Subject: Alameda Jason Follow Up

Hi Brittany and Brett- Please find our response to Jason and the neighbors with the follow up information they had asked for. Let me know if you are ok with us sending. Thanks!
---

Jason-

I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. I know the neighbors had some questions that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:

Didn’t they also ask for the peak hour volumes? We can provide those if needed…

Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?

Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day. I am curious where this 490 number came from (here and in the FAQs)… The latest diversion analysis I found anticipates a total of about ~575 veh/day (or 1-2 vehicles per minute, during the peak hour). Either way, if we keep 490 per day, we should also say “(or ~1 vehicle per minute, during the peak hour).”

Crash Data (1/1/19- 7/31/24)- 144 Crashes over the most recent 5 year period

Although no pedestrian and bike facilities are present on Alameda, the improvements will improve safety where pedestrian and bicycle facilities intersect with the corridor. This will make it easier and safer for all users to reach destinations along Alameda regardless of mode choice.

Many of the questions that were asked, are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know, we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.

Thank you-
Molly

f: 2025-07-11_16-17-18 - Alameda_ Email Follow Up to Jason and Neighbors.pdf
s: Alameda: Email Follow Up to Jason and Neighbors
to: "Holloway; Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager" <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; "Kuhn; Nancy - DOTI
cc: "Good; Karen A. - DOTI CE3476 Project Manager Engineering Supervisor"
b:
Hi All-

Please find below a draft of the email to be sent as a follow up to Jason concerning Alameda Lane Repurposing. Please let me know if you have any issues. Tykus, let me know if you also want me to share with Amy.

Once Brett is back in town, I am hoping to see what we can do from a design perspective and we can then review next steps with Amy.

Thanks and have a great weekend!
-----------
Message to be sent to Jason, which will be shared with Jill Anschutz, Kimberly Kolesar, Cody, Belzley, Amy Guttmann, Illana Poley and Lisa Diedrich

I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. There were also questions and requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:

Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?

Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute.

Crash Data

We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.

Peak Hour Volumes

Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM. In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.

I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts.

Speed Limit

The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation of the speed limit change.

Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know that we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.

Thank you-
Molly


Molly Lanphier, MPH | Community Designer
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Implementation Division
720.913.4549 Phone | 720.619.1018 Cell
Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org>

Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Subject: For Your Review: Alameda Lane Repurposing Email to Neighbors

Hi Amy- We have a meeting tomorrow at 2 pm to discuss design options for Alameda, but I wanted to share the below email we are hoping to send to Jason Gallardo and neighbors, as a follow up to our in person meeting a couple weeks back. They had asked for some specific follow up. Let me know if you are ok with me sending or if you have any questions or concerns.

Thank you!
------
Message to be sent to Jason, which will be shared with Jill Anschutz, Kimberly Kolesar, Cody, Belzley, Amy Guttmann, Illana Poley and Lisa Diedrich

I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. We are currently briefing DOTI leadership and hope to share more about our next steps by the end of July. There were also questions and requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:

Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?

Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute.

Crash Data

We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.

Peak Hour Volumes

Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM. In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.

I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts.

Speed Limit

The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation of the speed limit change.

Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know that we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.

Thank you-
Molly

Molly Lanphier, MPH | Community Designer
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Implementation Division
720.913.4549 Phone | 720.619.1018 Cell
Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org>

f: 2025-07-16_21-35-17 - _EXTERNAL_ Public Comment at Denver City Council Public Comm.pdf
s: Public Comment at Denver City Council Public Comment
to: mayorsoffice@denvergov.org; alan.salazar@denvergov.org; "Lanphier; Molly - DOTI CE2294 Project
b:
These remarks were made before the Denver City Council on July 14, 2025.


The residents of WIndsor Gardens wish that the Mayor would respond to our many requests for him to come and talk to our residents about the Dayton Street Reconfiguration, and listen to our concerns about how the street is being made less safe by the addition of parking on the East side of Dayton and the removal of the center turn lane.


On April 22, close to 300 members of the Windsor Gardens Community gathered to listen to a presentation regarding the Denver Office of Transportation and Infrastructure's proposed changes to South Dayton Street from Alameda to Mississippi. The residents were horrified to hear that DoTI decided to remove the Center Turn Lane and to allow parking on both the East and West side of Dayton. Over an hour of Windsor Gardens residents concerns were disregarded by DoTI's Community Designer who presented the plan at Windsor Gardens.


By removing the "Safety Lane" or Center Turn Lane, DoTI has once again shown its preference for parking over people, parking over public safety.


Changing Dayton Street's configuration from 3 lanes to 2 lanes will cause an increase in both head on and rear end collisions, as drivers are forced to cross the center line in an attempt to go around trash trucks which use Dayton to collect large dumpsters from Windsor Gardens' buildings, drivers attempting to parallel park, people darting out to cross the street between cars and drivers making any number of non marked left turns outside of the proposed marked short turn lanes at East Center Avenue and Alton Street.


DoTI also has included a "pedestrian rescue island" that may obstruct and limit the ability of Windsor Gardens residents to access one of our underground garages, which will impact over 300 residents.


District 5 Councilwoman Amanda Sawyer stated at the July 9, 2025 General Obligation Bond Working Group, that, and I quote "I don't trust DoTI, I do not trust the Department of Transportation and Infrastructure to get this done right."


Windsor Garden's residents feel the exact same way. We no longer trust DoTI to reconfigure Dayton Street so that it is safe, and we certainly feel that DoTI would rather risk the safety of Windsor Gardens residents for additional parking spaces along Dayton than to provide a safe street for all residents of the Windsor Community.


Also on July 9th, Councilwoman Sawyer stated that "I am out of patience with this process, I am incredibly frustrated and incredibly angry." The residents of Windsor Gardens feel exactly the same, especially as Windsor Gardens was excluded from the initial Memorandum regarding the Dayton Street project.


The only individuals included in the initial Memorandum were the Park Forest HOA, DoTI, and Councilwoman Sawyer, hiring Fehr & Peers to study traffic concerns on Dayton Street. Leaving out the voices of 3500 residents who use Dayton as 2 of 3 means of egress out of Windsor Gardens, was a major mistake and unfortunately, the residents of Windsor Gardens are being put at risk by DoTI's dangerous and negligent plan.


We would ask the council to consider suspending or rescinding the South Dayton paving project until DoTI creates a plan that does not put drivers from Windsor Gardens into dangerous situations as they exit and enter the property.


Windsor Gardens residents may be over 55, but that does not mean we are expendable!


Christine Shock
9625 East Center Avenue #8D
Denver, CO 80247
christine.shock@gmail.com<mailto:christine.shock@gmail.com>



__________________________________________


I am here today to discuss the proposed changes to the Dayton Street Repaving Project.  This project was the result of the parking issues on Dayton Street.   DoTi proposed to eliminate the middle lane and allow parking on both sides of Dayton.


You need that middle lane to allow the 3,301 Residents of Windsor Gardens, a 55+ Community, to make a left turn when driving North on Dayton Street to obtain access to their residence.  DoTI informed us that they are all about Safety.  By removing that middle lane, you are removing our safety and you will see accidents increase and someone will be rear ended on a weekly basis.


Has anyone noticed that Alameda Ave. is now 35 mph, but most drivers still go 40+ to 45 the old speed limit.  Speeds on Dayton are


Are frequently the same as those (40+ to 45 mph) that occur on Alameda.  By narrowing the road and removing the turn lane DoTI feels they are creating a safer driving situation???


The changes proposed by DoTI will lead to a loss of life.  Why are we waiting for this to occur before a real, usable, solution is reached.  Currently, we do not have that.  I also do not feel that DoTI came to work with Windsor Gardens residents.  One meeting was at George Washington H.S. during a snow storm, and Windsor Gardens Residents had to continuously ask at that meeting to meet with DoTI.  The first time DoTI did come to speak with us, they were not there to work with Windsor Gardens, but to dictate their final decision.


We, the residents, do not have a change that will work for residents who live on either side of Dayton Street.


Thank You,


Janis Mayer
615 S. Alton Way 4C
Denver, CO



___________________________



I am Bob Shock. I live in Windsor Gardens, a 55 plus community in east Denver.


We have 3500 residents. We are widely known for turning out in record numbers in every election. Our community is bordered on the east side by Dayton Street which currently has two way traffic, a central turn lane and parking on the west side only. Many of us are opposed to the city plans to reconfigure Dayton street which will remove the central turn lane and put parking on both sides of the street.


If this change is made, traffic will be held up by cars getting into and out of their parking spaces with no extra space for oncoming traffic to move out of the way. This will certainly cause more accidents, including head on accidents where cars in opposing lanes are swerving towards each other.


This change will also make it nearly impossible for all Dayton street traffic to get around the trash trucks which have to stop along Dayton three times a week to empty our community's dumpsters.


Finally, about 300 of our residents rely on the existing turn lane to safely get into our underground parking garage. This will be much more dangerous when that turn lane is removed.


The city is not currently even enforcing existing no parking zones with parking only on the east side of the street. This makes it much more dangerous to exit our apartment community onto Dayton. So how can we be sure that they will enforce any new no parking zones in the future?


Robert Shock
9625 East Center Avenue #8D
Denver, CO 80247
shockbob@gmail.com<mailto:shockbob@gmail.com>
x:

f: 2025-07-19_17-20-46 - _EXTERNAL_ Support for East Alameda Lane Repurposing project.pdf
s: Support for East Alameda Lane Repurposing project
b:
All,


I am a resident of Denver, living in the Wash park neighborhood.  I have three young kids (ages 6, 3, 3), a dog that we walk multiple times a day, and I am both a bicycle and auto commuter to my office in Cherry Creek.   We bike our kids to school 95% of the days to Steele elementary and St. John’s and have to cross Alameda Street to do that.   We walk our dog morning and night and my husband and I bike commute when weather allows us to and we drive to the office as well.   I wanted to paint this context as someone who is out in the streets and impacted by alameda through multiple modes (walk, bike, drive both individually and with dependents)


I am an advocate of the safety improvements proposed on Alameda street and thank you for your work on this project.


I recently got an email from our neighbors sharing this article for ActForAlameda.com which, in my opinion, is spreading misinformation to neighbors, and based on a classic nimby fear based reaction.   I am trying to counter their efforts but am a busy working mom.


I wanted to reach out and let you know many neighbors DO support this.  We want alameda safer.  We want less lanes.  We want more crosswalks.  We want a road diet and narrowing lanes which will slow traffic.  We want more friction so people pay attention when driving vs treating alameda as a highway.


But many of the neighbors who support it the most (working parents with kids) are so busy they never have the time to get involved in these issues.   So you typically only hear from the people who don’t support something.


Please know neighbors do support safe streets and support what you are doing.  Road diets are a good thing!


I don’t have much time but if I can help I just wanted you to know.


Thank you for your efforts in this.


Liz Chester
245 South Williams Street
Denver, CO 80209
503.706.3697
elizabeth.dilorenzo@gmail.com<mailto:elizabeth.dilorenzo@gmail.com>

f: 2025-07-21_11-29-58 - _EXTERNAL_ Re_ Follow Up from Alameda Lane Repurposing.pdf
s: Re: Follow Up from Alameda Lane Repurposing
to: "Lanphier; Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior" <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>
cc: "Holloway; Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager" <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; "Price; Brittany C. -
b:
Thank you, Molly,


I don’t see the attached file. Does it show where the counts come from, within the project being proposed or alameda in general. Is this section of alameda considered to be a part of the high injury network? Thanks for any info you can share.


Best,


Jason A. Gallardo J.D.
Partner | MG Public Affairs
www.mgpublicaffairs.com<https://www.mgpublicaffairs.com>

M: 970-366-1128
  ________________________________
From: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>
Sent: Wednesday, July 16, 2025 4:56:28 PM
To: Jason Gallardo <jason@mgpublicaffairs.com>
Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>
Subject: Follow Up from Alameda Lane Repurposing

Hi Jason-

I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. We are currently briefing DOTI leadership and hope to share more about our next steps by the end of July. There were also questions and requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:

Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?

Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute.

Crash Data

We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.

Peak Hour Volumes

Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM. In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.

I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts.

Speed Limit

The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation of the speed limit change.

Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know that we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.

Thank you-
Molly

Molly Lanphier, MPH | Community Designer
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Implementation Division
720.913.4549 Phone | 720.619.1018 Cell
Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org>

From: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>>
Sent: Wednesday, July 16, 2025 4:56:28 PM
To: Jason Gallardo <jason@mgpublicaffairs.com<mailto:jason@mgpublicaffairs.com>>
Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org<mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>>; Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org<mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>>; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org<mailto:Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>>
Subject: Follow Up from Alameda Lane Repurposing


Hi Jason-



I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. We are currently briefing DOTI leadership and hope to share more about our next steps by the end of July. There were also questions and requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:



Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?



Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute.



Crash Data



We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.



Peak Hour Volumes



Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM. In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.



I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts.



Speed Limit



The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation of the speed limit change.



Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know that we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.



Thank you-

Molly

to: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure
b:
Thanks, Amy. Also, wanted to address Mark Samuelson’s second question about the project a dozen years ago. We have a blurb on the FAQs regarding that:

Am I having déjà vu? Wasn't a lane reduction attempted on Alameda several years ago?
DOTI installed a lane reduction on Alameda back in 2008-2010 timeframe. In the eastbound direction, there was a lane drop at Downing necessitating a lane merge before Downing which resulted in stacking of vehicles on the eastbound approach to Downing Street with one lane fully queued and the other lane free flowing. During this time the Alameda average daily traffic was over 20,000. It is now much lower, so Alameda is a better candidate for a lane reduction.

Let me know if you need any more talking points/ help to get back to Mark, Nancy!

Thanks!
Molly

Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org<mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>>; Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director <Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org<mailto:Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org>>; Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org<mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org>>; Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>>; Good, Karen A. - DOTI CE3476 Project Manager Engineering Supervisor <Karen.Good@denvergov.org<mailto:Karen.Good@denvergov.org>>; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org<mailto:Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>>
Subject: For Your Review: Alameda Lane Repurposing Email to Neighbors

Hi Amy- We have a meeting tomorrow at 2 pm to discuss design options for Alameda, but I wanted to share the below email we are hoping to send to Jason Gallardo and neighbors, as a follow up to our in person meeting a couple weeks back. They had asked for some specific follow up. Let me know if you are ok with me sending or if you have any questions or concerns.

Thank you!
------
Message to be sent to Jason, which will be shared with Jill Anschutz, Kimberly Kolesar, Cody, Belzley, Amy Guttmann, Illana Poley and Lisa Diedrich

I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. We are currently briefing DOTI leadership and hope to share more about our next steps by the end of July. There were also questions and requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:

Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?

Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute.

Crash Data

We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.

Peak Hour Volumes

Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM. In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.

I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts.

Speed Limit

The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation of the speed limit change.

Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know that we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.

Thank you-
Molly

to: Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>
b:
Hi Tykus-

Including Brett here, as I had to confer with him, to be sure I was providing the most accurate information.

In 2024 the counts show the AADT of Alameda, west of Downing, is around 14,800 vpd (2024).

Here are the latest AADTs of the parallel E/W roads, where we anticipate some diversion loading:

  *   Virginia (between Logan and Downing) = 4,200 vpd
  *   1st Avenue (between Logan and Downing) = ~2,600 vpd
  *   1st Avenue/Speer (between Downing and University) = ~50,000 vpd

I have also attached the Diversion diagram, which shows the additional ADTs impacts’.

Let us know if you need anything else!

Thank you!
Molly

Sent: Monday, July 21, 2025 4:41 PM
To: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>
Cc: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>; Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director <Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org>
Subject: Re: Alameda project follow-up...

Thanks Molly for providing more info. Do we have traffic counts along any of the adjacent E/W roads (such as Virginia)? Also, what is the current ADT? Thx!


On Jul 21, 2025, at 4:16 PM, Lanphier, Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>> wrote:
﻿
Thanks, Amy. Also, wanted to address Mark Samuelson’s second question about the project a dozen years ago. We have a blurb on the FAQs regarding that:

Am I having déjà vu? Wasn't a lane reduction attempted on Alameda several years ago?
DOTI installed a lane reduction on Alameda back in 2008-2010 timeframe. In the eastbound direction, there was a lane drop at Downing necessitating a lane merge before Downing which resulted in stacking of vehicles on the eastbound approach to Downing Street with one lane fully queued and the other lane free flowing. During this time the Alameda average daily traffic was over 20,000. It is now much lower, so Alameda is a better candidate for a lane reduction.

Let me know if you need any more talking points/ help to get back to Mark, Nancy!

Thanks!
Molly
