Subject: For Your Review: Alameda Lane Repurposing Email to Neighbors

Hi Amy- We have a meeting tomorrow at 2 pm to discuss design options for Alameda, but I wanted to share the below email we are hoping to send to Jason Gallardo and neighbors, as a follow up to our in person meeting a couple weeks back. They had asked for some specific follow up. Let me know if you are ok with me sending or if you have any questions or concerns.

Thank you!
------
Message to be sent to Jason, which will be shared with Jill Anschutz, Kimberly Kolesar, Cody, Belzley, Amy Guttmann, Illana Poley and Lisa Diedrich

I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. We are currently briefing DOTI leadership and hope to share more about our next steps by the end of July. There were also questions and requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:

Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?

Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute.

Crash Data

We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.

Peak Hour Volumes

Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM. In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.

I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts.

Speed Limit

The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation of the speed limit change.

Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know that we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.

Thank you-
Molly

Thanks, Amy. Also, wanted to address Mark Samuelson’s second question about the project a dozen years ago. We have a blurb on the FAQs regarding that:

Am I having déjà vu? Wasn't a lane reduction attempted on Alameda several years ago?
DOTI installed a lane reduction on Alameda back in 2008-2010 timeframe. In the eastbound direction, there was a lane drop at Downing necessitating a lane merge before Downing which resulted in stacking of vehicles on the eastbound approach to Downing Street with one lane fully queued and the other lane free flowing. During this time the Alameda average daily traffic was over 20,000. It is now much lower, so Alameda is a better candidate for a lane reduction.

Let me know if you need any more talking points/ help to get back to Mark, Nancy!

Molly

Subject: For Your Review: Alameda Lane Repurposing Email to Neighbors

Hi Amy- We have a meeting tomorrow at 2 pm to discuss design options for Alameda, but I wanted to share the below email we are hoping to send to Jason Gallardo and neighbors, as a follow up to our in person meeting a couple weeks back. They had asked for some specific follow up. Let me know if you are ok with me sending or if you have any questions or concerns.

Thank you!
------
Message to be sent to Jason, which will be shared with Jill Anschutz, Kimberly Kolesar, Cody, Belzley, Amy Guttmann, Illana Poley and Lisa Diedrich

I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. We are currently briefing DOTI leadership and hope to share more about our next steps by the end of July. There were also questions and requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:

Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?

Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute.

Crash Data

We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor.

Peak Hour Volumes

Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM. In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.

I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts.

Speed Limit

The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation of the speed limit change.

Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>. Please let the neighbors know that we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.

Thank you-
Molly

From: Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director <Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org<mailto:Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org>>
Sent: Wednesday, July 23, 2025 2:21 PM
To: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>>; Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org<mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>>; Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org<mailto:Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>>; Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>>

Team, draft response for review.  Please review for accuracy.  This reporter wants to talk to someone about the need for the project, so anything we can do to make the need clear in this response would be helpful.  My previous response to him is attached.   It looks like, this time, we’d provide the crash data and if we could provide speed data, that would be helpful too.

Mark,

Our plan is to move forward with safety improvements on Alameda Avenue in the summer/fall timeframe, focusing first on making the corridor safe for all users improvements for people walking, biking, and rolling across Alameda Ave on the north/south streets. The work planned includes:
   *   Reduced speed limit to 25 mph
   *   New designated turn lanes at eight intersections (Pennsylvania St., Pearl St., Washington St., Clarkson St., Emerson St., Ogden St., Lafayette St., and Humboldt St.)
   *   Painted pedestrian bulbouts at every intersection between Pearl St. and Franklin St. (11 intersection)
   *   Access management safety enhancements at Grant St, Pennsylvania St, Corona St., and Franklin St.
   *   New striped medians and pedestrian refuge islands at these locations: Washington St. intersection (west side); Emerson St. intersection (east side); Marion St. intersection (east side), Downing, Marion, and Corona street intersections (east and west sides).
   *   Installation of a Rectangular Rapid Flashing Beacon, pedestrian refuge island, and marked crosswalks at Franklin; and new marked crosswalks at Grant St.
   *   New School Zone signage and markings east of Broadway, east of Logan St. and east of Humboldt St.
   *   Additional on-street parking east of Pearl St.
   *   Protected bike lane approaches and green crossing markings at Washington St. and Emerson St.

I don’t have an exact date yet for implementation. We’ve been giving travel patterns a little time to adjust to the Alameda underpass project happening between Bannock and Santa Fe, and have been affording time to consider and address new and lingering community concerns about the plan for the lane repurposing.

Some of the data we’ve collected that speak to the need for the project include:

Crash data
We gathered crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor. Safety improvements for drivers have been recommended in alignment with the crash data (attached).

Traffic Speed and Volume data
The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. 2024 speed data shows that speeding is an issue along this corridor (with 85th %tile speed = 38 mph). Following installation of our safety improvements, the posted speed limit is planned to be 25 miles per hour.

Also, related to predicting diversion to the side streets, we’ve looked at average daily traffic volumes
We’ve collected average daily traffic volumes to predict potential diversion to side streets as a result of the lane repurposing work.  2024 counts showed the average daily traffic volumes of Alameda Ave, west of Downing, to be around 14,800 vehicles per day.
   *   DOTI believes no more than 10% of vehicles total will reroute to side streets throughout the day.
   *   A diversion analysis conducted for Virginia Avenue predicts approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to about 1-2 vehicles per minute.  Current volumes on Virginia between Logan and Downing are approximately 4,200 vehicles a day.

Latest counts of average daily vehicle volumes on other parallel, east/west roads include:
   *   1st Avenue (between Logan and Downing) = ~2,600 vehicles per day
   *   1st Avenue/Speer (between Downing and University) = ~50,000 vehicles per day.

Wanted to mention that many of the questions that have been asked about the project are addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>.

Per your question about a similar, past project, DOTI installed a lane reduction on Alameda back in 2008-2010 timeframe. In the eastbound direction, there was a lane drop at Downing necessitating a lane merge before Downing which resulted in stacking of vehicles on the eastbound approach to Downing Street with one lane fully queued and the other lane free flowing. During this time the Alameda average daily traffic was over 20,000. What would be different now is that the average daily traffic is much lower, so Alameda is a better candidate now for a lane reduction.

Sincerely,
Nancy

  NANCY KUHN| Director
  City & County of Denver
  Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Communications Division
  Phone: 303-513-6046
  nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org<mailto:nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org>

Our plan is to move forward with safety improvements on Alameda Avenue, making the corridor safer for all users. The work planned includes:
         •         Reduced speed limit to 25 mph
         •         New designated turn lanes at eight intersections (Pennsylvania St., Pearl St., Washington St., Clarkson St., Emerson St., Ogden St., Lafayette St., and Humboldt St.)
         •         Painted pedestrian bulb outs at every intersection between Pearl St. and Franklin St. (11 intersections)
         •         Access management safety enhancements at Grant St, Pennsylvania St, Corona St., and Franklin St.
         •         New striped medians and pedestrian refuge islands at these locations: Washington St. intersection (west side); Emerson St. intersection (east side);  Downing, Marion, and Corona street intersections (east and west sides).
         •         Installation of a Rectangular Rapid Flashing Beacon, pedestrian refuge island, and marked crosswalks at Franklin (note: this RRFB is part of separate plans and is likely to be installed first and separately from the rest of the improvements)
         •         New marked crosswalks at Grant St.
         •         New School Zone signage and markings east of Broadway, east of Logan St. and east of Humboldt St.
         •         Additional on-street parking east of Pearl St.
         •         Protected bike lane approaches and green crossing markings at Washington St. and Emerson St.

We don’t have an exact date yet for implementation. We’ve been giving travel patterns a little time to adjust to the Alameda underpass project’s temporary lane closures happening between Bannock and Santa Fe, and have been affording time to consider and address new and lingering community concerns about the plan for the lane repurposing.

Some of the data we’ve collected that speak to the need for the project include:

Crash data
We gathered crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor. Safety improvements for drivers have been recommended in alignment with the crash data (attached).

Traffic Speed and Volume data
The posted speed limit along this segment of Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. 2024 speed data shows that speeding is an issue along this corridor (with 85th percentile speed = 38 mph). Following installation of our safety improvements, the posted speed limit is planned to be 25 miles per hour.

We’ve collected average daily traffic volumes to predict potential diversion to side streets as a result of the lane repurposing work.  2024 counts showed the average daily traffic volumes of Alameda Ave, west of Downing, to be around 14,800 vehicles per day.
   *   DOTI believes no more than 10% of vehicles total will reroute to side streets throughout the day.
   *   A diversion analysis conducted for Virginia Avenue predicts approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to about 1-2 vehicles per minute.

Average daily vehicle volumes on parallel east/west roads include:
   *   Virginia between Logan and Downing = ~ 4,200 vehicles a day
   *   1st Avenue (between Logan and Downing) = ~2,600 vehicles per day
   *   1st Avenue/Speer (between Downing and University) = ~50,000 vehicles per day

Wanted to mention that many of the questions that have been asked about the project are addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>.

Per your question about a similar, past project, DOTI installed a lane reduction on Alameda back in the 2008-2010 timeframe. In the eastbound direction, there was a lane drop at Downing necessitating a lane merge before Downing. This merge resulted in the stacking of vehicles on the eastbound approach to Downing Street with one lane fully queued and the other lane free flowing. At that time, the Alameda average daily traffic was over 20,000. What would be different now is that the average daily traffic is much lower. Alameda is a better candidate now for a lane reduction.

From: Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director <Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org<mailto:Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org>>
Sent: Wednesday, July 23, 2025 2:21 PM
To: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org<mailto:Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>>; Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org<mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>>; Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org<mailto:Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>>; Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org>>
Subject: RE: Alameda project follow-up...

Team, draft response for review.  Please review for accuracy.  This reporter wants to talk to someone about the need for the project, so anything we can do to make the need clear in this response would be helpful.  My previous response to him is attached.   It looks like, this time, we’d provide the crash data and if we could provide speed data, that would be helpful too.

Mark,

Our plan is to move forward with safety improvements on Alameda Avenue in the summer/fall timeframe, focusing first on making the corridor safe for all users improvements for people walking, biking, and rolling across Alameda Ave on the north/south streets. The work planned includes:
         •        Reduced speed limit to 25 mph
         •        New designated turn lanes at eight intersections (Pennsylvania St., Pearl St., Washington St., Clarkson St., Emerson St., Ogden St., Lafayette St., and Humboldt St.)
         •        Painted pedestrian bulbouts at every intersection between Pearl St. and Franklin St. (11 intersection)
         •        Access management safety enhancements at Grant St, Pennsylvania St, Corona St., and Franklin St.
         •        New striped medians and pedestrian refuge islands at these locations: Washington St. intersection (west side); Emerson St. intersection (east side); Marion St. intersection (east side), Downing, Marion, and Corona street intersections (east and west sides).
         •        Installation of a Rectangular Rapid Flashing Beacon, pedestrian refuge island, and marked crosswalks at Franklin; and new marked crosswalks at Grant St.
         •        New School Zone signage and markings east of Broadway, east of Logan St. and east of Humboldt St.
         •        Additional on-street parking east of Pearl St.
         •        Protected bike lane approaches and green crossing markings at Washington St. and Emerson St.

We don’t have an exact date yet for implementation. We’ve been giving travel patterns a little time to adjust to the Alameda underpass project’s temporary lane closures happening between Bannock and Santa Fe, and have been affording time to consider and address new and lingering community concerns about the plan for the lane repurposing.

Some of the data we’ve collected that speak to the need for the project include:

Crash data
We gathered crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor. Safety improvements for drivers have been recommended in alignment with the crash data (attached).

Traffic Speed and Volume data
The posted speed limit along this segment of Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. 2024 speed data shows that speeding is an issue along this corridor (with 85th percentile speed = 38 mph). Following installation of our safety improvements, the posted speed limit is planned to be 25 miles per hour.

Also, related to predicting diversion to the side streets, we’ve looked at average daily traffic volumes
We’ve collected average daily traffic volumes to predict potential diversion to side streets as a result of the lane repurposing work.  2024 counts showed the average daily traffic volumes of Alameda Ave, west of Downing, to be around 14,800 vehicles per day.
         •        DOTI believes no more than 10% of vehicles total will reroute to side streets throughout the day.
         •        A diversion analysis conducted for Virginia Avenue predicts approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to about 1-2 vehicles per minute.  Current volumes on Virginia between Logan and Downing are approximately 4,200 vehicles a day.

Latest counts of average daily vehicle volumes on other parallel, east/west roads include:
   *   1st Avenue (between Logan and Downing) = ~2,600 vehicles per day
   *   1st Avenue/Speer (between Downing and University) = ~50,000 vehicles per day.

Wanted to mention that many of the questions that have been asked about the project are addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>.

Per your question about a similar, past project, DOTI installed a lane reduction on Alameda back in the 2008-2010 timeframe. In the eastbound direction, there was a lane drop at Downing necessitating a lane merge before Downing. This merge resulted in the stacking of vehicles on the eastbound approach to Downing Street with one lane fully queued and the other lane free flowing. At that time, the Alameda average daily traffic was over 20,000. What would be different now is that the average daily traffic is much lower. Alameda is a better candidate now for a lane reduction.

Sincerely,
Nancy

  NANCY KUHN| Director
  City & County of Denver
  Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Communications Division

  nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org<mailto:nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org>

Thanks for sharing, Nancy. I’m not sure where they got the quote about traffic cutting through on Cedar. While there may be very minor cut through on adjacent local streets like Cedar, our diversion analysis showed the 10% diversion being distributed to Virginia and 1st Ave/Speer, as those streets are collectors/arterials that have connectivity to the surrounding area and signals at the major intersections. Maybe this is a misquote?

Thanks again,

f: 2025-07-25_16-15-44 - RE_ D8 CM Lewis SPEED Program Briefing.pdf
s: RE: D8 CM Lewis SPEED Program Briefing
to: "Lewis; Shontel M. - CC Member District 8 Denver City Council" <Shontel.Lewis@denvergov.org>; "Carey;
cc: "Hudson; Tymber - CC Marketing and Communications Staff" <Tymber.Hudson@denvergov.org>;
b:
Hello Councilmember Lewis and Jesse,

Thank you both for your time and questions earlier this week discussing DOTI’s SPEED program. Attached are the briefing slides as well as program collateral.

Also attached is the equity and mitigation report developed by DOTI and OSEI summarizing potential risks and associated mitigations to protect marginalized communities from unnecessary hardships caused by safety speed cameras. DOTI and OSEI have developed an action plan to track the implementation of developed mitigation strategies to ensure program success and community transparency.

We also have some follow up information for you related to your question about the data used to develop the local, regional, and short trip assessments for the Federal and Alameda corridors.
  1.  The DOTI travel demand model is based on DRCOG's regional travel demand model. DRCOG compiles and aggregates socio-economic and travel diary data from household travel surveys, RTD surveys, and census data sources. Individual people's locations are not tracked unless they willingly choose to participate in the household travel survey.
  2.  The data is then cleaned, aggregated, and validated to ensure it replicates regional demographic, land use, and traffic data. The data is updated every 5 years or so to reflect changing trends (such as work from home, changes in RTD's transit service etc.).
  3.  The travel diary data requested by DRCOG is very detailed and includes all kinds of information about a person's typical day - how many trips they took, how did they travel, what was the trip purpose, what time did they leave, what route did they use etc. More technical details are explained here - https://www.drcog.org/data-maps-modeling/data-modeling.

A quick reminder, we have no Council action items scheduled at this time, but I will let you know when that changes.

Let us know if you have follow up questions or would like to schedule a future briefing to dive into any additional details with the DOTI and OSEI team.

We will also be sharing a social media toolkit with Councilmembers to amplify safety messaging through social media and newsletters throughout the fall.

        Alaina McWhorter, MPA, CNP | Strategic Advisor
Transportation & Infrastructure | City and County of Denver
alaina.mcwhorter@denvergov.org<mailto:alaina.mcwhorter@denvergov.org>
Pronouns | She/Her/Hers
Contact 311 <https://www.denvergov.org/content/denvergov/en/denver-311-help-center.html> | Denver Moves Everyone<https://denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Everyone> | Trash Services Mobile App<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Recycle-Compost-Trash/Recycle-Compost-Trash-Schedules-and-Reminders> | Sidewalk & Solid Waste Rebate Application<https://denvergov.org/dotirebates> | Sidewalk Program<https://denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Sidewalks>


-----Original Appointment-----
From: McWhorter, Alaina - DOTI Strategic Advisor
Sent: Monday, May 12, 2025 1:00 PM
To: McWhorter, Alaina - DOTI Strategic Advisor; DOTI Ordinance; Lewis, Shontel M. - CC Member District 8 Denver City Council; Carey, Jesse - CC City Council Aide Senior; Decker, Jay - DOTI CE2159 City Planner Principal; Eisinger, John R. - DOTI CE2777 Manager Engineering and Science; Calderon, Jessica M. - OSEI Director of Operations and Innovation; Bryant, Jamila N. - OSEI CA2308 Administrator II; Smith-Coronado, Mayra - OSEI Senior Racial Equity Data Analyst; Hillhouse, Jennifer L. - DOTI City Planning Director; Gilbert, Kayla M. - DOTI Administrator II
Cc: Hudson, Tymber - CC Marketing and Communications Staff; MO Legislative Affairs; Tonikian, Victoria - MO Deputy Legislative Director
Subject: D8 CM Lewis SPEED Program Briefing
When: Wednesday, July 23, 2025 3:15 PM-4:00 PM (UTC-07:00) Mountain Time (US & Canada).
Where: Microsoft Teams Meeting; 3:00 PM

DOTI’s SPEED program – 2024 Accomplishments and 2025 look ahead
This is DOTI’s new program announced with the Mayor and City Council this past June. The program increases safety by reducing speeds through several targeted actions on Federal Boulevard and Alameda Avenue. These two corridors are a part of the city’s high injury network – the streets where most serious and fatal injury crashes are occurring – and will see new safety improvements implemented in 2025 aimed at reducing fatalities as Denver moves toward its goal of zero fatalities by 2030.
   *   Saturating corridors with speed limit signs and speed feedback signs, providing visual reminders of posted limits; Start directed marketing program to encourage drivers to slow down
   *   Programming traffic signals to slow vehicles down to the posted limits
   *   Engineering signal timing to prevent excessive speeding at night by having more signals rest in red until a car arrives
   *   Expanding use of signal timing that gives pedestrians a head start crossing the street before the light turns
   *   Deploying speed enforcement on both corridors including surging mobile speed vans and piloting automated speed enforcement cameras
   *    green
   *   Deploying speed enforcement on both corridors including surging mobile speed vans and piloting automated speed enforcement cameras

From: Mallory Huggins <mallory.huggins@gmail.com>
Sent: Monday, July 28, 2025 8:41 AM
To: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Alameda Road Diet / Gazette article
Hi Greg,

My neighbors and I are very excited about the Alameda Road Diet project, and were frustrated by the Denver Gazette article<https:/denvergazette.com/news/wash-park-residents-upset-alameda-avenue-construction-project/article_0840d019-ac7a-4928-bab5-42ac2c574907.html> from last week spotlighting neighbors who are petitioning against the project. I am reaching out to document my support for the project, as someone who walks, bikes, and drives around this neighborhood and finds Alameda to be a street in desperate need of reconfiguration, and to see if you have any advice on how my neighbors and I can best support the project. I know the project is already approved and in the works, but I also know that angry residents can have a lot of power and I want to make sure it's clear to the right folks that they do not speak for the neighborhood.

Thanks for any advice you can provide!
Mallory

--
Mallory Huggins
(303) 947-7013

Pronouns: she/her

b:

  ________________________________
From: Garrett Fardon Personal <garrett.fardon@gmail.com>
Sent: Monday, July 28, 2025 4:06:32 PM
To: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Alameda Road Diet
Hi, I'm a resident of Platt Park who commutes along Downing and Alameda regularly. I'm really excited about the proposed road diet along Alameda to calm traffic and to make it a safer experience when crossing the road as a driver, pedestrian, and cyclist. I recently saw that a group of residents and business owners in the nearby affluent neighborhood are opposing this proven, reasonable safety intervention over fears of bollards (https://www.actforalameda.com<https://www.actforalameda.com>). I'm writing to beg the office to please not backslide on this project- as someone who tries to use non-car modes of transportation I've seen a great deal of projects aimed at making streets safer for road users other than drivers be eliminated over unproven concerns from a few residents or business owners. Please listen to the traffic experts and not cave to a few rich constituents that are fear mongering over the idea of bollards. Thank you



-Garrett Fardon (he/his)

To: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org<mailto:Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Alameda Road Diet
Hi, I'm a resident of Platt Park who commutes along Downing and Alameda regularly. I'm really excited about the proposed road diet along Alameda to calm traffic and to make it a safer experience when crossing the road as a driver, pedestrian, and cyclist. I recently saw that a group of residents and business owners in the nearby affluent neighborhood are opposing this proven, reasonable safety intervention over fears of bollards (https://www.actforalameda.com<https:/www.actforalameda.com>). I'm writing to beg the office to please not backslide on this project- as someone who tries to use non-car modes of transportation I've seen a great deal of projects aimed at making streets safer for road users other than drivers be eliminated over unproven concerns from a few residents or business owners. Please listen to the traffic experts and not cave to a few rich constituents that are fear mongering over the idea of bollards. Thank you


-Garrett Fardon (he/his)

Subject: [EXTERNAL] Alameda lane reconfiguration
Greg,

I’m not sure how aware you are of the growing concern & opposition from neighbors regarding the E Alameda Lane Reconfiguration. There’s been a couple articles in the Denver Gazette<https:/denvergazette.com/news/wash-park-residents-upset-alameda-avenue-construction-project/article_0840d019-ac7a-4928-bab5-42ac2c574907.html> recently along with a website<https:/www.actforalameda.com/> about this. A supportive website<https:/safer-alameda.com> was also just created by other advocates.

I have arranged to meet with some of the neighbors concerned about the project on Tuesday, August 12 at 4pm at Blackbird Public House at Alameda & Downing. I’m also opening it up to leaders & neighbors in WWPNA. It’d be great if you could join us with any documentation that can further explain the project & impact expected to the adjacent streets, as this seems to be a concern DOTI has not addressed yet, at least not in public.

Please review the article & websites for concerns from the neighbors. While the focus has been very narrowly limited to Alameda & adjacent properties, it’s important that we look at & address the other issues that are present nearby both currently existing & expected from the project. My reading is that the intersections of Downing at Cedar, Dakota & Virginia are of the greatest concern, beyond the traffic volume changes to the e-w parallel streets.

I’m also inviting council members from districts 6 & 7 to join so they have an opportunity to learn about the neighbors concerns. Please confirm if you or an engineer familiar with the project can join us, not just Nancy Kuhn.

Thank you,
Thomas Topero

Council members Kashmann & Alvidrez, I am forwarding you a message I sent to Greg Cieciek at DOTI concerning an opportunity to meet with neighbors concerned about the Alameda Lane Repurposing. I know both of your offices have been contacted

Council members Kashmann & Alvidrez,

I am forwarding you a message I sent to Greg Cieciek at DOTI concerning an opportunity to meet with neighbors concerned about the Alameda Lane Repurposing. I know both of your offices have been contacted by concerned residents, including creating a website that states concerns about distribution of traffic onto parallel neighborhood streets.

In order to fill in the gaps of understanding about the project & timing (fluid due to SPEED initiative), I have arranged to meet with some of the neighbors who created the website. We will be meeting on Tuesday, August 12 at 4pm at Blackbird Public House. I request that you or one of your staff be present with us as we discuss this project & the issues that need to be addressed but have not been, at least not publicly by DOTI.

I’m also opening this up to WWPNA leaders & neighbors as it’s been over a year since the latest display was open to the public for comment. My goal is to have a civil discussion about the project, how it’s intended to resolve issues identified for prevention, not just reaction, and to consider solutions to the expected & planned-for additional pass-through traffic flow onto neighborhood streets. Your participation will send a strong message about your support for residents & their concerns as changes continue to be made to our neighborhoods.

Since the article was published, an additional website<https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://safer-alameda.com__;!!M87Ej6RJKlw!V6hwYwY73eZcvgPUvzJaPviW3U15QvwKWbPepUaYVcxsqw6z5tbx4F4u91PLV0Ig519AcApob5N4XH3q1HyTRsd0Fjc$> in support of the project has been created. While not by neighbors of the neighborhoods, it reflects support of many who do reside or pass through our neighborhoods.

Please respond with who will be available to be present to represent your office.

Civically,

b:
Hi Jon,

Let me know if this works:

      Thank you for taking the time to share your concerns regarding the Alameda Lane Repurposing project.

      To clarify, the project does not involve reducing Alameda Avenue from four lanes to two. Rather, the proposed design would convert the existing four-lanes to three lanes with a center turn lane at many intersections along the corridor. This configuration has been shown nationwide to reduce traffic accidents while maintaining reasonable traffic flow.

      The project will also not severely impact access to business along the corridor. The goal for this project is to strike a balance—improving safety for all users while maintaining vehicle access to residential areas and businesses along the corridor.

      Most importantly, the project does not include adding bike lanes along Alameda itself. Instead, it focuses on improving the safety and visibility of north–south bike and pedestrian crossings of Alameda. These enhancements aim to make it safer for people walking and biking across the corridor.

Thanks,
Greg

Customer message:
"I read an article in Westword magazine 7/25/25 that stated that the City of Denver will finalize a plan to reduce East Alameda Ave from 4 to 2 lanes and add bike lanes. This is another example of Denver strangling business on the major thoroughfares. The general public cannot access local businesses due to congestion caused by limiting car lanes to the point that the frustration builds to where people will longer commute to Denver due to the hassle and congestion. The businesses have already expressed concern to the City and the City does seem to care. Not everyone can ride a bike or wants to ride a bike to shop and for meals. Denver, like all western US cities were built around the car, this is another example along with the many reasons to leave Denver."

Subject: [EXTERNAL] Alameda Lane Repurposing
Council members Kashmann & Alvidrez,

I am forwarding you a message I sent to Greg Cieciek at DOTI concerning an opportunity to meet with neighbors concerned about the Alameda Lane Repurposing. I know both of your offices have been contacted by concerned residents, including creating a website that states concerns about distribution of traffic onto parallel neighborhood streets.

In order to fill in the gaps of understanding about the project & timing (fluid due to SPEED initiative), I have arranged to meet with some of the neighbors who created the website. We will be meeting on Tuesday, August 12 at 4pm at Blackbird Public House. I request that you or one of your staff be present with us as we discuss this project & the issues that need to be addressed but have not been, at least not publicly by DOTI.

I’m also opening this up to WWPNA leaders & neighbors as it’s been over a year since the latest display was open to the public for comment. My goal is to have a civil discussion about the project, how it’s intended to resolve issues identified for prevention, not just reaction, and to consider solutions to the expected & planned-for additional pass-through traffic flow onto neighborhood streets. Your participation will send a strong message about your support for residents & their concerns as changes continue to be made to our neighborhoods.

Since the article was published, an additional website<https:/safer-alameda.com> in support of the project has been created. While not by neighbors of the neighborhoods, it reflects support of many who do reside or pass through our neighborhoods.

Please respond with who will be available to be present to represent your office.

Civically,
Thomas Topero

Begin forwarded message:

From: Thomas Topero <thomas.topero@gmail.com<mailto:thomas.topero@gmail.com>>
Date: July 30, 2025 at 11:11:44 MDT
To: Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org<mailto:Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>
Subject: Alameda lane reconfiguration

﻿
Greg,

I’m not sure how aware you are of the growing concern & opposition from neighbors regarding the E Alameda Lane Reconfiguration. There’s been a couple articles in the Denver Gazette<https:/denvergazette.com/news/wash-park-residents-upset-alameda-avenue-construction-project/article_0840d019-ac7a-4928-bab5-42ac2c574907.html> recently along with a website<https:/www.actforalameda.com/> about this. A supportive website<https:/safer-alameda.com> was also just created by other advocates.

I have arranged to meet with some of the neighbors concerned about the project on Tuesday, August 12 at 4pm at Blackbird Public House at Alameda & Downing. I’m also opening it up to leaders & neighbors in WWPNA. It’d be great if you could join us with any documentation that can further explain the project & impact expected to the adjacent streets, as this seems to be a concern DOTI has not addressed yet, at least not in public.

Please review the article & websites for concerns from the neighbors. While the focus has been very narrowly limited to Alameda & adjacent properties, it’s important that we look at & address the other issues that are present nearby both currently existing & expected from the project. My reading is that the intersections of Downing at Cedar, Dakota & Virginia are of the greatest concern, beyond the traffic volume changes to the e-w parallel streets.

I’m also inviting council members from districts 6 & 7 to join so they have an opportunity to learn about the neighbors concerns. Please confirm if you or an engineer familiar with the project can join us, not just Nancy Kuhn.

Thank you,
Thomas Topero

b:
Was tracking it was coming into you today. Doing some final analysis on our project elements and will work to respond.


Get Outlook for iOS<https://aka.ms/o0ukef>
  ________________________________
From: Dolan, Jeff J. - MO Chief Strategy Officer and Counsel - Mayor's Office <Jeff.Dolan@denvergov.org>
Sent: Thursday, July 31, 2025 9:47:13 AM
To: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>
Subject: FW: [EXTERNAL] Constituent concerns re: proposed changes to Alameda Ave

Hi Amy,
Just received this.  presume you’ve received as well?  Pls advise on next steps/response etc.

Jeff


From: Jill Anschutz <jill.hamilton@mac.com>
Sent: Thursday, July 31, 2025 9:37 AM
To: mayor@denvergov.org
Cc: Dolan, Jeff J. - MO Chief Strategy Officer and Counsel - Mayor's Office <Jeff.Dolan@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Constituent concerns re: proposed changes to Alameda Ave
Dear Mayor Johnston,

On behalf of over 300 concerned residents and business owners along the Alameda corridor, we are reaching out to express serious concerns about the proposed changes to Alameda Avenue in the Wash Park area.

While we understand the goal of improving safety and traffic flow, our community strongly believes that removing a travel lane to add turn lanes will:

   *   Increase congestion and backups on Alameda,
   *   Push frustrated drivers into nearby residential streets, including Virginia Avenue, creating new safety hazards,
   *   Harm the accessibility and economic vitality of our local businesses, and
   *   Require future, reactive interventions due to unintended traffic and safety impacts.
Attached to this email, you will find:

  1.  A formal letter outlining our concerns in detail,
  2.  Over 300 resident and business owner signatures in opposition to the project, and
  3.  A recent Denver Gazette article highlighting the issue.
We respectfully request that the City pause this project and engage with our community to find a safer, more effective solution before proceeding.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Sincerely,

Jill Anschutz
502.938.2947

f: 2025-08-06_11-51-45 - FW_ Group Upset over proposed changes to Alameda.pdf
s: FW: Group Upset over proposed changes to Alameda
to: "Holloway; Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager" <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; "Ford; Amy - DOTI
b:
Vicente,

Providing you this overview of the E. Alameda Avenue safety project between Franklin and Lincoln streets and status.

Project overview
The plan is to move forward with safety improvements on Alameda Avenue, making the corridor safer for all users.

The work planned includes:
   *   New designated turn lanes at eight intersections (Pennsylvania St., Pearl St., Washington St., Clarkson St., Emerson St., Ogden St., Lafayette St., and Humboldt St.)
   *   Painted pedestrian bulb outs at every intersection between Pearl St. and Franklin St. (11 intersections)
   *   New striped medians and pedestrian refuge islands at these locations: Washington St. intersection (west side); Emerson St. intersection (east side);  Downing, Marion, and Corona street intersections (east and west sides).
   *   Reduced speed limit to 25 mph
   *   Installation of a Rectangular Rapid Flashing Beacon, pedestrian refuge island, and marked crosswalks at Franklin (note: this RRFB is part of separate plans and is likely to be installed first and separately from the rest of the improvements)
   *   New marked crosswalks at Grant St.
   *   New School Zone signage and markings east of Broadway, east of Logan St. and east of Humboldt St.
   *   Additional on-street parking east of Pearl St.
   *   Protected bike lane approaches and green crossing markings at Washington St. and Emerson St.

Status
We don’t have an exact date yet for implementation.  We’ve been giving travel patterns a little time to adjust to temporary lane closures happening a little west of there, between Bannock Street and Santa Fe Drive, for the Alameda underpass project, and have been affording time to consider and address new and lingering community concerns about the plan for the lane repurposing.

Project need:
Some of the data we’ve collected that speak to the need for the project include:

Crash data
We gathered crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor. Safety improvements for drivers have been recommended in alignment with the crash data (attached).

Traffic Speed and Volume data
The posted speed limit along this segment of Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. 2024 speed data shows that speeding is an issue along this corridor (with 85th percentile speed = 38 mph). Following installation of our safety improvements, the posted speed limit is planned to be 25 miles per hour.

We’ve collected average daily traffic volumes to predict potential diversion to side streets as a result of the lane repurposing work.  2024 counts showed the average daily traffic volumes of Alameda Ave, west of Downing, to be around 14,800 vehicles per day.
   *   DOTI believes no more than 10% of vehicles total will reroute to side streets throughout the day.
   *   A diversion analysis conducted for Virginia Avenue predicts approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to about 1-2 vehicles per minute.

Average daily vehicle volumes on parallel east/west roads include:
   *   Virginia between Logan and Downing = ~ 4,200 vehicles a day
   *   1st Avenue (between Logan and Downing) = ~2,600 vehicles per day
   *   1st Avenue/Speer (between Downing and University) = ~50,000 vehicles per day

Wanted to mention that many of the questions that have been asked about the project are addressed on our website in an FAQ<https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>.

Hope this is helpful.

Sincerely,
Nancy

  NANCY KUHN| Director
  City & County of Denver
  Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Communications Division
  Phone: 303-513-6046
  nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org<mailto:nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org>

Subject: RE: Group Upset over proposed changes to Alameda

Vicente,
Will send you some info shortly.
Will be back at my computer in a few minutes.
Sincerely,
Nancy

  City & County of Denver
  Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Communications Division

From: Vicente Arenas <Vicente.Arenas@kdvr.com<mailto:Vicente.Arenas@kdvr.com>>
Sent: Wednesday, August 6, 2025 11:41 AM
To: Mayor's Communications Office <MOComms@denvergov.org<mailto:MOComms@denvergov.org>>; Alvidrez, Flor C. - CC Member District 7 Denver City Council <Flor.Alvidrez@denvergov.org<mailto:Flor.Alvidrez@denvergov.org>>; Kashmann, Paul J. - CC Member District 6 Denver City Council <Paul.Kashmann@denvergov.org<mailto:Paul.Kashmann@denvergov.org>>; DOTI Communications <PW.Comms@denvergov.org<mailto:PW.Comms@denvergov.org>>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Group Upset over proposed changes to Alameda
Hello Mayor Johnston, Councilmember Kashmann, and Council Member Alvidrez, and DOTI,

I’m hoping to get a response to the story below for a story we are working on today.

A group called Act for Alameda is circulating a petition expressing concerns about proposed changes to Alameda Avenue near Virginia Avenue.

Here is a snippet from the petition.

We, the undersigned residents and business owners of the neighborhoods and commercial corridors along Alameda Avenue, are writing to express serious concerns regarding the proposed roadway redesign. The changes — particularly the conversion of travel lanes into a turn lane — threaten to create substantial and lasting negative impacts on traffic safety, neighborhood livability, and the vitality of local businesses.

Link to petition: https://www.actforalameda.com/letter-to-the-denver-mayor<https:/www.actforalameda.com/letter-to-the-denver-mayor>

If any of you are available for an on-camera interview at your earliest convenience, please let me know.

We would also consider inlcuding any written responses you may have as well.

The story is scheduled to begin airing at 4pm this afternoon.

Thanks,

Vicente Arenas
Fox 31/Channel 2 Denver Reporter
100 E Speer Blvd.
Denver CO 80203
832-788-8275
See my Stories at Vicente's Stories<https:/kdvr.com/author/vicente-arenas/>

Instagram Vicente's Instagram<https:/www.instagram.com/>

From: Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director <Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org<mailto:Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org>>
Sent: Wednesday, August 6, 2025 1:16 PM
To: Maass, Brian <BMaass@cbs.com<mailto:BMaass@cbs.com>>
Subject: Alameda project

<External Email>




Brian,

In advance of tomorrow, providing you this overview of the E. Alameda Avenue safety project between Franklin and Lincoln streets and status.

Project overview
The plan is to move forward with safety improvements on Alameda Avenue, making the corridor safer for all users.

The work planned includes:
   *   New designated turn lanes at eight intersections (Pennsylvania St., Pearl St., Washington St., Clarkson St., Emerson St., Ogden St., Lafayette St., and Humboldt St.)
   *   Painted pedestrian bulb outs at every intersection between Pearl St. and Franklin St. (11 intersections)
   *   New striped medians and pedestrian refuge islands at these locations: Washington St. intersection (west side); Emerson St. intersection (east side);  Downing, Marion, and Corona street intersections (east and west sides).
   *   Reduced speed limit to 25 mph
   *   Installation of a Rectangular Rapid Flashing Beacon, pedestrian refuge island, and marked crosswalks at Franklin (note: this RRFB is part of separate plans and is likely to be installed first and separately from the rest of the improvements)
   *   New marked crosswalks at Grant St.
   *   New School Zone signage and markings east of Broadway, east of Logan St. and east of Humboldt St.
   *   Additional on-street parking east of Pearl St.
   *   Protected bike lane approaches and green crossing markings at Washington St. and Emerson St.

Status
We don’t have an exact date yet for implementation.  We’ve been giving travel patterns a little time to adjust to temporary lane closures happening a little west of there, between Bannock Street and Santa Fe Drive, for the Alameda underpass project, and have been affording time to consider and address new and lingering community concerns about the plan for the lane repurposing.

Project need:
Some of the data we’ve collected that speak to the need for the project include:

Crash data
We gathered crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor. Safety improvements for drivers have been recommended in alignment with the crash data (attached).

Traffic Speed and Volume data
The posted speed limit along this segment of Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. 2024 speed data shows that speeding is an issue along this corridor (with 85th percentile speed = 38 mph). Following installation of our safety improvements, the posted speed limit is planned to be 25 miles per hour.

We’ve collected average daily traffic volumes to predict potential diversion to side streets as a result of the lane repurposing work.  2024 counts showed the average daily traffic volumes of Alameda Ave, west of Downing, to be around 14,800 vehicles per day.
   *   DOTI believes no more than 10% of vehicles total will reroute to side streets throughout the day.
   *   A diversion analysis conducted for Virginia Avenue predicts approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to about 1-2 vehicles per minute.

Average daily vehicle volumes on parallel east/west roads include:
   *   Virginia between Logan and Downing = ~ 4,200 vehicles a day
   *   1st Avenue (between Logan and Downing) = ~2,600 vehicles per day
   *   1st Avenue/Speer (between Downing and University) = ~50,000 vehicles per day

Wanted to mention that many of the questions that have been asked about the project are addressed on our website in an FAQ<https:/www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects>.

See you tomorrow.

Sincerely,
Nancy

  NANCY KUHN| Director
  City & County of Denver
  Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Communications Division
  Phone: 303-513-6046
  nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org<mailto:nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org>

Yes, I think that is fine if you want to do it that way.

From: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior
Sent: Wednesday, August 6, 2025 12:34 PM
To: Thomas Topero <thomas.topero@gmail.com>
Cc: molly.lanphier@denvergov.org
Subject: RE: [EXTERNAL] Alameda lane reconfiguration

Hi Thomas,

Thank you for reaching out and for organizing a meeting for neighbors to share their perspective views on the Alameda Lane Repurposing Project. We appreciate your continued engagement and advocacy on behalf of the community.

We are aware of both the growing opposition and the support the project has received. As with any proposed changes to a major corridor, it’s understandable that there are a range of opinions, and we’re committed to considering input from all voices.

We’re also currently reviewing our data collection with the latest numbers to ensure we’ve thoroughly examined traffic volumes, crash history, and potential impacts to nearby intersections and adjacent streets, including those you mentioned like Downing at Cedar, Dakota, and Virginia. Unfortunately, we will not have this analysis and data available before the meeting, so we will not be able to attend the meeting on August 12, but we truly value the dialogue that’s happening and want to continue gathering feedback from all sides.

We’ll be sure to share additional information as the project continues to develop and will continue to look for opportunities to connect with community and neighborhood groups. Please don’t hesitate to share any notes or follow-up questions that come out of your meeting—we’d be happy to review and respond where we can.

Thanks again for your outreach and for keeping us in the loop.

Best regards,
Greg

From: Thomas Topero <thomas.topero@gmail.com<mailto:thomas.topero@gmail.com>>
Sent: Wednesday, July 30, 2025 11:12 AM
To: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org<mailto:Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Alameda lane reconfiguration
Greg,

I’m not sure how aware you are of the growing concern & opposition from neighbors regarding the E Alameda Lane Reconfiguration. There’s been a couple articles in the Denver Gazette<https:/denvergazette.com/news/wash-park-residents-upset-alameda-avenue-construction-project/article_0840d019-ac7a-4928-bab5-42ac2c574907.html> recently along with a website<https:/www.actforalameda.com/> about this. A supportive website<https:/safer-alameda.com> was also just created by other advocates.

I have arranged to meet with some of the neighbors concerned about the project on Tuesday, August 12 at 4pm at Blackbird Public House at Alameda & Downing. I’m also opening it up to leaders & neighbors in WWPNA. It’d be great if you could join us with any documentation that can further explain the project & impact expected to the adjacent streets, as this seems to be a concern DOTI has not addressed yet, at least not in public.

Please review the article & websites for concerns from the neighbors. While the focus has been very narrowly limited to Alameda & adjacent properties, it’s important that we look at & address the other issues that are present nearby both currently existing & expected from the project. My reading is that the intersections of Downing at Cedar, Dakota & Virginia are of the greatest concern, beyond the traffic volume changes to the e-w parallel streets.

I’m also inviting council members from districts 6 & 7 to join so they have an opportunity to learn about the neighbors concerns. Please confirm if you or an engineer familiar with the project can join us, not just Nancy Kuhn.

Thank you,
Thomas Topero

b:
Hi All- Thank you for everyone’s continued work on the Alameda project. I just wanted to make note of follow-ups from this morning’s call with Amy and Tykus and make sure we are all on the same page as to next steps. From my understanding, they asked for:

  1.  Communications Plan- Greg and I can work on this and send to Brittany for review
  2.  Estimate on redesign for Westbound direction only- Brett & Steven, hoping you can tackle
  3.  Virginia/ Downing Analysis- do we want to include this at all either in an explanation of additional scope needed or an actual rough estimation of cost for proposed improvements? If we decide to include this would also land with Brett’s team.

Let me know thoughts on #3 and if I missed anything! I’ll leave it to Brett to give us a timeline of when these things can occur and we can hold ourselves to that (knowing next week will be weird).

Thanks!
Molly

cc: "Marfitano; Steven C. - DOTI CE0397 Engineer" <Steven.Marfitano@denvergov.org>
b:
Hi Matt,

I hope this week back from PNW-vacay hasn’t been too depressing 😊! Wanted to reach out to you regarding the Alameda Lane Reduction project. We have been working with leadership to triage some high level community and leadership concerns with constructing the project and recently received clear direction on a path forward. After evaluating different options for moving forward in light of community and leadership concerns, we have decided to move forward with a partial lane reduction (i.e. lane reduction only in the WB direction). This will constitute a redesign of the project but aligns with the initial idea that we started this project with.

I am copying one of our bright new engineer PMs, Steven Marfitano, who will be stepping in to manage this project from the city side. Could you both find a time for us to discuss these changes, the impacts to TO budget and schedule, and next steps?

I am sure there are a lot of questions we can discuss during this meeting but let us know if you have any key questions that come up in the meantime. Thanks!

Brett Boncore, PE| Engineering Supervisor
City & County of Denver
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Design
Phone: 720.865.3214
brett.boncore@denvergov.org<mailto:brett.boncore@denvergov.org>

Hi Matt,

We have been working with leadership to triage some high level community and leadership concerns with constructing the project and recently received clear direction on a path forward. From: Dave Hawkins <iamdavehawkins@gmail.com<mailto:iamdavehawkins@gmail.com>>
Sent: Wednesday, August 20, 2025 1:52 PM
To: Alvidrez, Flor C. - CC Member District 7 Denver City Council <Flor.Alvidrez@denvergov.org<mailto:Flor.Alvidrez@denvergov.org>>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] safer-alameda.com and support for Alameda Lane Reduction
Hello Councilwoman Alvidrez,

I’m a District 7 resident and created safer-alameda.com<https:/safer-alameda.com> with encouragement from my family and neighbors to connect others who support the Alameda lane reduction project.

In just a few weeks, 185 residents (and counting) have signed on, through a hand-built site, no funding, no PR machine. Just neighbors sharing with neighbors because we care about making Alameda safer.

I really appreciate your recent appearance on KDVR and your acknowledgment of Alameda’s designation as part of the High Injury Network.

As someone who crosses and drives Alameda daily, I’m eager to see this project move forward.

Thank you,
Dave Hawkins

84 S Clarkson St

Hi Brett,


Would you be able to get me access to the Alameda Masterworks - just as a user for now works. We can talk about transitioning things to me as PM when you are ready, I'd just like to see the project info.


Thanks.


steven




  Steven Marfitano, PE | Engineer
  City & County of Denver
  Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Implementation Division
  Phone: 720.437.0022
  steven.marfitano@denvergov.org<mailto:steven.marfitano@denvergov.org>

﻿

Dear Ms Lanphier
We are writing to express serious concerns regarding the proposed roadway redesign. The changes — particularly the conversion of travel lanes into a turn lane — threaten to create substantial and lasting negative impacts on traffic safety, neighborhood livability, and the vitality of local businesses.
​
Our key concerns are:
​
1. Increased congestion and traffic flow issues
Reducing Alameda’s travel lanes to add a dedicated turn lane will shrink roadway capacity and cause severe backups, particularly during peak hours. This bottleneck will create gridlock and slow traffic along a major east-west corridor.
​
2. Negative changes in driver behavior
Frustrated drivers will seek alternate routes through residential neighborhoods, leading to an increase in cut-through traffic. Over time, this shift in driver habits could become permanent, burdening roads that were never designed to serve as arterial routes.
​
3. Risk to Virginia Avenue and park-adjacent areas
Virginia Avenue, which borders Wash Park and community gathering areas, is particularly vulnerable. Redirected traffic will put pedestrians, cyclists, and families at risk, turning a recreational corridor into a high-traffic danger zone.
​
4. Neighborhood safety degradation
Residents who rely on Alameda for daily access will face greater difficulty entering and exiting their neighborhoods. More traffic will be diverted onto smaller residential streets, increasing the risk of collisions, speeding, and unsafe conditions for children, pedestrians, and pets.

5. Economic harm to local businesses
Businesses along Alameda depend on accessible, predictable traffic patterns. Increased congestion, confusion, and delays may deter customers, reduce visibility, and undercut foot traffic — threatening the health of small businesses that are vital to our community.

6. Lack of justifying safety data
Current traffic and safety data does not support the need for a major redesign. These changes appear to be driven more by theoretical modeling than by demonstrated issues on the ground.

7. Need for transparent, collaborative planning
We urge the City to pause the project and commit to a broader, more inclusive public process. Community voices have not been adequately engaged in a change that will deeply impact our quality of life and daily mobility.

We appreciate your leadership and ask that you work with DOTI to reconsider the scope and design of this project. We welcome the opportunity to meet with you and your staff to offer community-driven input that prioritizes both safety and common-sense transportation planning.
​
Thank you for your time and commitment to a safer, more inclusive Denver.
​
Respectfully,
Raymond & Robin Morgan
480 S Marion Pkwy 1103A
Denver, CO. 80209

From: Raymond Morgan <rlmrkm@gmail.com>
Sent: Monday, August 25, 2025 7:42 AM
To: Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] Alameda Lane Repurposing Project
﻿
Dear Mr Cieciek
We are writing to express serious concerns regarding the proposed roadway redesign. The changes — particularly the conversion of travel lanes into a turn lane — threaten to create substantial and lasting negative impacts on traffic safety, neighborhood livability, and the vitality of local businesses.
​
Our key concerns are:
​
1. Increased congestion and traffic flow issues
Reducing Alameda’s travel lanes to add a dedicated turn lane will shrink roadway capacity and cause severe backups, particularly during peak hours. This bottleneck will create gridlock and slow traffic along a major east-west corridor.
​
2. Negative changes in driver behavior
Frustrated drivers will seek alternate routes through residential neighborhoods, leading to an increase in cut-through traffic. Over time, this shift in driver habits could become permanent, burdening roads that were never designed to serve as arterial routes.
​
3. Risk to Virginia Avenue and park-adjacent areas
Virginia Avenue, which borders Wash Park and community gathering areas, is particularly vulnerable. Redirected traffic will put pedestrians, cyclists, and families at risk, turning a recreational corridor into a high-traffic danger zone.
​
4. Neighborhood safety degradation
Residents who rely on Alameda for daily access will face greater difficulty entering and exiting their neighborhoods. More traffic will be diverted onto smaller residential streets, increasing the risk of collisions, speeding, and unsafe conditions for children, pedestrians, and pets.

5. Economic harm to local businesses
Businesses along Alameda depend on accessible, predictable traffic patterns. Increased congestion, confusion, and delays may deter customers, reduce visibility, and undercut foot traffic — threatening the health of small businesses that are vital to our community.

6. Lack of justifying safety data
Current traffic and safety data does not support the need for a major redesign. These changes appear to be driven more by theoretical modeling than by demonstrated issues on the ground.

7. Need for transparent, collaborative planning
We urge the City to pause the project and commit to a broader, more inclusive public process. Community voices have not been adequately engaged in a change that will deeply impact our quality of life and daily mobility.

We appreciate your leadership and ask that you work with DOTI to reconsider the scope and design of this project. We welcome the opportunity to meet with you and your staff to offer community-driven input that prioritizes both safety and common-sense transportation planning.
​
Thank you for your time and commitment to a safer, more inclusive Denver.
​
Respectfully,
Raymond & Robin Morgan
480 S Marion Pkwy 1103A
Denver, CO. 80209

cc: flor.alvidrez@denvergov.org
b:
Dear Mayor Johnston, Ms. Ford, and DOTI Representatives:

Preface: This letter represents an organic grassroots effort organized by David Hawkins, a local father concerned about traffic safety in our neighborhood, neighbors, and community members. The signatures were collected through our community website safer-alameda.com<http://safer-alameda.com>, where neighbors could learn about the project and add their support. Over several weeks we have gathered 182 signatures from community members who support this important safety initiative.

We, the undersigned residents, business owners, and community members, write to express our strong support for the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project. This initiative represents thoughtful, evidence-based transportation planning that will improve safety, enhance livability, and benefit our community for years to come.

Why We Support This Project

Enhanced Safety for All Users: The project's safety improvements including protected crossings, pedestrian islands, and reduced crossing distances will significantly improve safety for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers alike. These evidence-based design elements have proven successful in cities across the nation.

Improved Traffic Flow: Dedicated turn lanes will prevent turning vehicles from blocking through traffic, actually improving traffic flow and reducing congestion. This is a win-win solution that addresses mobility while prioritizing safety.

Neighborhood Protection: Strategic bollards and turn restrictions will prevent dangerous cut-through traffic in residential areas, making our neighborhoods safer for families and children. This protects the residential character of our community.

Economic Benefits: Research consistently shows that walkable streets with appropriate traffic calming measures increase local business revenue. Slower, safer streets encourage people to stop, shop, and engage with local businesses.

Environmental Responsibility: By creating more walkable infrastructure and encouraging sustainable transportation options, this project aligns with Denver's climate goals and contributes to improved air quality for our community.

Addressing Concerns with Facts

We understand that some community members have expressed concerns about this project. However, extensive research and real-world examples demonstrate that well-designed road reconfigurations like this one:

Typically have minimal impact on travel times while dramatically improving safety
Reduce serious crashes by up to 25% according to Federal Highway Administration studies

Improve pedestrian safety by up to 40% with protected crossing infrastructure
Support local economic development through increased walkability Maintain emergency vehicle access while improving response times through reduced congestion

Our Request

We urge DOTI to move forward with the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project as planned. This project represents the kind of forward-thinking, evidence-based transportation planning that Denver needs to create safer, more livable neighborhoods for all residents.

The safety of our community members—pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers —should be our top priority. This project achieves that goal while maintaining mobility and supporting local economic development.

Thank you for your leadership in prioritizing safety and livability in our transportation infrastructure.

Sincerely,
David Hawkins & the following

f: 2025-08-25_14-21-00 - FW_ _EXTERNAL_ 182 signatures - Community Letter of Support.pdf
s: FW: 182 signatures - Community Letter of Support for the
to: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org>; Hillhouse, Jennifer L. -
b:
Amy Ford | Executive Director
City & County of Denver
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure
Phone: (303) 514-4913
amy.ford@denvergov.org<mailto:amy.ford@denvergov.org>

[cid:image001.png@01DC15CB.79F8DFD0]

From: Dave Hawkins <iamdavehawkins@gmail.com>
Sent: Monday, August 25, 2025 2:17 PM
To: Johnston, Mike - MO Mayor <Mike.Johnston@denvergov.org>; Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org>
Cc: Alvidrez, Flor C. - CC Member District 7 Denver City Council <Flor.Alvidrez@denvergov.org>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] 182 signatures - Community Letter of Support for the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project
Dear Mayor Johnston, Ms. Ford, and DOTI Representatives:

Preface: This letter represents an organic grassroots effort organized by David Hawkins, a local father concerned about traffic safety in our neighborhood, neighbors, and community members. The signatures were collected through our community website safer-alameda.com<http:/safer-alameda.com>, where neighbors could learn about the project and add their support. Over several weeks we have gathered 182 signatures from community members who support this important safety initiative.

We, the undersigned residents, business owners, and community members, write to express our strong support for the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project. This initiative represents thoughtful, evidence-based transportation planning that will improve safety, enhance livability, and benefit our community for years to come.

Why We Support This Project

Enhanced Safety for All Users: The project's safety improvements including protected crossings, pedestrian islands, and reduced crossing distances will significantly improve safety for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers alike. These evidence-based design elements have proven successful in cities across the nation.

Improved Traffic Flow: Dedicated turn lanes will prevent turning vehicles from blocking through traffic, actually improving traffic flow and reducing congestion. This is a win-win solution that addresses mobility while prioritizing safety.

Neighborhood Protection: Strategic bollards and turn restrictions will prevent dangerous cut-through traffic in residential areas, making our neighborhoods safer for families and children. This protects the residential character of our community.

Economic Benefits: Research consistently shows that walkable streets with appropriate traffic calming measures increase local business revenue. Slower, safer streets encourage people to stop, shop, and engage with local businesses.

Environmental Responsibility: By creating more walkable infrastructure and encouraging sustainable transportation options, this project aligns with Denver's climate goals and contributes to improved air quality for our community.

Addressing Concerns with Facts

We understand that some community members have expressed concerns about this project. However, extensive research and real-world examples demonstrate that well-designed road reconfigurations like this one:

Typically have minimal impact on travel times while dramatically improving safety
Reduce serious crashes by up to 25% according to Federal Highway Administration studies

Improve pedestrian safety by up to 40% with protected crossing infrastructure
Support local economic development through increased walkability Maintain emergency vehicle access while improving response times through reduced congestion

Our Request

We urge DOTI to move forward with the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project as planned. This project represents the kind of forward-thinking, evidence-based transportation planning that Denver needs to create safer, more livable neighborhoods for all residents.

The safety of our community members—pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers —should be our top priority. This project achieves that goal while maintaining mobility and supporting local economic development.

Thank you for your leadership in prioritizing safety and livability in our transportation infrastructure.

Sincerely,
David Hawkins & the following

Environmental Responsibility: By creating more walkable infrastructure and encouraging sustainable transportation options, this project aligns with Denver's climate goals and contributes to improved air quality for our community.

Addressing Concerns with Facts

We understand that some community members have expressed concerns about this project. However, extensive research and real-world examples demonstrate that well-designed road reconfigurations like this one:

Typically have minimal impact on travel times while dramatically improving safety
Reduce serious crashes by up to 25% according to Federal Highway Administration studies

Improve pedestrian safety by up to 40% with protected crossing infrastructure
Support local economic development through increased walkability Maintain emergency vehicle access while improving response times through reduced congestion

Our Request

We urge DOTI to move forward with the Alameda Lanes Repurposing Project as planned. This project represents the kind of forward-thinking, evidence-based transportation planning that Denver needs to create safer, more livable neighborhoods for all residents.

The safety of our community members—pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers —should be our top priority. This project achieves that goal while maintaining mobility and supporting local economic development.

Thank you for your leadership in prioritizing safety and livability in our transportation infrastructure.

Sincerely,
David Hawkins & the following
