Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes (not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.

Here was the original fee:
[cid:18e639ad-8b83-4984-85f4-50a632602cfb]
The original schedule lasted 9 months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.

The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get towards $50k.

  1.
Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation
This includes removal of all traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings, and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3.
(these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3 months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).

  2.
In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible, we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)


  1.
Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)

  2.
Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))


  1.
Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be used.

  2.
Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%, 90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process; propose removal of the 60% plan set and review.  (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

The signal modifications are the largest outstanding item in our approach. Please see a few notes below regarding an initial review of the intersections. I’d like to confirm the City’s desired approach for the design/construction of modified signal structures on these corners or if signal head modification is the desired approach. This will obviously impact the design and construction costs.

•         Logan – Signal modification/reconstruction not necessary.

•         Pearl – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road.

•         Washington – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Emerson – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road.

•         Downing – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Marion – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.



I was also hoping to discuss the Virginia & Downing intersection with you in more detail. Pending analysis and recommendation, there may be signal modifications at this intersection, but they are likely limited to minor equipment modifications. More importantly, you mentioned the potential of curb extensions. From a scoping perspective, I’m trying to determine if we should budget for this condition which would be our worst-case scenario level of effort. Furthermore, we would need to consider effort for design survey and storm modifications. I’d like to get your input on how to approach at this stage of the project.

Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes (not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.



Here was the original fee:

[cid:image002.png@01DC2C5A.92597FD0]

The original schedule lasted 9 months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.



The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get towards $50k.

  1.  Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation

This includes removal of all traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings, and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3.

(these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3 months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).



  1.  In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible, we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)



  1.  Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)



  1.  Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))



  1.  Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be used.



  1.  Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%, 90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process; propose removal of the 60% plan set and review.  (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

Hi, Steven. Thanks for the email. I’m realizing now that I never sent the invitation for us to connect following our correspondence about a meeting time this week, my apologies. I’ll send now for 2P, assuming that time still works for you.



The signal modifications are the largest outstanding item in our approach. Please see a few notes below regarding an initial review of the intersections. I’d like to confirm the City’s desired approach for the design/construction of modified signal structures on these corners or if signal head modification is the desired approach. This will obviously impact the design and construction costs.

•         Logan – Signal modification/reconstruction not necessary.

•         Pearl – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road.

•         Washington – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Emerson – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road.

•         Downing – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Marion – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.



I was also hoping to discuss the Virginia & Downing intersection with you in more detail. Pending analysis and recommendation, there may be signal modifications at this intersection, but they are likely limited to minor equipment modifications. More importantly, you mentioned the potential of curb extensions. From a scoping perspective, I’m trying to determine if we should budget for this condition which would be our worst-case scenario level of effort. Furthermore, we would need to consider effort for design survey and storm modifications. I’d like to get your input on how to approach at this stage of the project.



Regarding the status of a draft agreement; we are on track to have a draft developed by EOD with the exception of the items noted above. Let’s connect later to discuss these items. Thanks

  *   Alameda & Emerson Signal Modification – I am still working on fee development for this task including coordination with the surveyor and with Josh Jones (CCD) regarding required fiber interconnect improvements. Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes (not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.



Here was the original fee:

[cid:image002.png@01DC2C5A.92597FD0]

The original schedule lasted 9 months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.



The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get towards $50k.

  1.  Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation

This includes removal of all traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings, and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3.

(these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3 months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).



  1.  In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible, we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)



  1.  Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes (not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.

Here was the original fee:

[cid:image002.png@01DC2C5A.92597FD0]

The original schedule lasted 9 months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.

The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get towards $50k.

1.  Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation

This includes removal of all traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings, and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3.

(these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3 months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).

1.  In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible, we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)

1.  Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)

1.  Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))

1.  Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be used.

1.  Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%, 90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process; propose removal of the 60% plan set and review.  (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

Hi, Steven. Thanks for the email. I’m realizing now that I never sent the invitation for us to connect following our correspondence about a meeting time this week, my apologies. I’ll send now for 2P, assuming that time still works for you.

The signal modifications are the largest outstanding item in our approach. Please see a few notes below regarding an initial review of the intersections. I’d like to confirm the City’s desired approach for the design/construction of modified signal structures on these corners or if signal head modification is the desired approach. This will obviously impact the design and construction costs.

•         Logan – Signal modification/reconstruction not necessary.

•         Pearl – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road.

•         Washington – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Emerson – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road.

•         Downing – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Marion – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

I was also hoping to discuss the Virginia & Downing intersection with you in more detail. Pending analysis and recommendation, there may be signal modifications at this intersection, but they are likely limited to minor equipment modifications. More importantly, you mentioned the potential of curb extensions. From a scoping perspective, I’m trying to determine if we should budget for this condition which would be our worst-case scenario level of effort. Furthermore, we would need to consider effort for design survey and storm modifications. I’d like to get your input on how to approach at this stage of the project.

Regarding the status of a draft agreement; we are on track to have a draft developed by EOD with the exception of the items noted above. Let’s connect later to discuss these items. Thanks

Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes (not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.



Here was the original fee:

[cid:image002.png@01DC2C7A.9F0B4360]

The original schedule lasted 9 months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.



The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get towards $50k.

  1.  Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation

This includes removal of all traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings, and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3.

(these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3 months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).



  1.  In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible, we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)



  1.  Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)



  1.  Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))



  1.  Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be used.



  1.  Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%, 90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process; propose removal of the 60% plan set and review.  (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

•         Washington – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Emerson – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road.

•         Downing – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Marion – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes (not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.



Here was the original fee:

[cid:image002.png@01DC2C7A.9F0B4360]

The original schedule lasted 9 months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.



The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get towards $50k.

  1.  Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation

This includes removal of all traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings, and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3.

(these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3 months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).



  1.  In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible, we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)



  1.  Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)



  1.  Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))



  1.  Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be used.



  1.  Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%, 90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process; propose removal of the 60% plan set and review.  (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

Hi, Steven. Thanks for the email. I’m realizing now that I never sent the invitation for us to connect following our correspondence about a meeting time this week, my apologies. I’ll send now for 2P, assuming that time still works for you.



The signal modifications are the largest outstanding item in our approach. Please see a few notes below regarding an initial review of the intersections. I’d like to confirm the City’s desired approach for the design/construction of modified signal structures on these corners or if signal head modification is the desired approach. This will obviously impact the design and construction costs.

•         Logan – Signal modification/reconstruction not necessary.

•         Pearl – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road.

•         Washington – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Emerson – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road.

•         Downing – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Marion – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.



I was also hoping to discuss the Virginia & Downing intersection with you in more detail. Pending analysis and recommendation, there may be signal modifications at this intersection, but they are likely limited to minor equipment modifications. More importantly, you mentioned the potential of curb extensions. From a scoping perspective, I’m trying to determine if we should budget for this condition which would be our worst-case scenario level of effort. Furthermore, we would need to consider effort for design survey and storm modifications. I’d like to get your input on how to approach at this stage of the project.



Regarding the status of a draft agreement; we are on track to have a draft developed by EOD with the exception of the items noted above. Let’s connect later to discuss these items. Thanks

One other comment. I was thinking about your question regarding the review process. I'd recommend that we keep 90/100 ER for the whole plan set and approach the new Emerson work as an internal 60% review. I can facilitate specific reviewers on a preliminary review cycle (60%), which will require updates and revisions before we move the full document through into 90%. Please think through this as it relates to schedule and let's discuss if you have any questions.

Looking forward to seeing the final version -

Thanks again.

Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes (not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.



Here was the original fee:

[cid:image002.png@01DC2D5C.CC801720]

The original schedule lasted 9 months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.



The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get towards $50k.

  1.  Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation

This includes removal of all traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings, and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3.

(these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3 months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).



  1.  In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible, we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)



  1.  Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)



  1.  Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))



  1.  Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be used.



  1.  Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%, 90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process; propose removal of the 60% plan set and review.  (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes (not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.



Here was the original fee:

[cid:image002.png@01DC2DE1.9929FEB0]

The original schedule lasted 9 months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.



The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get towards $50k.

  1.  Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation

This includes removal of all traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings, and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3.

(these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3 months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).



  1.  In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible, we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)



  1.  Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)



  1.  Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))



  1.  Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be used.



  1.  Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%, 90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process; propose removal of the 60% plan set and review.  (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

Subject: Fw: ALR Coordination



Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes (not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.



Here was the original fee:

[cid:image002.png@01DC2E20.05C99380]

The original schedule lasted 9 months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.



The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get towards $50k.

  1.  Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation

This includes removal of all traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings, and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3.

(these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3 months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).



  1.  In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible, we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)



  1.  Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)



  1.  Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))



  1.  Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be used.



  1.  Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%, 90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process; propose removal of the 60% plan set and review.  (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes (not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.



Here was the original fee:

[cid:image002.png@01DC2DE1.9929FEB0]

The original schedule lasted 9 months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.



The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get towards $50k.

  1.  Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation

This includes removal of all traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings, and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3.

(these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3 months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).



  1.  In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible, we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)



  1.  Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)



  1.  Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))



  1.  Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be used.



  1.  Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%, 90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process; propose removal of the 60% plan set and review.  (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

Hi, Steven. Thanks for the email. I’m realizing now that I never sent the invitation for us to connect following our correspondence about a meeting time this week, my apologies. I’ll send now for 2P, assuming that time still works for you.



The signal modifications are the largest outstanding item in our approach. Please see a few notes below regarding an initial review of the intersections. I’d like to confirm the City’s desired approach for the design/construction of modified signal structures on these corners or if signal head modification is the desired approach. This will obviously impact the design and construction costs.

•         Logan – Signal modification/reconstruction not necessary.

•         Pearl – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road.

•         Washington – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Emerson – Mast arm extension on SEC is needed to align with center of future EBLT. Arm currently only extends to the center of the road.

•         Downing – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.

•         Marion – Minor signal equipment adjustments may be necessary.



I was also hoping to discuss the Virginia & Downing intersection with you in more detail. Pending analysis and recommendation, there may be signal modifications at this intersection, but they are likely limited to minor equipment modifications. More importantly, you mentioned the potential of curb extensions. From a scoping perspective, I’m trying to determine if we should budget for this condition which would be our worst-case scenario level of effort. Furthermore, we would need to consider effort for design survey and storm modifications. I’d like to get your input on how to approach at this stage of the project.



Regarding the status of a draft agreement; we are on track to have a draft developed by EOD with the exception of the items noted above. Let’s connect later to discuss these items. Thanks

Hi Matt, thanks for the time to connect - here's the analysis I had started - please focus on the bulleted changes (not on the fee, which are obviously just my assumptions). Def call or write if there's any questions. I will let you know how my conversation with Brett goes on Monday.

Here was the original fee:

[cid:image002.png@01DC2DE1.9929FEB0]

The original schedule lasted 9 months with the first 3-4 months representing the data collection and analysis.

The goal here is to identify the essential deliverables for a MVP; which means preserving the design for Alameda, signal timings for the corridor, and PI support. Here are the scope items we will need to cut to get towards $50k.

1.  Remove all data collection and majority traffic safety analysis and evaluation

This includes removal of all traffic counts, delay analysis, diversion analysis, KHA review and validation of the safety assessment, and memo/documentation. The only item preserved from this task would be development of future signal timings, and field observation and verification of timing upon implementation. This will mean we cannot answer queuing specific questions from Amy Ford's email on 9/3.

(these changes should reduce the schedule by approx 3 months, reducing task 1 by 33%; eliminate all data collection; and reduce traffic eval by 80%).

1.  In order to be able to answer questions about diversion, I have already talked to Rinal about using the travel demand model to make some measure of this by using the DRCOG travel demand model. I will need support from the team to prioritize this effort so that we can give a high-level diversion answer to the public. We will not incorporate this information into further operational analysis such as queuing. (If this is not preferred/possible, we could make a very crude assumption that because only half the corridor capacity reduction is planned only those volumes would be impacted - traffic counts show approximately 45% of traffic moves WB so we could assume 4.5% diversion. This is not preferred, because congestion induced diversion is not linear with volume and this would likely over-estimate our actual diversion impacts.)

1.  Scaled back public engagement support - given the removal of the traffic evaluation, materials updated provided by KHA will include translation and materials demonstrating the project components. We will use the materials prepared for the Aug Amy Ford meeting as a basis for the public information and may ask KHA to provide graphical support. (assume 60% reduction)

1.  Transfer Downing/Virginia In-house - many of the alternatives identified with Eric are easily accomplished in house with TME support and implemented by maintenance. Further discussion is needed to determine which components might be necessarily done by consultant and if the installation would be maintenance or construction. (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%))

1.  Preserve all signal modification design as these are essential to the safety performance of the corridor. May explore only signal pole replacement at Emerson by Ops, but design would be provided by KHA in case construction dollars can be found for the full pole, cabinet, and sidewalk work. At Pearl, would include bolted-on mast arm replacement knowing that if construction dollars are not available, the existing mast arm could be used.

1.  Removal of one review stage. Given the simplification of the overall scope of work (initially included 60%, 90%, 100%, and Last Call) and the fact this plan set has already been all the way through our review process; propose removal of the 60% plan set and review.  (assumes $9k design cost reduction (17%)) [Matt - this was my thinking before you proposed a redlined plan set, which I think we should continue to explore so we preserve budget capacity for Task 7.)

s: Alameda Lane Reduction - Downing Turning Movements
cc: "Gormley; Mary" <Mary.Gormley@kimley-horn.com>; "Pratt; Anthony" <Anthony.Pratt@kimley-horn.com>
b:
Hey, Steven. I connected with the team and had them take a (quick) look back to investigate the decision regarding the turning movements. Please find a few notes below regarding the Downing intersection based on our review of past files and correspondence.

In our alternatives analysis we did not introduce the EB or WB left turn movements at Downing Street. We did complete an intersection analysis in Synchro with these left turn movements being allowed but we did not go through with this configuration with our preferred alternative. We weren’t readily able to find formal reasoning/justification in our documents. We believe that the introduction of the left turn lanes was not supported by the City based on the following:

  *   This location has the 2nd highest number of crashes along the mainline.
  *   Keep the existing conditions with no left turn movements allowed
  *   The travel time along the corridor will increase if operations are protected permitted by adding another phase to the signal operations
  *   The pedestrian safety is exposed on the north and south crosswalks if left turn movements are introduced

Regardless, we can discuss this in more detail when the project gets going again.

Thanks for your consideration. Have a great weekend!

Matt Ciarkowski, P.E. (CO, TX, WA, WY, NE)
Kimley-Horn | 1125 17th Street, Suite 1400, Denver, CO 80202

b:
Hi Matt and Bryanna - 

We got questions back about your invoice:

"I'm not able to verify the rates/roles with a red X on page 1.  Can you share with me where I can find these, or have the vendor revise the invoice.  Also, Analyst II has 2 different rates for the same role.. Apologies if I'm missing these somewhere!

If the vendor is revising this invoice, please include some back up regarding the date they re-submit.  As this invoice is quite old, prompt pay will apply.  However if a revision is sent - the date sent is when the clock will restart."

I pulled the original contract with the categories listed and examined the remainder of the contracting documents I have on file but could find no changes to classifications/rates tables. I didn't see any rate changes in the task orders, so I am referencing the original agreement.

*	
	Analyst II - all employees must bill at the agreed rate $125
*	
	CAD Tech I - I don't see this as a classification on the rate sheet
*	
	Engineer/Profession - should bill at the agreed rate $160
*	
	Analyst III - should bill at the agreed rate $140


Should a new invoice be issued, please redate the document and we will process it as quickly as possible to get you paid.

Please call if you'd like to discuss.

steven

steven

s: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension
b:
Hi, Steven. I was able to connect with Mayo this morning and get a revised proposal. They revised their documentation to clarify their data collection labor to be nine (9) hours at $102/hour, $918 total. This resulted in a change in their fee of less than $1. This has been reflected in the updated table in the letter attached and in the updated agreement.

Please see the updated documentation attached and let me know if you have any further questions or comments.

Thanks!

Matt Ciarkowski, P.E. (CO, TX, WA, WY, NE)

To: Ciarkowski, Matt <matt.ciarkowski@kimley-horn.com>
Subject: Re: Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension

Thanks Matt, this is looking good - a couple of questions:

  *   Mayo Add Letter - please add "add" before Mayo in the first sentence

  *   Mayo Add Letter - Is there any additional proof that can be added that clearly states their hourly labor rate is $131.05 and the effort will take 7 hours? If their quote doesn't include that info, perhaps adding a second short table with the rate to the memo will be clear for our contracting person. Also, when they bill, they will need to itemize the number of hours against the labor category at the set rate, so that this doesn't look like a labor lump sum.

  *   Baseline Add Letter - please add "add" before Baseline in the first sentence.

Everything looks great, thanks for helping pull this together so quick - we are getting very excited about the project - you might even see something in the news soon.

steven

f: 2025-11-06_16-43-55 - Follow Up on NS Movements along Alameda.pdf
s: Follow Up on N/S Movements along Alameda
cc: Williams, Nicholas - DOTI Deputy Manager Internal & External Affairs
b:
Hi Jason-

 

I just wanted to clarify Jill’s question regarding restricting the North South movements along Alameda. The short answer is we will not be restricting movement, although there are proposed changes to the allowed turning movements at intersections along Alameda. For example, we are proposing "right-in right-out", "three-quarter movements", and "restricted lefts" at some locations. In all cases, two way travel will be allowed on every leg at every intersection (save one-way streets of course), the allowable movements accessing may be restricted however.

 

Hope that makes sense! Thanks for a productive call.

 

Best-

Molly

 

Molly Lanphier, MPH 
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Strategic Communications Liaison 
720.913.4549 Phone | 720.619.1018 Cell

Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org <mailto:Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org>

f: 2025-11-10_17-13-08 - _EXTERNAL_ RE_ Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension (2).pdf
s: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension
b:
Hi, Steven. Thanks for providing these comments. I haven’t been able to review in detail, but at first glance they look manageable. I wanted to note a few items, please see below.

Survey Limits – I can reach out to Baseline to get a revised fee that includes 100’ from the intersection on the east and south legs of the intersection. We originally included the entire intersection given some of the ultimate uncertainty regarding the conduit boring by the City, separate from this agreement. Also, the cost of the additional data pickup is usually nominal once they are in the field and I defer to getting a complete dataset for unforeseen circumstances. I’ll let you know what I hear back.

Survey Fees – We originally got a fee estimate from our internal KH survey team that were higher than expected so we reached out to Baseline who has historically been a quality provider who is also more affordable. Their estimate was not significantly lower than our internal team, but we knew that budgets were sensitive on this project and elected to move forward with them. Based on other conversations, it seems that this may be where survey fees are heading and/or reflect the current market. I’m happy to consider and contact an additional firm for an estimate, but want to be sensitive about the timing of the contract knowing it is sensitive for the City. Would you like me to contact another firm or move forward with Baseline?

TCE – There is a retaining wall immediately adjacent to the back of sidewalk on the project corner (Google Street View<https://www.google.com/maps/@39.7109747,-104.9769476,3a,48.9y,73.56h,86.25t/data=!3m7!1e1!3m5!1sT6cm0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w!2e0!6shttps:*2F*2Fstreetviewpixels-pa.googleapis.com*2Fv1*2Fthumbnail*3Fcb_client*3Dmaps_sv.tactile*26w*3D900*26h*3D600*26pitch*3D3.753913632287876*26panoid*3DT6cm0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w*26yaw*3D73.56351301562857!7i16384!8i8192?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI1MTEwNS4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw*3D*3D>). It also appears that the ROW is likely right at the back of walk/face of wall based on the (approximate) parcel lines in Nearmap. That being said, I  don’t anticipate that access to the private property will be necessary. Let me know if you have any further notes on this that we need to account for.

[cid:image002.png@01DC5265.47638030]

I’ll plan to get these to a stopping point and can finalize pending your feedback on Wednesday. Enjoy your time out of office tomorrow!

Matt Ciarkowski, P.E. (CO, TX, WA, WY, NE)
Kimley-Horn | 1125 17th Street, Suite 1400, Denver, CO 80202

b:
Hi, Steven. I hope you had a nice holiday. I wanted to follow up with an update on my email from yesterday. I’ve completed an updated draft of the agreement based on the comments provided, please see attached. I’ve also added a few notes in red to my email from yesterday below for additional information/context.

Let me know if you have any further comments or questions.

Thanks!

Matt Ciarkowski, P.E. (CO, TX, WA, WY, NE)
Kimley-Horn | 1125 17th Street, Suite 1400, Denver, CO 80202
Direct: 720.943.5660 | Mobile: 817.308.7306

From: Ciarkowski, Matt
Sent: Monday, November 10, 2025 5:13 PM
To: 'Marfitano, Steven C. - DOTI CE0397 Engineer' <Steven.Marfitano@denvergov.org>
Subject: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension

Hi, Steven. Thanks for providing these comments. I haven’t been able to review in detail, but at first glance they look manageable. I wanted to note a few items, please see below.

Survey Limits – I can reach out to Baseline to get a revised fee that includes 100’ from the intersection on the east and south legs of the intersection. We originally included the entire intersection given some of the ultimate uncertainty regarding the conduit boring by the City, separate from this agreement. Also, the cost of the additional data pickup is usually nominal once they are in the field and I defer to getting a complete dataset for unforeseen circumstances. I’ll let you know what I hear back. <-- Baseline confirmed that the project savings would only be two hours of field mobilization (~$500) if we were to reduce the data collection limits to the SEC only. I’d suggest we have them collect the entirety of the intersection in case something unforeseen comes up during design.

Survey Fees – We originally got a fee estimate from our internal KH survey team that were higher than expected so we reached out to Baseline who has historically been a quality provider who is also more affordable. Their estimate was not significantly lower than our internal team, but we knew that budgets were sensitive on this project and elected to move forward with them. Based on other conversations, it seems that this may be where survey fees are heading and/or reflect the current market. I’m happy to consider and contact an additional firm for an estimate, but want to be sensitive about the timing of the contract knowing it is sensitive for the City. Would you like me to contact another firm or move forward with Baseline? <-- I haven’t yet reached out to another firm for a quote. Please let me know if you would like me to.

TCE – There is a retaining wall immediately adjacent to the back of sidewalk on the project corner (Google Street View<https://www.google.com/maps/@39.7109747,-104.9769476,3a,48.9y,73.56h,86.25t/data=!3m7!1e1!3m5!1sT6cm0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w!2e0!6shttps:*2F*2Fstreetviewpixels-pa.googleapis.com*2Fv1*2Fthumbnail*3Fcb_client*3Dmaps_sv.tactile*26w*3D900*26h*3D600*26pitch*3D3.753913632287876*26panoid*3DT6cm0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w*26yaw*3D73.56351301562857!7i16384!8i8192?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI1MTEwNS4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw*3D*3D>). It also appears that the ROW is likely right at the back of walk/face of wall based on the (approximate) parcel lines in Nearmap. That being said, I  don’t anticipate that access to the private property will be necessary. Let me know if you have any further notes on this that we need to account for. <-- No changes made to the agreement at this time.

[cid:image002.png@01DC5327.F0E56E30]

I’ll plan to get these to a stopping point and can finalize pending your feedback on Wednesday. Enjoy your time out of office tomorrow!

Matt Ciarkowski, P.E. (CO, TX, WA, WY, NE)

Hi, Steven. I hope you had a nice holiday. I wanted to follow up with an update on my email from yesterday. I’ve completed an updated draft of the agreement based on the comments provided, please see attached. I’ve also added a few notes in red to my email from yesterday below for additional information/context.



Let me know if you have any further comments or questions.

From: Ciarkowski, Matt
Sent: Monday, November 10, 2025 5:13 PM
To: 'Marfitano, Steven C. - DOTI CE0397 Engineer' <Steven.Marfitano@denvergov.org>
Subject: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension



Hi, Steven. Thanks for providing these comments. I haven’t been able to review in detail, but at first glance they look manageable. I wanted to note a few items, please see below.



Survey Limits – I can reach out to Baseline to get a revised fee that includes 100’ from the intersection on the east and south legs of the intersection. We originally included the entire intersection given some of the ultimate uncertainty regarding the conduit boring by the City, separate from this agreement. Also, the cost of the additional data pickup is usually nominal once they are in the field and I defer to getting a complete dataset for unforeseen circumstances. I’ll let you know what I hear back. <-- Baseline confirmed that the project savings would only be two hours of field mobilization (~$500) if we were to reduce the data collection limits to the SEC only. I’d suggest we have them collect the entirety of the intersection in case something unforeseen comes up during design.



Survey Fees – We originally got a fee estimate from our internal KH survey team that were higher than expected so we reached out to Baseline who has historically been a quality provider who is also more affordable. Their estimate was not significantly lower than our internal team, but we knew that budgets were sensitive on this project and elected to move forward with them. Based on other conversations, it seems that this may be where survey fees are heading and/or reflect the current market. I’m happy to consider and contact an additional firm for an estimate, but want to be sensitive about the timing of the contract knowing it is sensitive for the City. Would you like me to contact another firm or move forward with Baseline? <-- I haven’t yet reached out to another firm for a quote. Please let me know if you would like me to.



TCE – There is a retaining wall immediately adjacent to the back of sidewalk on the project corner (Google Street View<https://www.google.com/maps/@39.7109747,-104.9769476,3a,48.9y,73.56h,86.25t/data=!3m7!1e1!3m5!1sT6cm0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w!2e0!6shttps:*2F*2Fstreetviewpixels-pa.googleapis.com*2Fv1*2Fthumbnail*3Fcb_client*3Dmaps_sv.tactile*26w*3D900*26h*3D600*26pitch*3D3.753913632287876*26panoid*3DT6cm0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w*26yaw*3D73.56351301562857!7i16384!8i8192?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI1MTEwNS4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw*3D*3D>). It also appears that the ROW is likely right at the back of walk/face of wall based on the (approximate) parcel lines in Nearmap. That being said, I  don’t anticipate that access to the private property will be necessary. Let me know if you have any further notes on this that we need to account for. <-- No changes made to the agreement at this time.



[cid:image002.png@01DC5327.F0E56E30]



I’ll plan to get these to a stopping point and can finalize pending your feedback on Wednesday. Enjoy your time out of office tomorrow!



Matt Ciarkowski, P.E. (CO, TX, WA, WY, NE)

From: Ciarkowski, Matt

To: 'Marfitano, Steven C. - DOTI CE0397 Engineer' <Steven.Marfitano@denvergov.org<mailto:Steven.Marfitano@denvergov.org>>
Subject: RE: Alameda Lane Reduction - Time Extension



Hi, Steven. Thanks for providing these comments. I haven’t been able to review in detail, but at first glance they look manageable. I wanted to note a few items, please see below.



Survey Limits – I can reach out to Baseline to get a revised fee that includes 100’ from the intersection on the east and south legs of the intersection. We originally included the entire intersection given some of the ultimate uncertainty regarding the conduit boring by the City, separate from this agreement. Also, the cost of the additional data pickup is usually nominal once they are in the field and I defer to getting a complete dataset for unforeseen circumstances. I’ll let you know what I hear back. <-- Baseline confirmed that the project savings would only be two hours of field mobilization (~$500) if we were to reduce the data collection limits to the SEC only. I’d suggest we have them collect the entirety of the intersection in case something unforeseen comes up during design.



Survey Fees – We originally got a fee estimate from our internal KH survey team that were higher than expected so we reached out to Baseline who has historically been a quality provider who is also more affordable. Their estimate was not significantly lower than our internal team, but we knew that budgets were sensitive on this project and elected to move forward with them. Based on other conversations, it seems that this may be where survey fees are heading and/or reflect the current market. I’m happy to consider and contact an additional firm for an estimate, but want to be sensitive about the timing of the contract knowing it is sensitive for the City. Would you like me to contact another firm or move forward with Baseline? <-- I haven’t yet reached out to another firm for a quote. Please let me know if you would like me to.



TCE – There is a retaining wall immediately adjacent to the back of sidewalk on the project corner (Google Street View<https://www.google.com/maps/@39.7109747,-104.9769476,3a,48.9y,73.56h,86.25t/data=!3m7!1e1!3m5!1sT6cm0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w!2e0!6shttps:*2F*2Fstreetviewpixels-pa.googleapis.com*2Fv1*2Fthumbnail*3Fcb_client*3Dmaps_sv.tactile*26w*3D900*26h*3D600*26pitch*3D3.753913632287876*26panoid*3DT6cm0PomeIB27q4Z_gdb5w*26yaw*3D73.56351301562857!7i16384!8i8192?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI1MTEwNS4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw*3D*3D>). It also appears that the ROW is likely right at the back of walk/face of wall based on the (approximate) parcel lines in Nearmap. That being said, I  don’t anticipate that access to the private property will be necessary. Let me know if you have any further notes on this that we need to account for. <-- No changes made to the agreement at this time.



[cid:image002.png@01DC53B5.AC2D7B00]



I’ll plan to get these to a stopping point and can finalize pending your feedback on Wednesday. Enjoy your time out of office tomorrow!

Subject: [EXTERNAL] Alameda Repurposing: Keep the Three-Lane Plan!
Honorable Mayor Mike Johnston,
I urge you to stand up for your hard-working DOTI staff and for the thoughtful Advisory Board volunteers who have given years of their time.  I urge you to stand by the previously approved plan to narrow Alameda from four lanes to three. That part of the design is catalytic and key.  The revised plan of keeping two lanes going east instead of one does not provide the five-foot buffer on each side for pedestrian safety, nor does it eliminate the westbound sightline problem.
Sincerely,
Kathryn Smith
560 S Ogden St. - 80209

Cc: Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org <mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org> >; Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director <Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org <mailto:Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org> >; Price, Brittany C. - DOTI CE2781 Engineer-Architect Manager <Brittany.Price@denvergov.org <mailto:Brittany.Price@denvergov.org> >; Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org <mailto:Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org> >; Good, Karen A. - DOTI CE3476 Project Manager Engineering Supervisor <Karen.Good@denvergov.org <mailto:Karen.Good@denvergov.org> >; Cieciek, Gregory M. - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org <mailto:Gregory.Cieciek@denvergov.org> >
Subject: For Your Review: Alameda Lane Repurposing Email to Neighbors

 

Hi Amy- We have a meeting tomorrow at 2 pm to discuss design options for Alameda, but I wanted to share the below email we are hoping to send to Jason Gallardo and neighbors, as a follow up to our in person meeting a couple weeks back. They had asked for some specific follow up. Let me know if you are ok with me sending or if you have any questions or concerns.

 

Thank you!

------

Message to be sent to Jason, which will be shared with Jill Anschutz, Kimberly Kolesar, Cody, Belzley, Amy Guttmann, Illana Poley and Lisa Diedrich

 

I wanted to follow up from our recent meeting. We are taking the neighbors’ concerns very seriously and assessing what we can do to address some of the concerns they brought up. We are currently briefing DOTI leadership and hope to share more about our next steps by the end of July. There were also questions and requests for information at the meeting that we promised to follow up on. Please find our responses below:

 

Will this project cause an increase in congestion on side streets, like E Virginia Ave and E Bayaud Ave?

 

Based on DOTI's analysis, the impact to side streets will be minimal; no more than 10% of vehicles will reroute throughout the entire day. A diversion analysis was conducted and anticipates that Virginia Avenue will experience approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to ~1-2 vehicles per minute. 

 

Crash Data 

 

We ran the crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor. 

 

Peak Hour Volumes

 

Existing peak hour turning movement counts were conducted at all 16 study area intersections along Alameda Avenue on Tuesday, September 20, 2022. The counts were conducted in 15-minute intervals during the morning and afternoon peak hours of adjacent street traffic from 7:00 AM to 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM to 6:00 PM. In addition, average daily traffic (ADT) volumes along three sections of Alameda Avenue were collected on the same day as the peak hour counts with the exception of the Lincoln Street to Logan Street ADT volume.

 

I have attached the data associated with these traffic counts. 

 

Speed Limit

 

The posted speed limit along Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. The revised speed limit post-project installation is planned to be 25 miles per hour. Any changes to the project scope could require a re-evaluation of the speed limit change.

 

Many of the questions that were asked are also addressed on our website in an FAQ <https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects> . Please let the neighbors know that we are always here to answer any other questions they may have.

 

Thank you-

Molly

 

Molly Lanphier, MPH | Community Designer
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Transportation Implementation Division
720.913.4549 Phone | 720.619.1018 Cell

Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org <mailto:Molly.lanphier@denvergov.org>

	On Jul 21, 2025, at 4:16 PM, Lanphier, Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org <mailto:Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org> > wrote:

	﻿ 

	Thanks, Amy. Also, wanted to address Mark Samuelson’s second question about the project a dozen years ago. We have a blurb on the FAQs regarding that:

	 

	Am I having déjà vu? Wasn't a lane reduction attempted on Alameda several years ago?

	DOTI installed a lane reduction on Alameda back in 2008-2010 timeframe. In the eastbound direction, there was a lane drop at Downing necessitating a lane merge before Downing which resulted in stacking of vehicles on the eastbound approach to Downing Street with one lane fully queued and the other lane free flowing. During this time the Alameda average daily traffic was over 20,000. It is now much lower, so Alameda is a better candidate for a lane reduction.

	 

	Let me know if you need any more talking points/ help to get back to Mark, Nancy!

	 

	Thanks!

	Molly
