From: Mark Schenberger <schenberger.1@gmail.com>
Sent: Tuesday, November 11, 2025 9:11:31 PM
To: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org>
Subject: Re: [EXTERNAL] Watered down Alameda project 
Hi Amy, 

I appreciate the quick response. I understand we probably won't ever see eye to eye on this, but I feel the need to address some of the points you mention.

1.	I find it hard to believe this timeline where DOTI was supposedly considering changes to the design all summer. My understanding is that you (or someone else at the department) told the DOTI advisory committee that the project was moving ahead as planned as of September or early October. Is that not true, or did something change after that? 
2.	Regarding your concern that the original design would send 500 more vehicles per day onto Virginia:

	*	Virginia already sees 5000+ vehicles per day according to the DRCOG traffic counts site. I often bike along the street to get to Wash Park and I can't imagine that it going up to 5500 vehicles would make it feel noticeably different. I'd certainly take that trade-off to make Alameda much easier to cross. 
	*	In any case, if traffic diversion onto Virginia and other neighborhood streets was really the main concern with the original design, why not add modal filters to some of those streets to completely prevent the option of cutting through? 

3.	Putting RRFBs and painted crosswalks on a four lane road is just asking for tragedy. That just invites opportunities where a person walking ends up with a false sense of security when a car in the right lane stops, only for someone to speed around in the left lane. A friend of mine's child was killed while biking this year in exactly that scenario, so please don't try to convince me that the new design is prioritizing safety. Having one lane in each direction would be significantly safer for people crossing the road - end of story. 
4.	Whatever small amount of sidewalk buffer you're able to salvage in the new design will almost certainly have to be less than what you would have gotten originally - again making it less safe than it should have been.

I'm also curious if you would be willing to share the data on the robust number of folks you heard from this summer and fall? I'd be curious to see how that compares to the engagement you received during the public meetings and surveys back in 2024 that led to the safer design. 

On Tue, Nov 11, 2025, 6:46 PM Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org <mailto:Amy.Ford@denvergov.org> > wrote:


	Thank you again for your note and we appreciate frustration in the process. Over the summer we continued to take input and feedback from the public (and it was certainly a robust number of folks). We were aware of meetings where neighbors were together to discuss the corridor. Our engineers continued to evaluate the impact of the diversion of traffic on neighborhood streets - for instance around 500 more vehicles a day along Virginia. 

	We spent time evaluating how we could achieve our safety goals and address the safety concerns also that people had about the neighborhood streets. The left turns were important. The pedestrian crossings were important. And we are maintaining all of those improvements (and still looking at how we create the additional buffer from the sidewalks). 

	This was not process done in concert with anyone, and one that looked carefully at data, our safety goals and the feedback from the community. I appreciate the dialogue. 

	Amy  


	Get Outlook for iOS <https://aka.ms/o0ukef> 
________________________________

	From: Mark Schenberger <schenberger.1@gmail.com <mailto:schenberger.1@gmail.com> >
	Sent: Tuesday, November 11, 2025 6:14 PM
	To: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org <mailto:Amy.Ford@denvergov.org> >
	Subject: [EXTERNAL] Watered down Alameda project 
	 
	This Message Is From an Untrusted Sender
	You have not previously corresponded with this sender.
	Report Suspicious <https://us-phishalarm-ewt.proofpoint.com/EWT/v1/M87Ej6RJKlw!hcPIz-zyiDR2wYa4MC6SpO0grwcK7KsSIxsY2P-azupATyVFP6TWz7Dxak6tMTu8Y1DDhtHS3Nh2pSn-5SaZC3U3213nZdEz_AsuEQq34I61JCqC9CcxYd0zleTfJO4$> 
	 
	Hi Amy,

	I just wanted to reach out to say that I'm incredibly disappointed at your agency's decision to water down the planned improvements for Alameda Avenue. 

	As someone who lives directly adjacent to Alameda and has to cross or travel along on it nearly everyday, usually on foot or bike, I was very much looking forward to the planned redesign. Despite DOTI's attempts to defend the new proposal, the bottom line is that it's much less safe than the original. While the pedestrian islands and additional crossings are nice, what the project won't do is shorten the crossing distance for people walking, move cars further away from the narrow sidewalks, or measurably reduce speeding. As we all know, lowering the speed limit doesn't do a thing if the road remains wide. 

	Most of all, it's disappointing that the neighborhood was completely shut out of the the process as the new plan was developed. The original design involved a lengthy and open community engagement process, while this new design seems to have been drawn behind closed doors and in close concert with the "Act for Alameda" group/Jill Anschutz. Given the way this last minute reversal happened, it makes me wonder why I should bother engaging in the public process when it comes to other street safety projects.  


	-Mark

From: Dolan, Jeff J. - MO Chief Strategy Officer and Counsel - Mayor's Office <Jeff.Dolan@denvergov.org <mailto:Jeff.Dolan@denvergov.org> >
Sent: Thursday, July 31, 2025 9:47:13 AM
To: Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org <mailto:Amy.Ford@denvergov.org> >
Subject: FW: [EXTERNAL] Constituent concerns re: proposed changes to Alameda Ave
Dear Mayor Johnston,

 

On behalf of over 300 concerned residents and business owners along the Alameda corridor, we are reaching out to express serious concerns about the proposed changes to Alameda Avenue in the Wash Park area.

 

While we understand the goal of improving safety and traffic flow, our community strongly believes that removing a travel lane to add turn lanes will:

*	Increase congestion and backups on Alameda,
*	Push frustrated drivers into nearby residential streets, including Virginia Avenue, creating new safety hazards,
*	Harm the accessibility and economic vitality of our local businesses, and
*	Require future, reactive interventions due to unintended traffic and safety impacts.

Attached to this email, you will find:

1.	A formal letter outlining our concerns in detail,
2.	Over 300 resident and business owner signatures in opposition to the project, and
3.	A recent Denver Gazette article highlighting the issue.

We respectfully request that the City pause this project and engage with our community to find a safer, more effective solution before proceeding.

 

Thank you for your attention to this matter. 

 

Sincerely,

 

Jill Anschutz 

502.938.2947

From: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org <mailto:Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org> >

To: Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director <Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org <mailto:Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org> >; Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org <mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org> >; Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org <mailto:Amy.Ford@denvergov.org> >; Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org <mailto:Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org> >
Subject: RE: Alameda project follow-up... 

 

Hi Nancy- This looks pretty thorough to me. I will lean on Brett to provide the speed data. Brett, I see there are some in the existing conditions report, but I don’t know if we have anything more recent to point to that would help explain the need.

From: Kuhn, Nancy - DOTI Marketing and Communications Director <Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org <mailto:Nancy.Kuhn@denvergov.org> > 
Sent: Wednesday, July 23, 2025 2:21 PM
To: Lanphier, Molly - DOTI Project Manager Engineering Senior <Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org <mailto:Molly.Lanphier@denvergov.org> >; Holloway, Tykus R. - DOTI Deputy Manager <Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org <mailto:Tykus.Holloway@denvergov.org> >; Ford, Amy - DOTI HE3258 Manager Department of Transportation and Infrastructure <Amy.Ford@denvergov.org <mailto:Amy.Ford@denvergov.org> >; Boncore, Brett - DOTI CE0403 Engineer-Architect Supervisor <Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org <mailto:Brett.Boncore@denvergov.org> >
Subject: RE: Alameda project follow-up... 

 

Team, draft response for review. Please review for accuracy. This reporter wants to talk to someone about the need for the project, so anything we can do to make the need clear in this response would be helpful. My previous response to him is attached. It looks like, this time, we’d provide the crash data and if we could provide speed data, that would be helpful too.

 

Mark,

 

Our plan is to move forward with safety improvements on Alameda Avenue in the summer/fall timeframe, focusing first on making the corridor safe for all users improvements for people walking, biking, and rolling across Alameda Ave on the north/south streets. The work planned includes:

*         Reduced speed limit to 25 mph

*         New designated turn lanes at eight intersections (Pennsylvania St., Pearl St., Washington St., Clarkson St., Emerson St., Ogden St., Lafayette St., and Humboldt St.)

*         Painted pedestrian bulbouts at every intersection between Pearl St. and Franklin St. (11 intersection)

*         Access management safety enhancements at Grant St, Pennsylvania St, Corona St., and Franklin St.

*         New striped medians and pedestrian refuge islands at these locations: Washington St. intersection (west side); Emerson St. intersection (east side); Marion St. intersection (east side), Downing, Marion, and Corona street intersections (east and west sides).

*         Installation of a Rectangular Rapid Flashing Beacon, pedestrian refuge island, and marked crosswalks at Franklin; and new marked crosswalks at Grant St.

*         New School Zone signage and markings east of Broadway, east of Logan St. and east of Humboldt St.

*         Additional on-street parking east of Pearl St.

*         Protected bike lane approaches and green crossing markings at Washington St. and Emerson St.

 

We don’t have an exact date yet for implementation. We’ve been giving travel patterns a little time to adjust to the Alameda underpass project’s temporary lane closures happening between Bannock and Santa Fe, and have been affording time to consider and address new and lingering community concerns about the plan for the lane repurposing.

 

Some of the data we’ve collected that speak to the need for the project include:

 

Crash data 

We gathered crash data for the most recent five years (July 1, 2020 through July 3, 2025) and found there have been 255 crashes which is up from the 192 (from 2018-2022) when we first assessed the corridor. Safety improvements for drivers have been recommended in alignment with the crash data (attached).

 

Traffic Speed and Volume data

The posted speed limit along this segment of Alameda Avenue is 30 miles per hour. 2024 speed data shows that speeding is an issue along this corridor (with 85th percentile speed = 38 mph). Following installation of our safety improvements, the posted speed limit is planned to be 25 miles per hour.

 

Also, related to predicting diversion to the side streets, we’ve looked at average daily traffic volumes

We’ve collected average daily traffic volumes to predict potential diversion to side streets as a result of the lane repurposing work. 2024 counts showed the average daily traffic volumes of Alameda Ave, west of Downing, to be around 14,800 vehicles per day. 

*         DOTI believes no more than 10% of vehicles total will reroute to side streets throughout the day. 

*         A diversion analysis conducted for Virginia Avenue predicts approximately 490 additional vehicles per day, which translates to about 1-2 vehicles per minute. Current volumes on Virginia between Logan and Downing are approximately 4,200 vehicles a day.

 

Latest counts of average daily vehicle volumes on other parallel, east/west roads include:

*	1st Avenue (between Logan and Downing) = ~2,600 vehicles per day
*	1st Avenue/Speer (between Downing and University) = ~50,000 vehicles per day.

 

Wanted to mention that many of the questions that have been asked about the project are addressed on our website in an FAQ <https://www.denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Department-of-Transportation-and-Infrastructure/Programs-Services/Projects> . 

 

Per your question about a similar, past project, DOTI installed a lane reduction on Alameda back in the 2008-2010 timeframe. In the eastbound direction, there was a lane drop at Downing necessitating a lane merge before Downing. This merge resulted in the stacking of vehicles on the eastbound approach to Downing Street with one lane fully queued and the other lane free flowing. At that time, the Alameda average daily traffic was over 20,000. What would be different now is that the average daily traffic is much lower. Alameda is a better candidate now for a lane reduction.

 

Sincerely,

Nancy

 

NANCY KUHN| Director

City & County of Denver 
Department of Transportation & Infrastructure | Communications Division

Phone: 303-513-6046

nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org <mailto:nancy.kuhn@denvergov.org> 

 



 

 

From: Mark Samuelson <mark.samuelson@denv
